I aim to elevate public schools in Wyoming to a standing where parents no longer seriously consider alternatives because their local public school is the best choice, not the only choice. This means advocating for educators across the state, creating environments that foster learning, and addressing the educating whole child through meaningful opportunities.

While I am pleased that the Legislature acted to remedy the recent underfunding of schools in Wyoming, I agree with Governor Gordon’s action to allow the bill to become law without Executive signature. Similarly, while I agree with the concept of “progress over perfection” I was disappointed that the educational funding recalibration bill, while moving in the right direction, was not moving Wyoming forward as far as I would have liked to see. However, I trust in the 2-year adjustment and 5-year recalibration process that we have mandating that public school funding is reassessed regularly, and that changes are made in an evidence-based and data-driven manner.

Simplifying Wyoming's progress in education to test scores is reductive and unfair to the efforts and achievements of students and educators alike. Wyoming consistently ranks in the top 10 states regarding 4th and 8th grade reading and math. While meaningful metrics are necessary to measure progress, I see student test scores as a piece of a much more complex and multifaceted endeavor. In addition to test scores, my offices would also include measures of community strength, teacher retention, disciplinary reports, college and career readiness, among others to gain a full picture of educational efforts and potential gaps in the services we provide the state.

I agree with the WEA’s position that funneling public, taxpayer-funded monies to private entities undermines public education systems. Again, my top priority as Superintendent would be to create a top-tier educational environment in Wyoming whereby the Steamboat Legacy Scholarship program is essentially unnecessary - because our schools are so vibrant, so successful, and so easily the natural choice for all parents. While I understand that some parents and caregivers might want to educate their children outside of public schools, I hope to elevate Wyoming’s public schools - all of them - to a place where parents no longer seriously consider alternatives because their local public school is the best choice, not the only choice.

I would work to advocate for teachers regarding the issues that affect their ability to teach. I would work with local and state governments to put a priority on education, including school safety, healthy learning environments, appropriate funding and budget management, and ensuring the focus is on student learning and growth. Additionally, I would support efforts  from these same entities to raise the quality of life in Wyoming regarding job growth, safe neighborhoods, reasonable housing costs, and community building.

The board should coordinate with local communities, including giving citizens the ability to give opinions to local and state governing entities - including many formats for collecting these contributions, not just in-person meetings. I welcome and encourage communities to work with leaders to create plans and initiatives based on shared goals and values.

I reside at 922 17 Mile Road, Arapahoe, Wyoming.  Built my home in 2019.  I returned to Wyoming from Chandler, AZ in 2006.  I was born in the metropolis of 
Riverton, Wyoming on a cold February morning as my dear mother told me, 1954
72 years young

I am both Northern Arapaho(enrolled) and Mexican American, High School 1972-Tucson,AZ, Undergraduate-Brigham Young University, Secondary Education 1978, Arizona State University Graduate, Education Adm.1988; Justice Studies-Law and Policy, Arizona State University, ABD-All course requirements completed, dissertation completed, no defense.  
I was appointed to the National Advisory Council on Indian Education(NACIE) for a two year term by George H.W. Bush, President of the United States.  I returned back to Wyoming in 2006.(spring)  Fall semester, 2006 I taught- Arapaho Charter High School one semester. January 2007 Northern Arapaho Tribe, Tribal Education Director through August 2009.  August of 2009 -Instructor at Central Wyoming College, College of Liberal Arts through 2017(May).  Rehired 2017 with Northern Arapaho Tribe as Tribal Education Director thru May 2024.  Began doctoral program in 2020 with University of Wyoming, College of Education.

Top Priority: assessment of WDE WY-Topp scores from 2020 to present; meet with all Wyoming Superintendents; field their goals, concerns and find consensus with staff, parent and superintendents in the development of the LEA, greater academic achievement.  Secondly, I will visit every Wyoming school district in Wyoming. I will bring our leadership teams to Cheyenne for biannual meetings, create site hubs of local success and share with our legislative body and Governor.  My commitment to success is a consensual, progressively positive action that will ensure the academic achievement of our K-12 students.

The Wyoming constitution guarantees acceptable education for our students.  The Wyoming legislative body obligates funding for our students K-12 education.  It is my observation that Wyoming is fulfilling its constitutional obligations.  Yes, there is a level of concern by some Wyoming citizenry.  That too will be addressed when I am elected to the office of State Superintendent of Public Education

The purpose of testing is to to measure student mastery of the state's content and performance standards in reading, math, and science.  These should be essential data to evaluate school accountability, inform teacher instruction and follow student growth.  I do maintain that student growth is multifaceted, inclusive of academic achievement as measured by standardized tests, mirroring larger tensions that revolve around who has the power to define what schooling is.  Standardized test arise from a paradigm that rank-orders students based on their mastery of a traditional curriculum.  Tests can also ignore outcomes that students' communities may value.  Wyoming has 48 public school district, 23 counties serving about 92,000 K-12 students. Yes, we are diversified.  The reality is that our students must confront this testing.

I have maintained that education should not be politicized.  There are two camps, Parental Choice, Financial Flexibility, and Healthy Competition. There is a level empowerment for families, as choice matters, secondly they can use the funds for tuition sand extra-curriculars, there is a market based choice built  in that this may improve student achievement. The argument against is realty based, it diverts Public Funds as public school should be the priority, the existing voucher program is harmful to rural communities, lastly, constitutional concerns, as this violates Article 7, section 8  of the Wyoming Constitution.  I support choice of the parents, yet at the philosophical level public funds(tax payor) dollars are being misappropriated.  Serious discussion at many levels MUST take place where consensus is found.

Our school districts along with the community should create strategies that promote teacher professionalism, development and greater levels of autonomy.  What we want is to reduce teachers making premature exits.  Factors include school climate, community culture, and school leadership. Does existing school leadership afford a voice to the teachers?  I maintain that supporting teacher autonomy is critical.  Does the district give teachers greater influence on selecting content, instructional materials and student evaluation as well as discipline?  Lastly, does the district community provide a  lifestyle that  provides avenues of entertainment outside of the classroom?  Inclusive of activities, social interaction and a genuine welcoming community should be in place.

There are five members of this board in which the notion of appropriateness coupled with civility and engaging in quality and constructive free discussion.  It is hoped that productive discourse is there as this relates to deciding state-sanctioned uses of trust lands.  Generating revenue for public uses requires  serious adherence to existing rule. This is dependent on use, recreation, leasing for agriculture or development. Wyoming has 3.6 millions acres of surface land, held in trust.  Issues may include easements, grazing, timber and total asset management.  An extremely important role that the Wyoming Superintendent of Public Instruction engages in.

My qualifications come from a lifetime of experience in agriculture, business, conservation, and public service. Most importantly, I understand the people and communities of northeast Wyoming because I have lived and worked alongside them my entire life. I am committed to continuing to be a steady conservative voice who puts Wyoming values first and delivers results for the citizens of District 1.

Wyoming's biggest challenge is preserving our quality of life while creating opportunities for future generations. We must grow and diversify our economy without abandoning the industries that built our state—agriculture, energy, mining, and tourism. Rising property taxes, workforce shortages, healthcare access, and federal overreach remain significant concerns.

Our greatest opportunity is Wyoming's people. With abundant natural resources, strong communities, and a business-friendly environment, we can attract investment and create good-paying jobs. We should support responsible energy development, expand value-added agriculture, reduce unnecessary regulations, and invest in workforce training.

As a legislator, I focus on results over rhetoric. I support protecting private property rights, strengthening Wyoming's savings, promoting economic growth, and preserving our agricultural heritage. My goal is to ensure Wyoming remains a place where families and businesses can thrive.

Affordable, accessible healthcare is essential to Wyoming families, especially in rural communities. While the Legislature cannot solve every healthcare challenge, we can remove barriers, increase competition, and support local providers.

I support strengthening rural hospitals and clinics, expanding telehealth, and addressing workforce shortages by recruiting and retaining healthcare professionals. We should also reduce unnecessary regulations that increase costs and limit access to care.

Wyoming should pursue market-based solutions that give patients more choices, greater price transparency, and improved access to care. Mental health services and preventive care are also important priorities.

As a legislator, I have supported efforts to improve healthcare access while maintaining fiscal responsibility. My goal is to ensure Wyoming residents can obtain quality healthcare close to home at a cost they can afford while preserving vital local providers.

Wyoming's public lands are essential to our economy, outdoor heritage, and way of life. I do not support wholesale sales or transfers of federal public lands that would reduce public access, threaten multiple-use management, or burden Wyoming taxpayers.

I support giving Wyoming a stronger voice in federal land management decisions. Too often, policies are developed in Washington without sufficient input from the people most affected. Local governments, landowners, permittees, sportsmen, tribes, and state agencies should have meaningful involvement in decisions affecting our communities.

I support active management of public lands, including responsible grazing, energy development, recreation, wildlife habitat improvement, and wildfire mitigation. Healthy forests and rangelands require stewardship, not neglect.

My goal is to keep Wyoming's public lands accessible, productive, and sustainable while protecting jobs, supporting local communities, and preserving opport

I support targeted property tax relief, particularly for long-term homeowners, seniors, veterans, and Wyoming residents on fixed incomes who are being squeezed by rising property valuations. At the same time, we must ensure local governments can maintain roads, public safety, emergency services, and other essential functions.

The Legislature should provide local flexibility and avoid shifting costs to future generations. We should also continue growing Wyoming's economy and broadening the tax base through responsible business development, energy production, tourism, and value-added agriculture.

My approach is to find practical solutions that provide meaningful tax relief while preserving essential local services. As Vice Chairman of the Senate Appropriations Committee, I have worked to strengthen Wyoming's savings and maintain fiscal stability, and I believe property tax policy should reflect that same balance.

The Legislature has an important role in economic development, but government does not create prosperity—people, businesses, and entrepreneurs do. Our job is to create an environment where private investment can succeed through low taxes, reasonable regulations, reliable infrastructure, and a skilled workforce.

Wyoming's economy is built on agriculture, energy, mining, and tourism. We should support those industries while encouraging opportunities in manufacturing and value-added agriculture. Economic development should focus on creating jobs, expanding opportunities, and broadening the tax base.

I have consistently supported policies that make Wyoming more competitive, including regulatory reform, workforce development, infrastructure investment, and responsible resource development. The Legislature's role is to remove barriers to success and provide a stable business climate so Wyoming businesses, communities, and families can prosper.

I believe campaign contributions are a form of free speech and an important way for citizens to participate in the political process. Voters deserve to know who is supporting candidates and how campaign funds are spent.

I have always supported transparency and timely disclosure of campaign contributions and expenditures. Wyoming's system generally works well because campaign reports are publicly available.

I am open to reforms that improve transparency and make campaign finance information easier to access and understand. One principle is non-negotiable: there should never be political contributions solicited, accepted, or exchanged in leadership offices, legislative chambers, or the halls surrounding those chambers.

My record demonstrates that I make decisions based on what is best for Wyoming and the people of Senate District 1, not campaign contributions. Public service requires independence, integrity, accountability, and public trust.

Cheyenne, Wyoming. I have lived here for three years. I am a Wyoming native and grew up in Farson, Wyoming. I was born in Rock Springs, WY. 
My Wyoming roots run deep. Raised in Farson, I have an appreciation for hard work, independence, and service. My experience in business, community engagement, and policy shaped my belief that our future should be built by the people who live, work, and raise families here. I am proud of the Wyoming way of life and I believe our families want safe communities, strong economies, protected freedoms, and conservative leaders who will fight for their values. I am running for Wyoming State Senate to bring principled, conservative leadership. As a wife, mother, and community leader, I understand the values that make our state strong: faith, family, freedom, and a deep respect for the people who call Wyoming home. I believe we deserves leaders who listen, stand firm in their values, and work collaboratively to get things done. I am committed to protecting our freedoms, defending constitutional rights, supporting local communities, and ensuring state government works for the people it serves.
Wyoming shaped me. Growing up in here, I learned hard work, self-reliance, and what it means to be part of a community. That was my Wyoming — and I want it to still be Wyoming when my daughter grows up.
Keeping Wyoming strong for our youth means protecting their constitutional freedoms. It means keeping parents in charge of their children's education, not bureaucrats. It means building an economy where they can actually stay — protecting our energy industry, growing opportunity, and making housing affordable so our kids don't have to leave the state they love.
When I look at my daughter, I see every Wyoming kid who deserves to grow up free, safe, and full of possibility. That is what I am fighting for.
Wyoming is a big state with small towns, and healthcare access is a real challenge. Here is how I would address it.
Expand telehealth so rural families can access quality care without driving hours to see a provider. Grow our healthcare workforce by continuing to build out programs and capacity at our colleges and creating support to recruit and retain providers across the state. Protect our hospitals — they are lifelines for small Wyoming communities and state policy should support them, not burden them. And increase competition and transparency so patients have options and can afford the care they need.
Healthcare solutions that work elsewhere don’t work for Wyoming. Our communities should have the flexibility to address their unique needs without one-size-fits-all mandates from the federal government.
Accessible healthcare isn't about expanding government — it's about removing barriers so Wyoming families can get the care they need, close to home, at a price they can afford.
I do not. Wyoming's public lands are part of who we are — where we hunt, fish, ranch, and make our living. Keeping them public means every Wyoming family has access to the land that defines this state.
But keeping land public is not enough. Federal lands require proper management and investment, and that means Wyoming needs a real seat at the table. Too often, decisions about our land are made by people in Washington who have never set foot here.
I believe in collaboration — where Wyoming's ranchers, energy producers, sportsmen, and local communities have a direct voice in how our federal lands are managed. We know this land. We depend on it. We are its best stewards.
Managed well, public lands support grazing, energy, recreation, and tourism — all of which matter deeply to Wyoming families. The goal is not less management. It is smarter, locally informed management that reflects Wyoming's values.
Wyoming's tax structure needs more than quick fixes — it needs a long-term, comprehensive review that looks at the full picture.
I support a measured approach that does two things at once: delivers real relief to Wyoming families and businesses, and ensures our state can still fund the services people depend on. Those goals are not in conflict — but achieving both requires honest, thorough work rather than reactive legislation.
Wyoming has always been a low-tax state and I intend to keep it that way. Smart tax policy is not just about cutting — it is about making sure every dollar collected is spent wisely and the burden is distributed fairly.
Wyoming families and businesses deserve a tax system they can understand, predict, and trust. That starts with a serious, data-driven review and a commitment to reform that puts people first.
The legislature's role in economic development is to create conditions where Wyoming businesses can grow and thrive. That means smart infrastructure investment, a predictable regulatory environment, and support that empowers communities.
The Wyoming Business Council needs to evolve into a clear, outcome-driven model with defined goals, measurable results, and real accountability. This work must be built from the ground up. Local communities know their assets and gaps better than anyone in Cheyenne.
Workforce development is a key driver this conversation, which is what I work toward daily in my career. Businesses cannot grow without people, and people cannot stay without opportunity. Infrastructure — roads, broadband, water, and energy — is the foundation that makes all of it possible.
Wyoming deserves leadership that sets clear priorities, tracks outcomes, and works hand in hand with local communities to build an economy built to last.
Campaign donations are an investment in the democratic process — nothing more, nothing less. When someone donates to my campaign, they are helping fund the practical work of running for office: yard signs, mailers, events, and the tools needed to reach voters across Senate District 5. That is what donations do. They do not buy influence, they do not guarantee alignment, and they absolutely do not purchase a vote.
I answer to the people of Senate District 5 — all of them — not to any donor, organization, or outside interest. Every decision I make in the State Senate will be guided by what is right for my constituents and for Wyoming.
Wyoming's campaign laws are not stringent like other states, and I hope we can continue on a process that keeps government out of the way. CheckGate challenges Wyoming's values, but I do not believe we need more regulation, we need Wyoming voters to come to the polls on August 18th. That you for your consideration and support!

I live at 808 West Dale Blvd. in Cheyenne. I have lived here since 2008. I have lived in Wyoming all of my life, except for a short stint in Denver from 1981-1982 and Anchorage in 1990. I was born in Cheyenne, Wyoming.
I will be 67

I graduated from Cheyenne East High School in 1997. When I was growing up, my family had a small ranch south of Encampment Wyoming. I spent every weekend and summer there. That is where I developed my love of the mountains. The ranch borders Medicine Bow National Forest, which was a very big back yard! I received a BS in Chemical Engineering from the University of Wyoming. I worked in the oil business for 10 years and for a chemical manufacturing company for 12. I worked for several small consulting companies before starting my own consulting company, Hanlon Process Consulting, LLC, in 2013. My background gives me unique insight into the industries that drive Wyoming's  economy. As a consultant I helped multiple companies operate more safely, efficiently, and profitably. The state of Wyoming needs the same kind of support.

We are challenged by a constitutional mandate and supreme court rulings to fund a quality public education.Developing the holistic funding and oversight mechanisms to ensure that we provide every child with the best education possible without a contentious and divisive disagreement every year is a challenge I am ready for.

Wyoming has the opportunity to become the "power house" for the rest of the country. We need to listen to the people who live and work here and develop the resources with Wyoming jobs and quality of life as our primary objectives. We can do that. The challenge is to develop those resources in a way that allows our children and grandchildren to stay here because they have outstanding job opportunites and a way that causes them to want to stay here because of the quality of life.

We can take advantage of the federal support available to us to maximize coverage and reduce premium costs. Wyomingites have paid the federal taxes that fund these programs. It is foolish to reject the funding and make life difficult for so many Wyoming families. We can work with Wyoming insurance companies to establish creative programs to reduce their risk and pass that savings on to Wyoming people. Those are easy steps that will require very little state funding.

When we take those necessary steps, there will still be working Wyoming families that can't afford their health insurance premiums. It is a more difficult and contentious task to create programs to ensure all Wyomingites can afford their health insurance but the resources are available. If we have the will, we can provide affordable insurance to everyone who wants insurance.

Everyone in Wyoming (with the possible exception of a few politicians who have been influenced by East Coast agendas) opposes the sale or transfer of public lands. No other issue brings us all together as one community in the same way. I stand with all my friends and neighbors who want to keep public lands in public hands!

To manage the lands in the best interests of the people of Wyoming, we must ensure that management practices are science-based and that the agencies that manage state and federal public lands have the financial resources to manage the lands well. At the same time, we should ensure that we take maximum advantage of new technology to reduce management costs.
The legislature must ensure that the tax structure developed by the state has a high enough "ceiling" that each local government can provide the services that their constituents require.

The purpose of government is to secure collective stability, protect individual rights, and manage systemic risks, creating a predictable framework where people can safely cooperate and prosper. If the legislature takes that purpose seriously, they will always have a central role in economic development.

The system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures seems to work pretty well. It would be nice if all campaigns were funded the same, but that isn't the way the world works and it certainly isn't something that I have the power to change.

I currently live in Laramie, where I have lived continuously since August 2006. I first moved to Wyoming with my family in 1985, and was away for graduate school and work from 1997 to 2006. In total, I have lived in Wyoming for about 31 years. I was born in Ann Arbor, Michigan.

I grew up in Casper, graduated from Natrona County High School, and attended the University of Wyoming as a Trustees’ Scholar. At UW, I received a BA in International Studies, captained the Cowboy Debate Team, and completed an MS in Chemical Engineering. I earned a PhD in Chemical Engineering and an MS in Applied Physics from the University of Washington.

Heather and I returned to Laramie in 2006 to raise our four kids. I am a faculty member in the UW Honors College where I have taught since 2007. My career has crossed engineering, advanced and emerging technologies, diplomacy, higher education and public policy, including service as an AAAS S&T Diplomacy Fellow at the U.S. Department of State.

I have represented Senate District 9 since 2011, including 12 years as Senate Minority Leader. I am the ranking member of the Education and Minerals Committees and chair the Select Committee on Blockchain and Financial Technology.

Wyoming’s biggest challenge is that our economy and tax structure too often push away the people we most need to keep, including our young people, skilled workers, entrepreneurs and families building their futures. At the same time, we attract too many people drawn by low taxes and low public expectations, who then resist the schools, healthcare, infrastructure and community investments that make a state thrive. That weakens Wyoming.

Our opportunity is to reverse that cycle and build communities where Wyoming kids can stay, work, start businesses and raise families. That means strong public schools, high-quality healthcare people can afford, starter homes, reliable infrastructure, outdoor access, personal freedom, and a government that supports people without trying to control their lives. I will keep working for a Wyoming that protects personal freedom, invests in opportunity, and gives our kids real reasons to build their futures here.

Wyoming is failing on healthcare, and we need to admit it and change. First, Wyoming must expand Medicaid. We are one of only 10 states that still have not, leaving roughly 19,000 Wyomingites without coverage while rejecting well over $1 billion in federal support that could strengthen families, providers and rural hospitals. Refusing expansion has shifted costs to hospitals, counties, employers and families.

Wyoming is a small-population, rural state, and private insurance alone will not solve our problems. We need stronger rural healthcare, including hospitals, clinics, EMS, mental health, obstetric care, developmental disability support, long-term care and telehealth. We also need more providers.

The Legislature should protect healthcare choice, not interfere with it. Personal medical decisions should be made by patients in consultation with doctors and providers, not dictated by insurance companies, second-guessed by politicians or controlled by the state.

I strongly oppose selling, transferring or privatizing public lands in Wyoming. These lands belong to the people, not billionaires, private interests or politicians looking for budget fixes. Some leaders, including in Wyoming, continue looking for ways to sell off or transfer our public lands. We have to fight those efforts directly.

Public lands are part of Wyoming’s identity, economy and way of life. They support hunting, fishing, grazing, tourism, wildlife, clean water, outdoor recreation and the freedom to access the places that make Wyoming special. State and federal lands should be managed for multiple use, long-term stewardship and public access. I support practical land exchanges, like Pilot Hill, when they clearly increase public benefit by protecting or expanding public access.

These lands are not inventory to liquidate. They are an inheritance. Our responsibility is to manage them wisely and pass them on to our children, grandchildren and their grandchildren.

Property taxes have risen because assessed valuations have increased dramatically, creating pressure for many Wyoming homeowners. But property taxes are essential. They fund K-12 education, community colleges, counties, municipalities, special districts, hospitals and other important local services. Cutting property taxes always means cutting important services.

Wyoming already has significant relief programs for homeowners, seniors and people in need. We should improve those tools while looking at fair ways to pay for them, including additional taxes on high-value second homes and other properties that can better absorb the cost.

The proposed 50% residential property tax initiative on the November ballot would be devastating for Wyoming. It would not solve the real problem. It would undermine the everyday services that hardworking people across our state rely on. We need to defeat it and then focus on modernizing and diversifying Wyoming’s revenue structure.

I support a strong, positive role for the Legislature in economic development, but that role should not be picking winners and losers or simply writing checks to favored industries. Our job is to build the conditions where Wyoming people, communities, entrepreneurs, workers, and businesses can succeed.

That means investing in the foundations of a strong economy, including schools, workforce training, housing, healthcare, childcare, broadband, transportation, energy infrastructure and quality communities. It also means helping where help is needed and getting out of the way where government creates barriers that serve no public purpose.

I support the work and role of the Wyoming Business Council, Laramie Main Street and the Laramie Chamber Business Alliance. The Legislature should empower organizations like these to carry out their missions, build local capacity, support entrepreneurship and create real opportunities for Wyoming people.

I have never taken a single dollar of PAC money. That is intentional. Wyoming’s campaign finance system, like the national system, is legalized and structured corruption. It favors wealthy donors, PACs and special interests over citizens, and then pretends no harm is done unless someone proves a quid pro quo.

Disclosure is fine, but it does not fix the problem. If wealthy interests can spend unlimited money to dominate elections, disclosure does not make it fair. It only shows who bought influence after the damage is done.

Wyoming’s election integrity is exceptional. The corruption is not voter fraud. It is campaign finance. We focus on the wrong problem when we attack honest elections while ignoring the money shaping them. Voters should watch who finances whom. Outside groups cloaking themselves in words like freedom, liberty or progress should be judged by who funds them and what they really want. Our government should belong to Wyoming people. We need to take our state back.

I live in the North Cheyenne area, in my current house for 26 years. 
My parents brought me here at the tender age of 4- 69 years living in Wyoming almost makes me a native.
Boise City, OK
74, with age comes wisdom.

I am a retired general contractor who helped build southeast Wyoming, with projects in Laramie, Pine Bluffs, Wheatland, and Cheyenne. After attending Colorado State University for one year in 1973, I left deeply disappointed by what I saw as socialist teaching and became even more committed to free-market principles.

My years as a general contractor taught me how to stretch a dollar, meet a payroll, and survive the boom-and-bust cycles Wyoming has endured. Those lessons shaped my approach to public service. As a legislator, I understand that government should spend wisely and responsibly. I do not see taxpayer dollars as someone else's money - I see them as hard-earned dollars entrusted to us by the people of Wyoming.

I am proud to stand with the Wyoming Freedom Caucus. I am a Christian, committed to the Constitution, happily married to Christine, 5 children, and 9 grandchildren. and firmly believe in the right to life. My values are simple: God, Country, and Family.
Responsible growth, an excellent education for all of our children, and the protection of our  Constitutional Rights.

I believe that one's own health is one's own responsibility, and many of the decisions we make in life are not healthy and not necessarily the best for us. We need to change the structure of the health insurance industry.  Health insurance should offer catastrophic insurance coverage, and a runny nose should be paid for by the patient. I have yet to find a doctor who will not accept immediate payment with a substantial discount.

I am adamantly against the sale or transfer of state lands.  The legislature does not have much control over land sales; all land sales are supposed to be conducted through the auction process according to the state Constitution. I am concerned about land sales that do not benefit the citizens of Wyoming.

Local needs need to be locally controlled.  The legislature has added a grant office to help local governments take advantage of available grants.  There are other ways that local governments should be able to examine and manage local needs.  This question is complex in that the balance of local control and revenue is generally upset with legislative interference.  

For example, Niobrara County most recent financial statements (Comparative Statement of Activities 2025) show that overall revenue decreased $512 thousand dollars.  However, the decrease was from reductions in grant funding.  Property tax revenue actually increased because of increased mineral production.   

Many counties found that lowered property taxes actually stimulated other economic activity.  Some counties are thriving in the current environment.  Some have been feeling a pinch.  In each case, the decisions at the local level are key.

It is not the legislature's role to pick winners and losers.  Economic development that would affect the entire state is beyond the Wyoming Business Council's capability.  It is within the Governor's office and the legislature.  For instance, universal choice in education would attract new citizens and new industry to Wyoming.  Education quality will also bring new economic opportunities.  This is especially true in higher education.  The new president of UW should develop areas of excellence to attract economic development and work closely with the governor in these efforts.  My parents moved to Wyoming so that I could attend better schools.  The quality of education is far more important than simply spending more dollars.  In both of the last two sessions, I have voted for higher teacher pay.  We need that to translate to better opportunities for students.

I have voted for transparency at every opportunity.  The systems in place for reporting campaign finance in the Secretary of State's office are adequate.  There needs to be better reporting and controls for lobbying.  There is also a greater need for candidate disclosures in Wyoming.  I have seen instances where conflicts of interest are disregarded.  The voters would be better served with greater transparency.

I live in Evanston, where I have resided for 31 years. In total, I have lived in Wyoming for 32 years. I was born in American Fork, Utah, to parents whose deep Wyoming roots trace back to my grandparents and great-grandparents, who homesteaded in Otto, Wyoming. This multi-generational connection to our state shapes my lifelong commitment to Uinta County.

I will be 64 years old on Election Day. At this stage in my life, I bring over three decades of deep roots in Uinta County alongside the wisdom, professional maturity, and real-world experience needed to lead. My age reflects a lifetime of service as a special education teacher, a former journalist, and a community advocate. I am running for the State Senate not to build a political career or chase higher office, but to protect our families, defend our property rights, and ensure Wyoming remains a free, prosperous sanctuary for the next generation of our children and grandchildren.

Chris Katzl is a certified special education teacher and Republican candidate for Wyoming State Senate District 15, bringing over two decades of experience working with Uinta County families. A former journalist and editor, Katzl emphasizes community-first leadership, aiming to apply his skills in navigating complex systems to advocate for local property rights and fiscal accountability. For more details, visit the [Ballotpedia Profile for Chris Katzl](https://ballotpedia.org/Chris_Katzl)

Wyoming faces fiscal volatility due to reliance on mineral revenues alongside rising cost-of-living challenges for residents. My proposed platform aims to implement disciplined budgeting, enhance property tax protections, defend constitutional rights, and support pro-life initiatives with compassion.

The Legislature must expand access by cutting regulatory red tape and proactively educating the public on existing coverage avenues. Having navigated the pre-Medicare gap as a retired teacher, I know affordable care exists through options like the federal marketplace. However, the system is far too complex. Finding coverage shouldn't take years of trial and error, or depend on an insider showing you "which buttons to push."As State Senator, I will push for state-backed educational initiatives and localized resource hubs so Uinta County families can easily find and secure affordable care. Furthermore, my special education background drives my commitment to protecting our critical rural hospitals from closing, lowering prescription drug costs through market transparency, and cutting regulations to recruit more doctors to Wyoming without expanding government bureaucracy

I strictly oppose transferring Wyoming’s public lands to out-of-state corporate monopolies or private equity giants like BlackRock. Our lands must be managed exclusively for verified, long-term Wyoming residents who possess deep roots in our communities and lack predatory motives.We see this dangerous trend in Uinta County with the massive Prometheus data center planned near Evanston. For years, global financial interests have plotted resource-heavy developments on our landscape. These projects threaten to drain local water supplies, strain our electrical grid, drive up utility rates, and erode our rich agricultural and ranching heritage.When globalist entities treat our open spaces as an extraction machine, local voices get ignored. As State Senator, I will stand as an iron-clad wall against these maneuvers. I will demand strict residency verification, enforce total transparency, and block out-of-state exploitation. Wyoming must stay Wyoming for Wyoming citizens.

Wyoming sits on massive budget surpluses tied up in complex reserve accounts, yet local governments face funding uncertainty. We must reform how we manage this excess capital. I propose establishing a permanent sovereign fund modeled after the Alaska Permanent Fund. By capturing our mineral revenue surpluses and letting investments compound, we can achieve true fiscal stability.The interest generated from this permanent fund can directly offset local municipal shortfalls and fund essential infrastructure. This allows the Legislature to grant permanent, comprehensive property tax relief to homeowners without stripping funding from local police, rural roads, or public schools. We must stop burying surpluses in bureaucratic backrooms and start utilizing our state's wealth to protect Wyoming taxpayers and their home equity.

The Wyoming Legislature should approach economic development through a free-market lens, adhering to a Milton Friedman philosophy: the government’s role is to get out of the way, eliminate red tape, and maintain minimal regulations necessary to protect citizens' rights. True growth comes from small businesses and local workers, not top-down state planning or picking winners and losers with taxpayer dollars.However, a hands-off approach does not mean corporate lawlessness. The legislature must act as a barrier when predatory out-of-state entities, global private equity giants, or resource-heavy developments threaten our local resources, utility grid, or resident home equity.Cheyenne must focus on creating a stable, predictable, and low-tax climate. Government should never run the economy, but it must vigorously defend property rights and personal liberties so Wyoming citizens can thrive.

My campaign values are rooted in a lean, community-first approach. I refuse to be bought by special interests or out-of-state political action committees. My campaign relies on direct phone calls, personal conversations, and a strong digital footprint.We proved the power of a grassroots, low-cost approach when my digital push mobilized Uinta County citizens to defeat the massive, sneaky $40.6 million sales tax hike last November. We didn't need deep-pocketed donors to protect taxpayers; we just needed the truth, local organization, and a clear voice.Wyoming's monitoring system should focus on ensuring transparency, particularly regarding dark money and out-of-state special interest groups trying to buy local elections. We must make campaign finance disclosures simple and accessible. Elections should be decided by the voters of District 15, not by the size of a candidate's war chest or wealthy outside influence.
I live in Evanston. I have lived in Evanston for 43 years. I have lived in Wyoming my entire life. I was born in Evanston, Wy.
73 but I feel like 60

I’m a 5th generation lifelong resident who grew up on a ranch outside of Mt. View. We also ran a construction company where I learned valuable skills. I graduated from UW with an Education degree and taught and coached in Cheyenne, Lyman H.S. and Evanston H.S.for 40 years. I served 2 terms on the Evanston City Council and 2 terms in the Wyoming Senate. I have served on a number of local boards: Uinta Senior Center Board, EYC (Evanston Youth Board) SWOT (SW Outdoor Trails, Soroptimist Biard,etc.

Economic diversification, housing, health care, and outmigration of our youth.I think we need to look at incentives and better marketing to let people know we are open for business.  We need to tout our low tax burden, and do a deep dive into why we aren’t getting more business and projects coming our way particularly in our smaller communities. We definitely need to be proactive with the housing and look at barriers that are affecting our growth. Healthcare is a tough one but I think the new funds coming from the health transformation could help if we are creative.

We have to be proactive with the health transformation funds but we need to also bump up our Medicaid rates, and support our hospitals as best we can.  Many of our issues are due to CMS regulations and insurance but the Legislature needs to think outside of the box to come up with solutions to those problems.
I am NOT in favor of selling our public lands. They belong to the public. I will support any and all legislation to ensure that our public lands remain in public hands.

I think we need to stop and really get the data from all of the property tax legislation we have passed in the last few years before we move on any other property tax legislation.  We still don’t have enough data yet to see what the real impacts are to cities, counties, special districts, and schools before we jump from the frying pan into the fire.

We should be a part of the conversation. Even if the WBC is reformed or changed drastically the Legislature should have skin in the game along with funding issues. We need to encourage businesses to come to Wyoming and the Legislature can be an important stakeholder.

The system we have probably needs some reforming.  I think it would behoove whomever is the new Sec. of State along with input from the Legislature to take a deep dive into what other states do and come up with the best system possible. I think it’s ok to take donation as everything is much more expensive but those donations should never taint or affect your votes. If you remember always that you are working for the Wyoming people instead of outside interests then your votes can remain ethical and above board.

committeeName: COMITTEE TO ELECT MIKE GIERAU SENATE
responses: Teton County is home. I have owned a home there since 1986. Full time residence since 1980 born Klamath Falls OR

Husband, Dad, Business owner (1980) community volunteer,
Jackson planning commission 
Jackson Town Council
Teton County Commission 
Wyoming House
Wyoming Senate
Elks 
Cowboy Joe cluba

To me our challenges are the desire to have a way of life we love in a time when all is changing around us. To take advantage of opportunities and to do it on our terms. Thanks to years of hard work by our predecessors we have the financial capacity to do anything! Not Everything but anything.
As our population ages this question is at the top of our list. No single answer but we are working this summer to drill down and move forward.Nothing should be off the table.

I have a record on this one and I can say the key is working with all parties. The beautiful thing is we still have the vast majority of our state in public hands. The energy industry, the Tourism industry, the Agriculture industry 
All have a critical role to play. Conservation and wildlife protection along with industry are not mutually exclusive. We should recommit ourselves to being the National leaders in this area.

Four years ago the number one issue for my constituents was tax reform. Today every homeowner has the ability to access programs to provide relief. Now we must keep watch and adjust where necessary to fund local governments, and all special districts. I do not believe the ballot measure on taxes is good idea. 
The State is listening and working to strike that balance

Today we as a State compete in a global market place. We must leverage our strengths whenever possible. Folks need to remember government working for economic development has been going on for a while. I would ask anyone who has an issue with that to ask if the railroad project had not been backed by the government would we be a state? 
How we allocated our resources in this field has been and should be questioned. It should not be defunded.

I have told every donor to me the same thing. “Your support is welcome but if you expect anything more than a thank you might be disappointed.” 
 That said a main focus for us should be pay legislators. We are at a crossroads here. To serve in the Legislature is a great honor. It also comes at a great cost. Too many of us can’t afford to serve. This lowers the pool of candidates. It also increases the chance of dark money moving into the process itself.
We saw it unfold just this past session. We as a state need to look at this very hard.
680 Road 11, Powell, Wyoming. Since 1989, 73 years, Greybull, Wyoming

Born in Greybull, Wyoming, Atrtended Basin Schools, 1971 Rocky Mountain College, Billings, MT, Graduated in 1975 With a Double major, 
Married in August 1975 to Caroline Sanborne, We have two children.
1975-1982 Hired for first teaching job. Dubois Fremont County School District #2
Taught Math 7-12, Coached Football, Girls Basketball, Track, Middle School Wrestling, 
Athletic Director (5 years for 2 hours a day, the rest was for teaching)
Municipal Judge for 3 years Dubois
1982-2006 Hired by Park County School District #1 Powell, Wyoming
High School Math instructor, Coached Football, and Wrestling
In 1989 received my Masters in Curriculum and Instruction from the University of Wyoming.
2006-2018 Hired by Park County School District #1 as the Curriculum Coordinator
2018 – 2026 Retired 
2019 – 2022 Senator for District 19 (Our District)
2023 – Appointed to the State Board of Education

Biggest challenges: Adequate workers for jobs, Affordable housing, Public Land, Education: Addressing them: survey needs for jobs and educate for them, Finding ways for citizens to afford reasonable housing, Do not allow the sale of public land, Continue to be innovative and move towards the Profile of a Graduate which was developed by surveys to citizens of the state for input. The profile addresses those qualities.

Affordable healthcare comes with more insurance companies in the state so their is competition. And hospitals getting more pay for Medicare and Medicaid. Currently getting 45 to 60 cents on the dollar for reimbursement isn't adequate. Certainly less coverage in the hospitals is not the answer.  Accessible starts with finding ways to keep hospitals above the profit line and not continuing to fall below it. We want good doctors but we complain about the cost. Which way do we go. Good Doctors or cheaper costs?

I do not support the sale of public lands and will do everything in my power to protect our public lands for our use and enjoyment. We have to be diligent in our efforts to manage and oversee those maintaining the lands.

First of all, I am opposed to the 50% reduction in property tax. We are the second lowest property taxed state in the United States. Property tax goes to the citizens of the counties and the municipalities and education. Our students are our future so cutting education doesn't make sense. Property tax has not increased because the government has raised it but because people coming in are willing to pay more money for land and homes than what we have paid and they see that as a good deal. It seems they just don't want to pay the tax. We don't either but we are not sad when we see our homes valued at 3 or 4 times what we paid for them. Why is it the responsibility of the state to pay for something we are not willing to pay for or for us to be cutting the condition of our roads and other needs in the county and the municipalities. It is a situation where we either pay for the property tax to cover costs or we accept the loss of those quality of living things the property tax provides.

Incentives to bring in businesses in is a must and yes, economic development should have a role in our Legislature. It never hurts to evaluate a program and look for changes that would enhance the success of economic development. The state needs to consider paying a little so there is a bigger gain in the end. We don't have the transportation and ability to transport people and materials in and out. if we don't find ways to accommodate businesses why should they come. Cheyenne is growing leaps and bounds and because they have I80 and I25 passing through plus a large airport close by. the rest of us can't say that and then we expect businesses to just want to be here. What incentive is there to get them here. Economic development will allow for better options for the businesses to feel welcomed to the state. Without economic development we will continue to just get by.

Yes, Wyoming needs a few reforms but, campaign donations should be at the top. The amount coming in from who knows where and the size of those donations clearly leaves the middle class citizens who would consider running out of the picture. Then it becomes a rich mans government and only addresses their challenges in our state.

My husband and I have lived just outside Dayton, Wyoming for the past 20 years. I was born in Sheridan and have spent most of my life in Wyoming. Aside from six years in Oregon and Washington immediately after college, I have lived, worked, and built businesses in Wyoming for more than three decades.

I attended Casper College, the University of Oregon and Trend Business College. My professional background includes service in both the public and private sectors. After college I worked as a legal assistant in Washington State. Since returning to Wyoming in 1995 I have worked as the administrative services manager for the Central Wyoming Regional Water System, a public involvement specialist for the Wyoming Department of Transportation, executive director of the Sheridan County Chamber of Commerce, and for more than 30 years as a public process facilitator helping communities navigate complex issues and long-range planning. My husband and I own and operate businesses in Sheridan County, including Innominate Coffeehouse & Bakery and the Lazy R Campground in Ranchester, and The Outpost in Sheridan. We previously owned and operated a sporting goods store in Ranchester that sold hunting and fishing supplies. Over the years I have served on several nonprofit boards in Sheridan County.

Wyoming's biggest challenge is keeping the promise of Wyoming within reach for the next generation. Families are facing rising property taxes, healthcare costs, housing pressures, workforce shortages, and concerns about long-term economic stability. At the same time, Wyoming has tremendous opportunities in energy, agriculture, tourism, entrepreneurship, and emerging industries.

As a legislator, I would focus on practical conservative solutions: disciplined budgeting, responsible tax relief, strong infrastructure, workforce development, and policies that support local communities rather than imposing one-size-fits-all mandates from Cheyenne. My career has been spent helping people work through difficult decisions and find common ground. I believe government works best when it listens carefully, solves real problems, and measures success by results rather than rhetoric.

Healthcare affordability and access are major concerns across Wyoming, especially in rural communities where provider shortages and long travel distances can make care difficult to obtain. The Legislature should focus on strengthening rural hospitals and clinics, expanding telehealth, reducing barriers to recruiting healthcare professionals, and exploring ways to make insurance more affordable for working families and small businesses.

I also believe Wyoming should have an honest, fact-based discussion Wyoming-specific alternatives to Medicaid expansion. The question should not be ideological. It should be whether a policy improves access, supports rural healthcare systems, encourages work and personal responsibility, and delivers value for Wyoming taxpayers.

I do not support broad efforts to sell or transfer Wyoming's public lands. Public lands are an important part of our economy, outdoor heritage, and way of life. They support grazing, hunting, fishing, recreation, wildlife habitat, and responsible energy development.

That does not mean federal management is always effective. Wyoming should continue pushing for greater local input, responsible multiple-use management, improved access, and decisions that reflect the needs of the people who live and work closest to the land. My goal is to keep public lands productive, accessible, and available for future generations while ensuring Wyoming communities have a meaningful voice in how they are managed.

Property tax relief is necessary. Many Wyoming homeowners, especially seniors and families on fixed incomes, have seen tax bills rise faster than their ability to pay. People should not be taxed out of homes they worked hard to own.

At the same time, property taxes help fund many of the things that make Wyoming communities strong: local schools, roads, fire protection, law enforcement, hospitals, senior services, libraries, and other essential services. Strong communities don't happen by accident, and neither do the services that support them.

I support property tax relief that is targeted, sustainable, and honest about its impacts. That means focusing relief on primary residences, protecting those most at risk of being priced out of their homes, and recognizing the varying needs of different counties. The goal should be to provide meaningful relief without simply shifting costs elsewhere or undermining the local services that families and businesses depend on every day.

Economic development is not about government creating prosperity. It is about creating the conditions that allow people, businesses, and communities to succeed.

The Legislature's role is to provide stability: responsible budgeting, predictable tax policy, reliable infrastructure, quality education, workforce development, and a regulatory environment that is fair and consistent. When those fundamentals are in place, Wyoming people and Wyoming businesses do what they have always done—innovate, invest, and build.

For me, economic development is ultimately about opportunity. Can a young person graduating from high school or college find a future here? Can a family afford to stay and put down roots? Can a small business owner invest with confidence? Can a community remain vibrant as it grows?

The goal is a Wyoming where hard work pays off, where people can build a life, and where our communities remain strong for generations to come.

Campaign donations should never buy influence. My responsibility is to the people of Senate District 21, not to any donor or political organization.

I believe voters deserve transparency about who is funding campaigns and political advertising, especially when outside groups are involved. Wyoming's reporting system should be easy to access, easy to understand, and timely enough to help voters make informed decisions before they cast their ballots. I would support practical reforms that improve transparency and public confidence while protecting citizens' right to participate in the political process.

committeeName: COMMITTEE TO ELECT KEN PENDERGRAFT FOR HOUSE DISTRICT 29

responses:
I live in Gillette, and was raised here. I did move away for a couple of years when I was first married during college but always knew we would move home to raise our family. I have been here in Gillette for the past 23 years and was born in Buffalo Wyoming.
I am a wife, mother of three daughters and a small business owner in Gillette. I've worked in Property and Casualty insurance and several ministry positions over the last 20 years that included, Youth Ministry, Young Adults and Women's Ministry. Currently, I am serving my second term in the Wyoming House of Representatives for House district 3 and am honored to serve and represent my constituents. 
I get calls weekly from constituents concerned with non-transparent economic development. They feel they will be left out of a decision that could change their communities, property values and quality of life. Most are not against change, but would like to be including and educated with open communication from their elected officials. Legislation that protects people, private property rights, and resources while allowing businesses to flourish is essential and something I'm looking forward to working on. Some economic development may not be right for all communities and the people in those communities should be collectively making that decision.  
This is a top issue and one that anytime the government gets involved it tends to skyrocket the price. My own health insurance costs are expensive as a small business owner. We as a nation are living with the consequences of the federal governments decision to meddle in the healthcare prices. One area that could be a starting point here in the state is working on malpractice insurance reform. Currently, physicians including OB/GYN's have extremely high malpractice rates and that has aided in the maternity desserts across the State. This is also a concern with our rural hospitals as well. 
Public Lands needs to be multi use although complicated. We see minerals being produced and that revenue flowing into the state and the enjoyment of hunters and outdoorsmen on our public lands. As far as selling state land to the feds, I'm against it. The federal government already owns almost 50% of Wyoming lands and our land is our heritage.
This is an issue that has been wrestled with during every debate for property tax relief. Some counties can weather the decrease and some of our hardship counties could potentially struggle. Every property tax exemption has to be looked at from each counties perspective and we have done that. When the 25% was being debated there was an effort to backfill our hardship counties in the house. After living with the 25% we see that some of our hardship counties didn't see the drastic declines that they thought would come. Interestingly a majority of residential homeowners didn't apply this year, whether they didn't know to or they chose not to. Property tax relief is a fine balance and finding what works takes time, input, and research.  
This has been a topic of discussion over the last year and as a member of the Appropriations committee we are discussing this over this summer. There does seem to be a desire to foster an environment to diversify our economy. How the legislature directs and has oversight is important but remembering that each community has to decide how that looks for them. We as a legislature should make sure that people, private property rights and  recourses are protected. We as a legislature should not be pitting businesses against each other or making our businesses that have been around for decades compete with a competitor that they have been forced to subsidize with their tax dollars. Local municipalities and Counties really are where the actual economic development should happen. They know their communities best and local input and partnership from residents is crucial. 
I believe in transparency in donations, all donations I receive are filed with the Secretary of State. I currently do not see concerns in how donations are monitored. If you receive a donation you are required to report it.

My home is in Lander at 787 S 4th St. I have lived in this house all my life except when I was in college and a three-year period when I worked for the Illinois Commerce Commission. I was born in Lander.
I am an economist (PhD) with a long record of public service in the Wyoming Legislature. Except for the years I was working away or going to school, I have lived in Wyoming my whole life. I have had the great privilege of working around the world on public policy issues involving governance and democracy, natural resources and public utilities. 

With my son and his mom, I built and operate a successful business in Lander.  I have made a payroll for 30 years. 

I am proud to be a thoughtful and effective legislative leader where I have served on the Revenue, Corporations (Corporations, Elections and Political Subdivisions, and Tribal Committees, frequently as Chairman. I am known for being constitutionally minded, fair and thorough. 

I love Wyoming, especially her sturdy people, great outdoors and immense opportunities. 
The most immediate threat to Wyoming has been this short-term turn to populism advanced by new proponents under the Republican banner.  Newer members of the Legislature, elected in a populist wave, have produced conflicting and often unconstitutional policy directions for Wyoming.  Witness the reckless property tax measures, the attacks on public education and our institutions of higher learning, and blatantly unconstitutional legislation surrounding women’s health.  

More than ever, steady experienced hands are needed that have respect for civility, process, separation of powers, and our constitutionally-defined legal framework.  An economist by training, I am a longtime successful legislator with a record of service and respect for our institutions of government.
Healthcare is important to everyone, but the working poor suffer the most. Expansion of health services for the working poor would make peoples' lives better and improve productivity overall while putting money back in our communities to support healthcare facilities for all.  I have been a tireless advocate for the expansion of Medicaid. In my own community I directly support the healthcare services needed for the poor.

Most of rural Wyoming is facing a crisis with respect to emergency medical services. The staggering cost of providing the safety net service including air transport has challenged traditional funding methods. More needs to be done but I did sponsor and pass legislation to allow for the creation of EMS districts. Hopefully the rural transformation funding will go a long way towards addressing these problems across the state. 
No. Our public lands are a treasure that we need to maintain for future generations. Limited exchanges are needed on occasion in very specific circumstances. 

Our Office of State Lands does a pretty good job of managing our state lands. It’s not easy when you consider the competing interests of surface uses, minerals, recreational and open space. The five elected officials ultimately decide significant policy in this area.  

Probably the worst job we are doing is to not account for the cumulative impact of gigantic wind and solar development on our ecosystems and landscapes. 

We share a common interest with federal land managers and good relations are critical. Federal royalties are a significant source of state revenue.
The populist relief efforts have produced bad public policy and that has not made Wyoming better. Property taxes fund local governments and education - not state services.  These shortsighted efforts have increased local government funding disparity.  

Severing the connection between citizens and paying for those local government activities is myopic populist policy and terrible fiscal practice. Backfilling by the state is also a bad idea - everyone would vote away their taxes if they could get someone else to pay to fund their services.  

There is a need for thoughtful relief based on a person’s economic circumstance.  Inconsistent wealth is an ongoing issue with local government funding.  Property tax reductions can make the disparity worse.
Most recently government's role came to light in a populist driven attack on the Wyoming Business Council. 

Most of our communities lack facilities and expertise necessary to attract business interests. If low taxes were the complete answer, Wyoming would be overwhelmed with new economic activity.  We are facing a rapidly aging population, the slowest population growth in our region, and slower relative grown in GDP per capita.

Excepting minerals, most new business recruitment imposes more burden on government than they return in taxes.  Fueled by tax breaks and lax policies, data centers and renewable energy have implications for water, wildlife, landscapes and the reliability of our electrical system.  An inadequate siting process means that we are not fully considering the cumulative impacts of these super-industrial scale developments.

The states that are growing emphasize quality of life, robust essential services like education and healthcare, and amenities. 
I have never taken a PAC contribution for legislative races until this year. I made this decision reluctantly because the cost of races has skyrocketed and rational policy promoters are being outgunned by unaccountable third-party ideologues from in and out of state. I am a seasoned legislator that knows how to stand up to political pressure and look towards the long term for Wyoming, not the shallow populism comporting as conservative ideology.
I currently live in Casper, WY.  I have lived here nearly 6 years.  I was born in Truckee, CA which is near Lake Tahoe.

I am a husband, father, business person, and community volunteer.  While in business, I managed various roles from account servicing, compliance, and delivering incentive payments to internal and external business partners.  In this role, I worked with auditing staff from all levels of government.  I also managed staff in each of the roles I held.

I have also been involved in various community service efforts.  I was the head of a mens group within my church, which on an annual basis put on a fundraiser for a local food bank and participated in a program to provide winter coats to children in need.   I am volunteer National Ski Patroller up at Casper Mountain. Our role is to maintain safety and provide basic first aid and safe transport of injured skiers to next level care.

The biggest challenges for Wyoming is reliance on mineral revenues which tend to fluctuate, the need to diversify economic opportunities, and spending at all levels of government.  I would start by making sure our regulatory statutes are not contributing to these challenges and we are promoting policy to encourage opportunities.  We need to closely watch spending to make sure essential services are the first to be funded without increasing taxes to support them.  And challenge government to look at different ways to provide services more efficiently and cost effective - ie. Internet Delivery, New Technologies.  I want to demonstrate to the public that government provides high level of service in the most efficient possible way.

The first effort is looking at the ability to deliver services.  I believe Telehealth is one option that can assist and we want to make sure there are no impediments in the use of this option. Another is looking into options to incentivize traveling nurses and other specialty providers that will encourage them to come provide services in Wyoming. 

Finally, we need to look at underserved areas and find ways to encourage healthcare delivery - perhaps tax incentives, targeting loan forgiveness, and promotion of rural medical training tracks.
I don't support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming.

I support the most recent legislative efforts of property tax relief as they have attempted to balance relief to the tax payer while providing funding for local revenue needs.  The legislature has offset cuts with backfill of other revenue.  I would look to make these measures more permanent which will help the state and local governments plan growth within a consistent framework.

I think the Legislature should create an economic, regulatory and educational environment that would incentivize business to develop in Wyoming and encourage existing business to strongly consider moving operations and jobs to our state.  And I think there are many opportunities to do so.  I think the current method used has not shown solid results and it appears all involved realize change in this process is necessary.

I believe in complete transparency when it comes to campaign donations.  I think the current Wyoming system works well with the exception of the timing.  I think the timing of disclosure be shown earlier than a week before in-person voting.

I live in Casper, Wyoming, where I was raised and where my family and I have chosen to build our lives.  I was born in Laramie while my father was attending school there and moved to Casper around age 5.  Other than leaving Wyoming for my education and medical training, I have spent virtually my entire life in Wyoming.

After graduating from high school in Casper, I pursued my medical education through the WWAMI program and continued with my surgical training at Dartmouth, Stanford University, and Harvard Medical School, where I served as an Instructor of Surgery.  My goal was always to bring that knowledge and experience back home.  After training ended in 2015, I returned to Casper to practice surgery and have been here ever since.

Today, I am a surgeon, small business owner, husband, father, and am deeply invested in the Casper community.  Wyoming has given me so many opportunities, and I am committed to helping ensure future generations can build successful lives here as well.

I am a Wyoming-raised surgeon, small business owner, husband, and father. After growing up in Casper, I pursued my education through the WWAMI program and completed medical training at Dartmouth, Stanford, and Harvard before returning home to practice surgery in Wyoming.

Today, I own and operate Precision Surgical, caring for patients from across Wyoming while employing Wyoming residents. Through my work, I see firsthand the challenges facing our state including healthcare access, rising costs, workforce shortages, and the pressures facing small businesses.

Most importantly, I am living many of the same experiences as the people I hope to represent. My wife and I are raising children, helping care for aging parents, planning for the future, and managing the rising costs of everyday life. I am running because I believe Wyoming needs practical, thoughtful leadership grounded in faith and service, focused on strengthening families, communities, and opportunities for future generations.

Wyoming faces challenges and opportunities. Rising costs pressure families, retirees, and small businesses, making affordability a top concern. We must maintain a strong economy, support job creators, and ensure government spends taxpayer dollars responsibly.

We also need to strengthen educational and career pathways that encourage young people to stay in Wyoming or return after training. Expanding opportunities in skilled trades, technical education, and professional careers will help build the workforce our communities need.

Affordable, accessible healthcare remains a critical priority, especially in rural areas where workforce shortages and rising costs threaten access to care.

Finally, Wyoming's public lands, natural resources, and outdoor heritage are central to our economy and way of life. We must protect these assets while ensuring they continue to benefit future generations. My focus will be practical, conservative solutions that strengthen Wyoming families and communities.

Healthcare costs and access are among the most important issues facing Wyoming families. As a practicing surgeon, I see these challenges every day. If elected, I would be the only physician serving in the Wyoming Legislature, bringing firsthand experience caring for patients and recruiting healthcare professionals to our state.

We must strengthen Wyoming's healthcare workforce by supporting the recruitment and retention of physicians, nurses, and other providers, especially in rural communities. I also support continued investment in programs such as the Rural Health Transformation Program, which helps preserve local access to care and better coordinate services across Wyoming.

To improve affordability, we should encourage greater competition and innovation in the health insurance market, increase transparency, and reduce unnecessary administrative costs. Wyoming families deserve affordable coverage and access to high-quality care close to home.

Wyoming's public lands are one of our state's greatest assets. They support hunting, fishing, recreation, grazing, energy production, tourism, and our way of life. I do not support broad efforts to sell or transfer public lands out of public ownership. Once public access is lost, it is often impossible to restore.

As someone who enjoys Wyoming's great outdoors, I understand firsthand the importance of protecting access for future generations. Public lands belong to the people of Wyoming and should be managed in their long-term interests, not for short-term gain.

I support a balanced, multiple-use approach that protects public access while allowing responsible recreation, grazing, conservation, and resource development. My priority will be ensuring Wyoming's public lands continue to benefit the people who live, work, hunt, and recreate here.

Property taxes have become a major concern for many Wyoming families, especially seniors and those living on fixed incomes. No one should be forced to sell or lose a home they spent a lifetime paying for simply because property values have increased faster than their income.
I support property tax relief for homeowners while recognizing that property tax revenue is necessary to fund essential services such as law enforcement, roads, fire protection, and infrastructure. Wyoming's counties have different economies and revenue sources, therefore, a one-size-fits-all approach may not work.
As a Senator, I would work to balance taxpayer relief with funding of necessary local services. We must find opportunities to improve government efficiency, reduce spending and unnecessary burdens on property owners while preserving the services that Wyoming communities rely upon. Wyoming residents must be able to keep their homes and we must maintain strong local communities.

The Wyoming Legislature should focus on creating the conditions for economic success, not picking winners and losers. That starts with maintaining a stable tax and regulatory environment that allows small businesses to grow and create jobs.

We must also invest in the infrastructure that growing communities need, including housing, transportation, energy, and broadband. Economic growth depends on our ability to attract and retain both employers and workers.

Workforce development is equally important. Wyoming should continue expanding educational pathways, apprenticeships, technical training, and professional opportunities that help young people stay in Wyoming or return after completing their education.

Finally, we should remain open to emerging industries and new technologies while thoughtfully evaluating their long-term impacts. The goal should be sustainable growth that creates opportunity, strengthens communities, and preserves Wyoming's quality of life for future generations.

Citizens have a right to support candidates and causes they believe in. I believe voters have a right to know where campaign funding comes from. Transparency is key to public trust in the electoral process.
Wyoming requires candidates to disclose contributions and expenditures. Voters can review that information before casting their vote. I believe transparency is more important than creating restrictions that could discourage citizen participation in the political process.
If reforms are considered, they should focus on making campaign finance information easier for the public to access, while preserving the rights of individuals to support the candidates they believe will best serve Wyoming.
