Ultimately, I believe elected officials should be accountable to the voters they represent. The best safeguard against undue influence is an informed electorate and a political process that allows citizens to evaluate candidates based on both their positions and their actions.
I currently reside in Casper, Wyoming. I have lived in my current home since July 2019. I have lived in Wyoming since 1978. I was born in Broken Bow, Nebraska.

I am uniquely qualified to serve Senate District 27 because of my professional experience and a strong record of community service. I work as a paralegal in family law helping families through difficult situations and gaining practical knowledge of the legal system and working with the statutes that are considered and possibly passed on the Senate floor. I am working toward my Master's in Mental Health Counseling to further help our community. I am serving in the Republican party and volunteer on nonprofit boards to engage in different facets of our communities. As a wife, mother, and Wyoming woman, I understand the values and challenges facing our communities and will bring principled, conservative leadership to Cheyenne.

Wyoming faces serious challenges in mental and physical healthcare access, the rising cost of living, and the need for a stronger, more diverse economy. 
As a legislator, I would support efforts to expand access to mental and physical healthcare services, recruit and retain healthcare providers, and remove barriers that keep care out of rural communities. I would also fight for responsible policies that will help Wyoming families such as encouraging business growth, job creation, and economic diversification so Wyoming can remain a place where families thrive and future generations can stay and build lives.

We need to recruit and retain providers to all of Wyoming. This can be accomplished by reducing unnecessary regulations that make it harder to practice in Wyoming. Provide incentives for providers to care for individuals in rural areas. Promoting price transparency, support competition and looking at possible medical liability reforms to help bring down the cost of malpractice insurance.

No, I do not support the sell or transfer of public land in Wyoming. Land management decisions should be made by the people of Wyoming. We are the owners of the land. I will support strong state and local input, protect multiple use, defend access for recreation, grazing, and responsible energy production, and making sure that our land is managed in a way that respects our heritage and way of life. Decisions should be made openly, with meaningful involvement from local communities, so that Wyoming voices are heard and our natural resources are managed responsibly.

Any solution to this must balance the homeowner and property owner with the need for counties, schools, and local services to remain funded. I support thoughtful, responsible tax relief that helps families with rising costs, while also respecting local control and recognizing the different revenue structures and needs across Wyoming. I would look for solutions that provide relief without creating unintended harm for essential services of public safety, roads, and education. Working with local governments, taxpayers, and community leaders to develop fair and flexible policies for each Wyoming community.

The Wyoming Legislature has a limited but important role in economic development. The legislature should help encourage a stable, business-friendly environment where private enterprise can grow. The legislature can help by reducing unnecessary regulation, support infrastructure, encourage workforce development, and adopt policies that help businesses invest, hire, and succeed in Wyoming.

Campaign donations should be legal, transparent, and never allowed to influence the voice of the people I am elected to serve. My campaign will be conducted with honesty, integrity, and full compliance with the law. 
Wyoming's system for monitoring campaigns should be reviewed regularly to ensure that it is transparent, easy for the public to understand, and candidates can clearly and transparently comply with campaign disclosures.
I live at 2240 W. 43rd street, Casper WY  82604  I have lived in this home for 19 years.  I have lived in Wyoming for 59 years and was born here in Casper.

I own my business Forefront Real Estate and have been a business owner for 10 years.  I have worked in various industries - oilfield, trucking, newspaper, and real estate.  I served on Casper City Council for 4 years and was Vice Mayor for a 1 1/2 years.  I am currently the Vice President of Wyoming Realtors, I served on Natrona County Historic Preservation Board, and I serve on Central Wyoming advocacy board for Central Wyoming Realtors.  I love our great state and want to preserve our values, support economic growth, and ensure future generations can enjoy the freedoms we cherish today.

Wyoming's biggest challenge is protecting our way of life while preparing for the future.  We are facing rising property taxes, housing shortages, rural healthcare gaps, education concerns, workforce shortages, and an economy that still depends heavily on energy and mineral revenue.  I believe our biggest opportunity is growth done the Wyoming way.  I support responsible energy development, including nuclear, Ai data centers, traditional energy, renewables, and new industries that bring good jobs while respecting water, property rights, and local communities.  I would make Wyoming more business-friendly, reduce unnecessary red tape, and keep taxes predictable so companies want to invest here.

Healthcare affordability and access are among the most important issues facing Wyoming families, especially in our rural communities.  No Wyoming resident should have to drive hours to see a specialist, wait months for care, or choose between paying for medication and paying their bills.  As a legislator. I would focus on practical solutions such as:  Strengthening rural healthcare, expand telehealth services, reduce healthcare costs through competition and transparency, support preventative care and wellness programs, and protect rural hospitals and clinics.  I believe healthcare decisions should remain as close to patients and their doctors as possible.  We need to remove barriers, encourage innovation, support local healthcare providers and ensure Wyoming families have access to affordable healthcare regardless of where they live.

I believe public lands are on of Wyoming's greatest assets and should be managed in a way that benefits the people of Wyoming.  I do not support large scale sales or transfers of public lands if they would reduce public access, harm Wyoming's outdoor heritage, or place additional financial burdens on taxpayers.  I believe we should have a strong voice in how our federal lands within our borders are managed.  We live, work, ranch, farm, hunt, recreate, and we raise families here, and our voice should be considered in Washington D.C.

Property tax relief is important because many Wyoming families, seniors, and small business owners are feeling the pressure of rising property values and rising tax bills.  People should not be taxed out of their homes simply because the market value of their property has increased.  The long-term solution is to continue to grow Wyoming's economy by attracting new businesses, supporting energy development, encourage innovation to create more jobs so we can broaden our tax base and reduce pressure on property owners over time.

The Wyoming Legislature has an important role in economic development but is not government's job to pick winners and losers in the marketplace.  Our role should be to create an environment where businesses, entrepreneurs, and workers can succeed.  We should create the opportunities so our children and grandchildren can build their futures in Wyoming instead of feeling they have to leave the state to find them.

My approach to campaign donations is simple:  transparency, integrity, and accountability.  Voters deserve to know who is supporting candidates and where campaign money is coming from.  Public trust in government depends on an open and transparent process.
I was born and raised in Cheyenne, WY.

Senator Evie Brennan is a consistent conservative who is working to preserve Wyoming for her children and their generation like her grandparents preserved Wyoming for her. Evie is a third generation Wyoming native whose great-grandfather homesteaded in Wyoming. Married with four children, she and her husband are raising a new Wyoming generation.

​​Evie Brennan graduated from Cheyenne East High School in 2000. She attended the University of Wyoming where she graduated with a Bachelors of Science in Nursing in 2004.

Evie has worked as a nurse in Cheyenne since graduating college. For the past 12 years she has worked in the ICU. This experience has prepared Evie to work hard, think critically and defend the freedoms of Wyoming.

Evie has served on the Joint Transportation Committee. She is currently a member of the Joint Education Committee, The select committee on School Finance-Recalibration and is the Senate Chairman for Labor, Health and Social Services.

Some of the biggest challenges facing Wyoming today are jobs and our economy. Because of the state’s boom‑and‑bust cycle, we must get serious about diversifying our economy. We have outstanding natural resources and should continue to develop them responsibly while protecting our landscapes and communities. At the same time, we must look 50–100 years ahead and ask: What industries are young people entering, and how can we provide the education and jobs for those careers here in Wyoming? What resource‑based industries can locate here and offer good, long‑term careers for our families? What steps will attract the right companies and industries to our state? By intentionally aligning our legacy industries with emerging sectors and planning and developing strong infrastructure we can build a more stable economy and create well‑paying jobs for the next generation.

Healthcare is a complex system that can be difficult for patients and families to understand and navigate. Much of this challenge stems from the intricate relationships among providers, insurers, pharmaceutical companies, and government programs. We must work to reduce unnecessary complexity and improve transparency so patients can access care more easily. Compounding these challenges is a nationwide shortage of healthcare professionals, and Wyoming's vast rural geography makes access even more difficult. We need targeted strategies to strengthen the healthcare workforce, improve provider recruitment and retention, expand telehealth and mobile health services, and support local clinics and hospitals. By focusing on access, affordability, and quality, we can ensure all Wyoming residents receive timely, high-quality care close to home.

Wyoming's public lands are not for sale. They belong to the people of Wyoming and are a vital part of our heritage, economy, and way of life. These lands support outdoor recreation, tourism, agriculture, energy development, and local communities across the state.

Public lands provide access for hunting, fishing, hiking, camping, and other outdoor activities while preserving the natural beauty, wildlife habitat, and open spaces that make Wyoming unique. As stewards of these resources, we have a responsibility to keep them accessible, productive, and well managed for future generations.

I oppose efforts to sell or transfer public lands that would reduce public access or compromise Wyoming's long-term interests. Protecting these lands ensures that future generations can enjoy the same opportunities and freedoms we value today.

Over the last several years, the Wyoming Legislature has worked thoughtfully to address rapidly rising property taxes while recognizing the need for counties and local governments to maintain essential infrastructure and public safety services. We have enacted a 4% cap on annual property tax increases and provided a 25% property tax reduction to deliver meaningful relief to homeowners.

Moving forward, we must continue to balance taxpayer relief with the fiscal needs of our communities. Taxes should remain low, fair, and broad-based, while ensuring that counties have the resources necessary to maintain roads, bridges, emergency services, and other critical infrastructure that Wyoming residents depend on every day.

Economic development in Wyoming should focus on creating good-paying, stable jobs that allow families and communities to thrive. We must remain competitive with the other 49 states in attracting new industries and investment while continuing to support and strengthen our legacy industries, including energy, agriculture, tourism, and mining.

Wyoming's business-friendly environment, skilled workforce, and quality of life should provide a strong foundation for growth. As we recruit new companies and industries, they should complement our existing economy, respect Wyoming values, and contribute positively to our communities. The goal is not growth for growth's sake, but sustainable economic development that expands opportunities, diversifies our economy, and preserves the Wyoming way of life for future generations.

Transparency is essential to maintaining public trust in our elections and government. Wyoming law requires campaign contributions and expenditures to be reported to the Secretary of State's office, and that information is readily accessible to the public.

Voters have a right to know who is funding political campaigns and how campaign funds are being spent. Public disclosure promotes accountability, helps ensure compliance with campaign finance laws, and strengthens confidence in the electoral process. Campaign contribution and expenditure records should continue to remain publicly available and easily accessible to Wyoming citizens.

committeeName: COMMITTEE TO ELECT EARL COLLIER FOR HOUSE DISTRICT 3

responses: I live in Wright, Wyoming and have been in Wright and Wyoming for 20 years. I grew up in Wells, Minnesota
I have been an educator for 31 years with 20 being in the Campbell County School District.

As a legislator, I’ll tackle rural healthcare by backing smart, stable funding that keeps our local clinics and hospitals open without hitting property owners with higher taxes. We need to expand telehealth so folks in remote areas can connect right with specialists, and I’ll push for state matching funds to make that happen. I also want to grow our medical residency programs and offer student loan forgiveness to get doctors and nurses to put down roots in our rural counties.

When it comes to keeping our workforce strong and supporting our schools, I'll fight for predictable, inflation-adjusted funding so our districts can recruit and keep great teachers. I’ll also work to connect our K-12 schools, community colleges, and local businesses to build better technical and career training pipelines. This gives our young people a clear path to great Wyoming jobs right here at home.

To make healthcare more affordable, we have to cut through the red tape and look at practical solutions. First, we need to take a hard look at prescription drug costs. By forcing more transparency on pharmacy benefit managers, we can make sure discounts actually get passed down to folks at the pharmacy counter.

We should also give small businesses more flexibility to pool together and buy group insurance, which would drive down monthly premiums for working families. Finally, we need to cut the administrative paperwork cluttering up our rural clinics. When doctors spend less time filling out state forms, they can spend more time treating patients, which lowers overhead costs for everyone. By focusing on common-sense transparency, cutting waste, and boosting our local community health centers, we can keep high-quality care within reach without breaking the bank.

When it comes to our public lands, I’m 100% against selling or transferring them to private interests. These spaces are a massive part of Wyoming's heritage, tourism, and outdoor lifestyle. To make sure they're managed in the best interest of everyone, the Legislature needs to foster strong, common-sense partnerships with local ranchers, conservationists, energy producers, and federal agencies. We need a balanced, multi-use approach that fully supports our traditional industries like agriculture and energy, while firmly protecting open access for hunting, fishing, and outdoor recreation. By keeping local Wyoming voices at the head of the table, we can protect our beautiful landscapes and support our economy for generations to come.

Balancing property tax relief with local revenue needs is all about finding a middle ground. We need to protect homeowners without cutting the legs out from under the vital services our counties rely on, like our local law enforcement, emergency services, and roads.

To do that, we should expand the state's property tax refund program so it gives immediate, meaningful help to seniors, veterans, and working families who are really feeling the squeeze right now. We also need to put a sensible cap on how much annual assessments can jump so people aren't blindsided by massive tax spikes. To make sure our communities don't lose out, the state should use its budget surpluses to backfill that revenue for the counties. This keeps our local emergency services and roads fully funded while giving everyday Wyoming residents some much-needed breathing room.

The Legislature definitely has a big role to play in economic development, but it shouldn't be about the state picking winners and losers with your tax dollars. Instead, our job is to build a rock-solid foundation so local businesses can thrive and new industries actually want to set up shop here.

I truly feel that the system we have in place right now works well. From our end on the campaign trail, my team and I make it a point to input all of our donation and expense information weekly. Keeping up with it regularly like that makes sure the reporting doesn't turn into some massive, daunting task right before the deadlines, and it keeps us completely accountable to the public in real-time. Because our current rules do a great job of keeping things transparent and are straightforward enough for candidates to manage responsibly, I don't see a need for major reforms right now.
Wheatland Wy all my life, all my life, Wheatland

I am born and raised in Wheatland I worked as a mechanic and welder for 12 years after that I became a pastor at Impact Ministries. I served on a number of different boards through the years and I have now served house district 4 for the last 6 years.

One of the biggest challenges today in Wyoming is diversity of economy and keeping our young people in the state. CTE is the backbone of Wyoming's workforce. We need to inform our youth of the job opportunities in the state and how they can make a living in these feilds. We also need to keep tax structure beneficial for industry so that we can keep businesses in our state.

Health care has to become a private market again. Obama care has killed health care and made it finically unattainable for many people. We dont need more govornment trying to fix what the government broke in the first place. Also we need to adress the insurance/PBM issue for pharmaceuticals .

No I am not for selling public lands. I feel that we have had a transfer system in place for many years that has worked and we don't need to allow it to open up any more. We need to as a state make sure that we don't allow property sale or transfer to be used for the finical gain of a select group of people.

I agree every county is different, As i have been in this discussion of property tax I feel that it is time to consider the sales tax increase and elimination of property tax on residential. this might not be possible but needs to be looked at further. it is important to understand that we cant give tax relief to some and not all that is unconstitutional.

We need to assure that the regulatory framework for the state is favorable to economic development. We also need to make sure that the infrastructure of our communities is capable of supporting development.
I only take donations from people. I have not taken pac money and so feel that is the best way to stay transparent.
I live on the family farm west of Lingle. I have lived here my entire life (46 years). I was born in Scottsbluff, Nebraska.

I am a lifelong Goshen County resident and third-generation family farmer from Lingle. 
I graduated from Lingle-Fort Laramie High School and then attended EWC before earning my undergraduate and law degrees from the University of Wyoming. 
I currently practice law in Torrington while actively working on my family’s cattle feeding and farming operation near Lingle. My hands-on experience as an agricultural producer and attorney gives him practical, firsthand knowledge of the challenges facing Goshen County families, including private property rights, water rights, taxes, and regulatory burdens.
I served for ten years on the Lingle-Fort Laramie Conservation District Board, and I am an active member of the Torrington Rotary Club. I have been married for 22 years to my wife Caren. We have three daughters, Natalie, Charlotte, and Lydia. I am a strong supporter of local youth activities and regularly attend my daughters’ sporting events.

The two greatest opportunities before Wyoming today are our people and our resources.
The world is hungry for the energy, agricultural products, and natural resources Wyoming produces, positioning our state to power the economy of tomorrow. At the same time, we are blessed with a talented, energetic generation of young people.
As a legislator, I will champion policies that retain our youth in Wyoming. I support strong education and career training, expanded trade and technical programs, and a pro-growth regulatory environment that allows businesses to thrive and create opportunity for our families.
By keeping our kids in Wyoming and responsibly developing our resources, we will build a stronger, more prosperous future.

In our vast rural state, too many families face high prices, long travel distances, and shortages of doctors and specialists. As a legislator, I will work to make care more affordable and accessible by expanding telemedicine so Wyomingites can access providers even in rural communities, strengthening our healthcare workforce through targeted incentives to attract and retain doctors, nurses, and specialists, and combating high costs by requiring clear pricing and eliminating unnecessary regulations that drive up costs.

I do not support the wholesale sale of Wyoming's public lands to private interests. These lands belong to the people and must serve the needs of the people. I support a multiple use strategy including energy development, agricultural use, recreation, and hunting.
As a legislator, I will support transfer of management authority over suitable federal lands from Washington to the State of Wyoming. Our state has a proven track record of responsible stewardship and local control resulting in decisions that reflect the needs and values of Wyomingites rather than national environmental agendas.

The government has constitutional obligations to the public. Taxes should never be more than is necessary to meet these obligations. As a legislator, I will work closely with our local leaders, special districts, municipalities, and school districts to ensure that tax revenues collected are narrowly tailored to meet our mandated obligations.

The Wyoming Legislature's role in economic development is to create an environment where businesses, entrepreneurs, and families can thrive without picking winners and losers with taxpayer subsidies. Government's job is to get out of the way. We should focus on fair taxes, reasonable regulations, quality infrastructure, and a skilled workforce. As a legislator, I will support regulatory reform, workforce education and development, responsible resource development, pro-growth tax policies, and strategic infrastructure investment.
True economic prosperity comes from free enterprise and innovation, not government programs.

When it comes to campaign donations, transparency, integrity, and respect for free speech is important. I welcome support from individuals, businesses, and organizations who share my commitment to high moral values, fair tax policy, and responsible resource development. Additionally, voters have a right to know who supports a candidate. Therefore, I am committed to prompt and full disclosure of every donation I receive.
I have lived in Douglas Wyoming all my life.  I was born in February 1967 in Cheynne while my dad was serving in the legislature.

I was raised on a ranch south of Douglas but moved to town 25 years ago.  I am a Douglas High School graduate and have a BS in Agriculture Economics from the University of Wyoming.  I have served as a County Commissioner for the past 19.5 years and that has provided me with a wealth of knowledge and experience.  I have advocated for Converse County and Wyoming at the state and national level, including the US Departments of Energy, Interior, Agriculture, BLM and EPA.  Multiple use, streamlined regulations and local control are a regular theme when I am speaking to those agencies. I am the stadium announcer for Douglas Bearcat Football and have been a High School Basketball Official for 23 years.  My wife Tione and I are small business owners and very active in our community.  We have 2 sons and 1 granddaughter.

We need to continue investing in economic development and diversity by appropriately funding and directing the Business council so they can be an effective partner with local entities as they seek out and grow local businesses.  If we fail to stay in the game as some have suggested, we lose, plain and simple.  Economic development is a process not an event.  Our core industries of Tourism, Energy and Agriculture employee and provide a large part of our revenue.  We must continue to research new prospects and build upon existing partnerships to expand those opportunities.  Rare earth elements and the resurgence of uranium have huge potential for Wyoming.  Finally, we need to invest more into our ageing infrastructure.  Much of infrastructure is at an age where if we fail to invest now, the cost will become unbearable later.  Roads, Bridges, water, sewer and the like are basic quality of life needs we must maintain and improve.

First of all, we need to make sure health care is available.  Rural hospitals are in desperate shape and lack of care is real.  The health care transformation proposal from the Dept of Health was trying to help ensure this in the long term.  Unfortunately, the proposal was rejected by the feds. However, some of the ideas in the proposal are worth looking at for state implementation.    Another factor is the limited number of companies offering health insurance in WY, which has appeared to lead to higher premiums.  The real fact is that the problems with health care are larger than Wyoming and real reform and vision is needed at the national level.  We must do what we can, but also pressure Congress to do better.

* There should not be any large-scale sale or trade of public lands. Public lands in Wyoming are a treasurer and we must protect them.  They provide recreation, wildlife habitat, support agriculture, mineral wealth and a host of other benefits.
Limited trades that block up public land with the goal of providing better access and more efficient management should be considered, but in a restricted manner and only with local input.

Targeted property tax relief has been very effective in Wyoming.  As a County Commissioner I advocated for and voted to implement the highly successful Converse County Property Tax refund program.  Over the last 3 years we have refunded over $620,000 to residential owners in Converse County.  That works well in a county with significant mineral production.  I support letting Wyoming citizens vote on the 50% reduction initiative, however if it passes, local services will be cut in many cities, towns and counties, the math is that simple.  Local governments will have to make very tough decisions, as I do not expect the legislature to be able to fill that gap as they will also need to fill the gap in school funding caused by the reduction.

*As noted before, we must remain in the economic development game.  If we fail to play, we lose.  Some have characterized economic development as picking winners and losers, that is incredibly short-sighted.  It is about creating jobs for our kids and grandkids.  It is about reducing our dependence on mineral production and increasing job diversity.   Activity breeds activity and sound economic development strategies will pay dividends.  One thing the state can do to help is investment into infrastructure to support work force housing.  In many places economic development is hampered by the limited places for new workers to live.  If we can help with the infrastructure, we can lower the cost of the residential lot and get affordable houses for our workforce.  We also need to be sure the state vision includes local input.  Each community will have different desires, and we need to accommodate local visions.

I believe the campaign reporting requirements for candidates is clear and transparent, which is very important.  Filing donation reports a week before the primary allows the voters to know who is supporting each candidate and evaluate accordingly.

I lived at 6225 Mountainview Drive, Cheyenne, WY since 2005.  I am a 4th generation Wyomingite, born and raised in Lander. I have lived in Cheyenne for 28 years and in Fremont County for over 35 years.
68 (but whose counting?)

I am a graduate of Lander Valley High School and the University of Wyoming.  While growing up I worked on the family ranch feeding cows before class.  My first job out of law school was clerking for the district court in Lander.  In 1985 we moved to Cheyenne where I worked in the Attorney General's office.  Two of our three kids were born in Cheyenne.  We moved to Riverton in 1990 where I opened my own law practice.  In 2005 the family moved back to Cheyenne. I continue practicing law today. I have served in the Wyoming legislature since 2011 representing Cheyenne and Laramie County.  My service to the State has been one of the great honors of my life.

There are challenges and opportunities wherever you look.  To name a few:  Health care desserts coupled with ever increasing medical costs; community impacts from multiple property tax relief proposals with no backfill; Aging community infrastructure coupled with declining and aging populations and shrinking local revenues; fewer children and the less school resources due to legislative actions.  Despite such challenges, Wyoming remains one of the best kept secrets in America.  We have the people and natural resources to secure our way of life now and for years to come.  We have the lowest individual tax burden in the U.S.  Wyoming is consistently ranked as one of the best managed state governments in the country.  We do more with less.  We have a balanced and stable state budget, one of the best school programs in the world and developable natural resources to protect our standard of living and Wyoming way of life for generations to come.

We work on aspects of this problem every session. Health care related matters constitute the largest component of the State budget.  This is a all hands on deck issue. This includes ensuring adequate funding our healthcare workforce, reducing red tape to direct more time and dollars for direct care, expanding telehealth services and supporting rural emergency service districts.  We have funded new construction for the State Hospital in Evanston and Life Resource Center in Lander.  We have provide grants and matching funds of rural hospitals an clinics ARPA dollars for rural hospitals like the new hospital in Riverton.  Much more must be done, directly and indirectly.  Vibrant, attractive communities draw medical professional to Wyoming.  Maintaining existing services keeps providers here. All of us must continue to work together to protect our citizens and communities across the state.
No, with minor exceptions, such as adding state land to national parks (like the Kelly parcel) and potential small building sites to facilitate the construction of low-income employee housing.

We are the lowest taxing state in U.S. Our schools, local governments and special district rely on property tax revenue to provide essential services and maintain our standard of living for all of Wyoming. Further cuts will impact our ability to retain good health, quality education and essential services. Everyone loses.
To compete in regional and national markets, bring in new business, and help expand existing industries fostering economic development is a fundament building block for Wyoming's future.

Yes.  The political terrain in Wyoming has morphed in the past several years.  Out of state dark money involving negative, misleading, and often patently false statements are common place. Almost overnight it has become a multi-million dollar industry.  Transparency is essential to ensure fair and informed decision making by the public.
Cheyenne Wyoming, born and raised, I have lived here cumulatively for 26 years.

I bring experience in municipal government, cybersecurity, and the legal field. As a paralegal and having written legislation, I have conducted legal research, analyzed policy, and helped draft effective legislation. My background in government operations and technology gives me a practical understanding of today's challenges and the skills to serve our community with integrity and accountability.

Wyoming faces a shrinking and aging population, limited economic diversification, workforce shortages, and the outmigration of young people seeking opportunities elsewhere. In addition, we are grappling with access to healthcare, reliable infrastructure, and affordable housing.

I believe that addressing affordability is the key starting point. If we can ensure that people can both work and live in our communities, we can better retain young people, help fill workforce shortages, and strengthen access to essential services like healthcare. Improving access to healthcare, especially in rural areas, is critical to keeping families and communities healthy and stable. With a stronger population base, we can also expand our tax base, creating additional resources to invest in infrastructure, healthcare systems, and public services.

I believe the legislature can address this in several ways. One option is allowing the state to act as a reinsurer for health insurance providers operating in Wyoming, helping to stabilize risk and reduce costs. Similarly, the state could explore creating an opt-in public insurance pool, modeled after how municipalities pool resources to secure coverage.

Such approaches could increase bargaining power, improve market stability, and potentially provide greater flexibility in shaping rates and coverage options, while still preserving choice for consumers.

I do not support the sale or permanent transfer of public lands. However, I do support exploring alternatives such as land exchanges, long-term leasing, and cooperative management agreements that preserve public access while also allowing for responsible local economic development where appropriate.

I support addressing excessive property taxes through a combination of targeted relief and broader structural reform. This includes providing relief for homeowners, particularly those on fixed incomes, through tools such as homestead exemptions or circuit breaker programs that prevent tax burdens from rising faster than a household’s ability to pay.

At the same time, I believe we should pursue reforms that improve fairness and predictability in property tax assessments, helping prevent sudden or disproportionate increases in tax bills. We should also explore reasonable limits on excessive growth in property tax collections to ensure taxpayers are not overburdened by rapid increases in local levies.

I believe economic development works best as a partnership that combines the strengths of the public and private sectors bringing together the dependability and consistency of government with the speed, creativity, and innovation of the private sector.
The legislature should focus on creating a fair, stable, and predictable regulatory environment, while guiding investment in essential infrastructure such as roads and broadband and ensuring permitting and licensing processes are efficient and accessible.
The private sector is the primary driver of growth, leading through entrepreneurship, job creation, and innovation, and responding quickly to changing market needs.
When targeted incentives are used to attract investment or support key industries, they should be applied carefully, with clear accountability, transparency, and measurable results.

I believe campaign finance should be guided by transparency, accountability, and public trust. Contributions should never influence policy outcomes or create the perception that access to elected officials is for sale. Voters deserve to know who is funding campaigns and how that funding may be shaping political priorities.
I support reasonable and transparent campaign donation rules that allow for civic participation while ensuring full disclosure of contributions and expenditures. Public confidence in government depends on clear reporting, timely disclosure, and strict compliance to existing ethics laws.

My current address is 7504 Schrader Lane, Cheyenne, WY  82009;
We have been at this address for 20 years.
We have lived in Wyoming for 39 years.
I was born in Africa.  My parents were Lutheran Missionaries.

I am a Christian, Husband, Father, Grandfather, retired American Soldier, and a lifelong public servant, running as a Conservative, non-caucus Republican.  I enlisted in the Army as a teenager and served for 36 years, spending the last eight as the Commanding General of the Wyoming Military Department, where I was responsible for the roughly 3000 men and women of the WY Air and Army Guard.  I responded to multiple disasters within Wyoming and deployed to Kuwait in 2004.  After retiring from the Guard in 2019, I was privileged to lead another group of men and women as the Director of WYDOT for 4 years, retiring in 2023.  The twelve years spent leading a Wyoming Agency provided an excellent opportunity to observe State Government in action and work about 120 bills through the State Legislature.   

Education:  Master's in Public Administration from UW; Master's in Strategic Studies from the US Army War College; BS Education, BA Business / Accounting from Concordia University, Seward, NE

Challenges:  1) Reliance on fossil fuels and the associated revenue fluctuations; 2) Retaining our youth; 3) Healthcare access, specifically in rural areas. 

Opportunities:  1) Innovation and expansion in the fossil fuels and minerals sector, e.g. rare earths, nuclear power, and uranium; 2) Continued economic diversification; 3) Expand the tourism sector 

As a Legislator, I will work to:
1)	Support our legacy industries (energy, agriculture, tourism) and ensure we support innovation and development in those sectors.
2)	Continue to diversify our economy through retaining our favorable tax structure, working to reduce our regulations while lowering the cost and speeding up the permitting process.   
3)	Work to build thriving communities that create opportunities for our youth. I see this as providing good jobs, affordable housing, accessible healthcare, excellent education, and reliable infrastructure.
4)	Work to better match the skills and interests of our workforce to industry.

The establishment of the Health Insurance Affordability TF is an excellent first step by the Legislature to address the costs of health care insurance. The results of that task force should provide specific concepts for Legislative action.  In that same vein, I would support the Legislature participating in a multi-sector, multi-level health care task force that involves government, providers, and patients to address health issues across the state.  
Some specific areas I recommend exploring include: 1) Expanding the scope of practice; e.g. leverage the skill sets and expand the legal authority of midwives and nurse practitioners; 2) Growing the use of telehealth; 3) Working to stabilize our rural hospitals through leveraging the federal Rural Health Transformation Program 4) Tackling the uncompensated care issue; 5) Expanding the very successful WWAMI program; 6) Exploring incentives to encourage Medical Professionals to practice in Wyoming such as loan repayment.

I do not support the sale of public lands and applaud the actions taken by the Legislature to express the same.  Many residents of Wyoming use that land for recreation and hunting, which is one of the key reasons they live and work in Wyoming. Public lands also support our tourism industry, one of our key economic drivers.
We should be cautious about transferring Federal land to the State given the required costs of maintaining those lands, such as fire mitigation.  
I plan to support the management of these lands by: 1) Insisting on local input and respect for land-use plans before federal land decisions; 2) Pushing for adequate funding of the BLM and Forest Service so understaffing is not used as a reason to sell; and 3) Supporting Legislative resolutions such as SJ9 affirming opposition to broad federal land sales/exchanges.
I would also be interested in exploring the ability to execute narrow, locally-backed land swaps that improve public access, but not blanket transfers.

There is some real tension in this space, compounded by November’s ballot measure to reduce the Property tax by 50%.  My general approach will be to find a reasonable balance based on the following principles:

1)	Property tax provides revenue for essential services at the local level, revenue that is important for creating the thriving communities we need in Wyoming to retain our youth and support our economy.
2)	Government closest to the people is typically the best and should be locally supported.  
3)	I am an advocate for targeted tax relief, rather than broad exemptions.
4)	Local communities should be kept whole, either through backfill or other funding sources.  
5)	I am not an advocate for an increase in Sales tax.  I think it is regressive and would have negative consequences for the optional 2 cents.
6)	I am interested in exploring a “one size does not fit all” approach relative to mill levies by allowing local communities to adjust their levies by vote.

The State Legislature plays a key role in economic development across the state.

Fundamentally, I think the Legislature is responsible for establishing the business climate and environment so that businesses can prosper in their communities.
  
The government is responsible for providing the road transportation system our economy operates on, oversees and regulates utilities like water, wastewater, power and broadband that are essential for business development, ensures the funding of our education system, which trains the workforce our economy uses, and maintains law and order, which is essential to the stability of our economy.  

The Legislature establishes the tax structure that directly affects who invests in Wyoming and how, and funds capital-type grant and loan programs, such as the Business Ready Community Grant Fund, used across the state to set the conditions for economic development.

My opinion is that transparency and the ability to see who is donating is critical.  It is also important that we keep the playing field accessible to candidates without deep pockets or PAC funding.  I recommend that we allow the public to see who is funding candidates well before they vote.  

Some reforms that make sense to me include earlier and more frequent disclosure, a closer look at independent expenditure groups, and an easier search function online.
I live in Cheyenne, and have lived here since 1979, except for a short stint away to earn my bachelor's degree.  I was born in Columbus, Georgia, while my father was stationed in the US Army.
68 years old

I am a retired attorney that still serves my community as a lawyer.  I served as an administrator in the Child Support Enforcement, SNAP and TANF programs for the Wyoming Department of Family Services for several years.  I most recently represented debtors in the National Debt Relief program.  I'm active with St. Paul’s Lutheran Church in Cheyenne as the choir director and member of the Gifts Committee and Foundation Board.  I served for 16 years as an elected Trustee to the Laramie County Community College Board of Trustees.   I serve on the Board of Directors for My Front Door, a local housing program.  I also act and direct for the community theatre and play saxophone for local band ensembles.  I'm married to John and enjoy a blended family of four children and six grandchildren.  I want to bring real Wyoming values and people into the leadership of the state.  I strongly support public education, public land and water, and the vision of adequate and affordable health care for all.

I asked Wyoming voters on my Facebook page what they saw as the three top issues facing Wyoming's people.  The top three responses were Protecting Public Education, Affordable and Accessible Healthcare, and Affordable and Accessible Housing.  The cost of living and protecting our public lands and water were also named multiple times.  I would address protecting public education by making the constitutionally mandated funding a top priority.  We need to elect legislators that value education.  I come from a family of teachers and school administrators, and I served on the LCCC Board of Trustees for 16 years.  I saw the value of education for individuals and communities.  Medical bills was the #1 reason my clients faced financial crisis.  I would address a Wyoming solution to the ACA enhanced premium tax cuts, and continue to explore Medicaid expansion.  I would address affordable and accessible housing by proposing property tax breaks to new home buyers.

First, we need to explore a Wyoming solution to the federal cuts to the Affordable Care Act's enhanced premium tax cut.  We also need to continue to look at Medicaid Expansion and the services Wyoming Medicaid will cover.  I want to work with hospitals, doctors and insurers to discover solutions to these issues.  No one should go into financial crisis because they got sick. I would like to find out more about Wyoming becoming self-insured for healthcare so that insurance companies do not need to purchase secondary insurance, such as the programs in Florida and New Mexico. This could reduce the cost of healthcare.

I do not support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming.  Our public lands are valuable to our health and culture.  I met a young couple who said one reason they moved to Wyoming was enjoyment of our public lands.  Tourism and outdoor recreation are the second-highest drivers of our economy.  Our public lands attract business development and talented workers.  Our lands help diversify our economy.  We must be respectful stewards of the gifts of our lands.  We need to guarantee that our State Lands and Cultural Resources agency stays adequately funded.  We also need to elect state officials to the State Lands and Investment Board (SLIB) that value our public lands and support their management in Wyoming people's best interest.

I oppose a statewide elimination or 50% reduction in property taxes.  These cuts benefit the wealthy, high-value property owners in the state.  An owner of a $3 million-dollar home will benefit much more than an owner of a $300,000 home, and yet will expect the same services as good schools, police protection, trash removal, and maintained streets.  Our valuable public schools and reliable public services would suffer.  Reducing property taxes and increasing sales and other taxes is not the answer.  I want look at reductions for low-income households and for new home buyers.  We can use property tax cuts for those who need to buy a home and who need to remain in their home.

I am a strong proponent of small business development.  The new small businesses I see in Cheyenne are started by folks who are much younger than me and want to build a future in Wyoming.  The Wyoming Legislature can reduce barriers to small business development and can create a culture of growth and vision for the future.  Our leadership can create a business-ready and welcoming environment.

Every candidate must report campaign donations to the Wyoming Secretary of State.  The Secretary and staff receive these reports and should take action if irregular or illegal activities occur.  I cannot say at this point that Wyoming's system needs reform; but we need to elect a Secretary of State who will monitor campaign donations and take action against any illegal or corrupt action.
I was born in November of 1971 in Virginia where my father was station in the Army.  Our family returned to Pine Bluffs Wyoming in 1972 where I have lived for 54 years

I am a fourth-generation farmer and small business owner, representing the people of House District 10. Raised in Pine Bluffs, I graduated from Pine Bluffs High School in 1990 and earned a bachelor's degree from the University of Wyoming in 1995. After college, I returned home to farm with my brother and help grow several local businesses.

My commitment to service includes 16 years as President of Laramie County Fire District 5, election as Mayor of Pine Bluffs in 2021, and appointment to the Wyoming House of Representatives in 2025. I also serve on the Board of Directors for the Wyoming Municipal Power Agency and represent Eastern Laramie County through Cheyenne LEADS.

My wife, Kara, and I have built our life in Pine Bluffs and are raising our daughter, Keefer. I believe in limited government, fiscal responsibility, strong communities, support for agriculture and small business, and protecting Wyoming's way of life for future generations.

Wyoming’s biggest challenges are affordability, healthcare access, workforce shortages, housing, infrastructure and protecting our core industries such as energy and agriculture. Our opportunities include: energy, agriculture, tourism, small business, outdoor recreation and a strong quality of life. As a legislator, I would focus on practical, conservative solutions: keeping taxes low, supporting local control, investing wisely in infrastructure and workforce, and making sure state government lives within its means.

Healthcare is especially difficult in a rural state with long distances and small populations. Wyoming should support rural hospitals and clinics, expand telehealth, grow our healthcare workforce, reduce unnecessary regulation and look for ways to increase competition and transparency in pricing. I also believe we must carefully review federal opportunities that improve rural care without creating unsustainable long-term obligations for the state.

I generally oppose the sale of public lands. Public lands are an important part of Wyoming's heritage and support recreation, grazing, hunting, tourism, and economic development. Once public lands are sold, they are often lost to public access permanently. However, I recognize that limited and carefully considered land exchanges can sometimes be beneficial when they improve management, enhance public access, resolve boundary issues, or support critical infrastructure projects. Any proposed exchange should be transparent, provide a clear public benefit, and protect Wyoming's long-term interests.

People need property tax relief, especially homeowners on fixed incomes, but counties, schools, fire districts and local governments also rely on that revenue. I support targeted, responsible relief that protects residents without creating budget crises for essential services. The Legislature should look at exemptions and caps while being honest about impacts on local governments and special districts.  While recent legislation has addressed tax relief, we should consider a long term solution that would likely require a complete overhaul of the system.

The Legislature should not pick winners and losers, but it should create the conditions for growth: low taxes, reasonable regulation, reliable infrastructure, workforce training, housing availability and support for Wyoming’s core industries. Economic development should be locally driven, transparent and focused on long-term private-sector jobs.

My values are simple: be transparent, follow the law and never let a donation influence a vote. People deserve to know who is supporting candidates and how money is being spent. Wyoming’s system should be easy for the public to access, simple for candidates to comply with and strong enough to maintain trust in elections.  I believe our current system provides for those things so don't think there is a need for reform.

I was born and raised in House District 11, and moved back six years ago to be near my parents and siblings. I've spent stretches outside Wyoming over the years for school, my career, and military service, but Wyoming has been home my whole life, and it's where I've chosen to raise my own family.

I went to Deming, Miller, McCormick, Central High, and LCCC. I worked in the trades in my teens and 20s, then earned a bachelor's in business and a master's in engineering at the University of Colorado, with doctoral work in economics at the University of Hamburg. These days I direct the Wyoming Center for Business and Economic Analysis at LCCC, teach economics and public policy there, and serve as a space operations officer in the Air Force Reserve. Outside of that I'm a small business owner, I coach cross country at East High, and I help out with the Unaccompanied Student Initiative, the Children's Museum, and Rooted in Cheyenne. On both the civilian and military sides, the research I do and the decisions I make carry real consequences, and I'll bring the same discipline and expertise to my work in the legislature.

One of the best parts of running for office is going door to door and learning what matters to voters in HD 11, and many of those issues aren't the ones making headlines. Cost of living, data centers, and property taxes come up, but so do sales taxes, a shrinking workforce, and an instance where someone listed a constituent's address on a registry with no proof required. From talking with neighbors and employers and digging into the research, I see real opportunities to lower costs, expand our workforce, and keep our kids safe. We can address the fees and rules that drive up the cost of housing. We can broaden the economy with new industries without relying on taxpayers to foot the bill. We can support a variety of educational pathways that build our future workforce. The biggest challenge is looking past the noise to find what truly matters. It starts with listening, because the most important issues to me are the ones HD 11 cares about.

Healthcare is expensive here partly because demand keeps rising while supply stays thin. Wyoming is rural, providers are spread out, and there aren't many options. Much of what the Legislature can do is on the supply side, and we've made real progress: we repealed our Certificate of Need rules and joined the compacts that let out-of-state nurses and doctors practice here. The next steps are to extend that approach to more of the professions we're short on, expand telehealth so distance matters less, and grow our own providers through our community colleges. We can also require real price transparency, so families see what care costs before they're billed. None of this is a silver bullet, but more providers, more competition, and clearer prices move things in the right direction.

No. I don't support selling off our public lands or a wholesale transfer to the state. Around ninety percent of Wyomingites use public land to hunt, fish, work, and get outside, and once it's sold it's gone for good. Last year Washington came close to forcing the sale of millions of acres across the West, and Wyoming voters showed up to say no. The Legislature was right to pass a resolution opposing it, and I'll hold that line. Federal law already has a careful process for the rare case where a specific parcel should change hands for a clear public purpose, with public input. That's where any disposal belongs, not in a budget bill or a sales quota. Where I'd push is management. Wyoming should have a stronger, earlier voice in how these lands are run under multiple use, so grazing, energy, recreation, and conservation keep coexisting.

This is where my day job and homegrown knowledge of Laramie County both come in. The recent spike in tax bills wasn't a rate hike, it was inflation pushing up home values and assessments with them. Voters were right to want relief, and the state has now put real reductions in place, including help aimed at those who needed it most. We tend to picture one Wyoming economy, but no two counties are alike. Some lean on minerals, some on sales tax, some have very little to work with. So relief can't be one-size-fits-all, and it can't quietly shift the burden onto local businesses or the next town over. My approach is to protect homeowners without blowing a hole in the services people count on, and to hold the line on spending so we aren't forced to backfill with a shakier, more volatile tax. When revenue jumps just because prices went up, that windfall shouldn't automatically become more spending. Relief and discipline have to go together.

The Legislature's job is to set the table, not pick the winners. We do the most good by keeping the climate competitive for everyone: a simple tax structure, sensible regulation, reliable power and water, and a workforce trained for the jobs we actually have. I'd rather lower the barriers for the hundred businesses already here than write a special deal for one. Where I'm cautious is bespoke giveaways, the lifetime tax credit or discounted land for a single company that our existing employers never get. Those distort the market and hand taxpayers the risk. The better model is the one we've already seen work in Cheyenne, where major investments come in and pay their own way instead of leaning on local taxpayers. The piece I care most about is workforce. I work at our community college, and matching real training to real jobs is the most durable economic development there is. Build the people, and the businesses follow.

Voters deserve to know who's paying for and backing the campaigns asking for their vote. I'd rather run on what matters to HD 11 and what I actually believe and let voters see exactly where my support comes from. Sunlight does more good than complicated limits the well-connected always find a way around. On the system itself, the reports can be hard for an ordinary voter to find and make sense of. That's where I'd focus reform: keep existing rules but provide clearer, timelier, easier-to-search reporting, so the information is actually usable and not just technically filed. Transparency only works if people can see it. Give voters the facts and trust they're capable of weighing who stands behind a candidate.
Cheyenne, Wyoming. I was born in Wheatland and have lived in Cheyenne since i was two weeks old.

I am a lifelong Wyomingite, born in Wheatland and raised in Cheyenne. My career is defined by executive leadership and community advocacy. As CEO of the Cheyenne Animal Shelter and founder of Black Dog Animal Rescue, I have managed large-scale operations, balanced complex budgets, and built statewide coalitions. I hold a Master’s in Nonprofit Management (MNM) and am a Certified Animal Welfare Administrator (CAWA).

My civic dedication runs deep. Appointed by Governor Gordon to the Wyoming State Board of Veterinary Medicine, I have worked directly on statewide regulatory oversight. My leadership as past chair of the Wyoming Nonprofit Network and Leadership Cheyenne, combined with my experience in Leadership Wyoming, gives me deep insights into our local communities. I have a proven track record of bringing people together to achieve meaningful results, and I am ready to bring that transparent, fiscally responsible leadership to the Wyoming Legislature to serve Cheyenne.

Wyoming’s greatest challenge and opportunity lie in securing a vibrant future for our young people and creating communities where every family can thrive. True long-term fiscal stability doesn't just come from balancing budgets; it comes from investing in our people. Currently, we face the critical challenge of retaining our youth, who too often leave the state in search of better housing options, accessible healthcare, and diverse professional paths.

By shifting our focus toward sustainable community development, we can turn these challenges into monumental opportunities. As a legislator, I will bring my executive experience in building broad coalitions to advocate for public policies that strengthen local infrastructure, protect our unique quality of life, and ensure our public services are dependably funded. By prioritizing the well-being of our citizens and empowering our youth to build their lives right here, we will create a more resilient, prosperous Wyoming for all.

To ensure healthcare stability, the Legislature must prioritize a pro-people framework that leverages every available funding source. I support utilizing the proposed healthcare perpetuity fund to secure predictable, long-term investments in our medical infrastructure. We must take immediate action to protect our vulnerable communities, putting people first by directly addressing the devastating closures of rural hospitals and the critical lack of maternal healthcare and labor and delivery centers across the state.   

Furthermore, we need to direct robust state funding straight to hospitals to keep their doors open. The Legislature must also work closely with insurance providers to ensure equal coverage and access to hospital-based surgeries, ensuring patients aren't penalized for needing hospital care over an ambulatory surgery center. By stabilizing funding, we can ensure every Wyomingite has access to the care they deserve.

I do not support the sale or transfer of public lands. Our public lands are Wyoming’s greatest legacy, and responsibly managing them is critical to protecting our wildlife, preserving our heritage, and maintaining our thriving outdoor recreation and tourism industries. To ensure these lands are managed in the best interest of Wyomingites, the state must prioritize collaborative stewardship over privatization.

A specific, proven policy initiative I will continue to champion is the robust allocation of state funds—matched with federal infrastructure grants—specifically dedicated to wildlife crossing corridors and game fence construction. Projects like these directly protect migration routes, significantly reduce vehicle-wildlife collisions, and keep our ecosystems intact. By actively supporting these targeted conservation investments, the Legislature can practically safeguard our natural resources while keeping public lands open, accessible, and safe for all.

I do not support further property tax reductions. While relief for struggling homeowners is important, we must recognize that Wyoming taxes directly fund the essential public services our communities rely on. Past state analysis highlights that the average Wyoming resident receives upwards of $13,000 per year in public benefits and services—far exceeding what the typical household pays in taxes. Further eroding this revenue stream threatens the very fabric of our communities.

Instead of cutting resources, our focus must be on maximizing these investments to support our people where they need it most. We must protect this revenue to ensure our aging population has the ability to age with dignity at home, access critical healthcare, and secure dependable support for their daily needs. By maintaining strong public funding, we can stabilize our local emergency services, senior centers, and care networks, keeping Wyoming a safe, supportive place to live for a lifetime.

The Legislature’s role in economic development must be pro-people and rooted in meaningful collaboration. State entities like the Wyoming Business Council should serve as supportive partners, working hand-in-hand with our municipalities rather than imposing top-down mandates. The state’s role is to provide a helpful framework, robust resources, and guidance tied directly to the unique benchmarks and goals identified by individual communities themselves, ensuring we protect the distinct character that makes our towns special.

Instead of one-size-fits-all solutions, we must utilize state infrastructure and workforce training funds to invest heavily in human capital—supporting homegrown entrepreneurs, improving child care access, and building attainable housing. By empowering local leaders to guide their own economic futures, we can create an environment where young people choose to build their lives right here, strengthening Wyoming from the ground up.

Wyoming’s system for monitoring campaign finance requires meaningful reform to ensure absolute transparency and protect the integrity of our elections. My chief concern centers on the rise of independent expenditures by third-party organizations. Currently, hidden contributions—such as paid political door-knockers and dark-money operations—influence our local races without their expenses ever appearing on a candidate's individual filing. Voters have a right to know exactly who is funding the messages hitting their doorsteps.

To restore trust, all contributions to PACs and candidates must be clearly, promptly, and transparently reported to the public. Furthermore, the Legislature should implement strict limits on both monetary and in-kind contributions coming from out-of-state entities. Wyoming elections should be decided by Wyoming citizens, and our campaign finance laws must evolve to prevent outside interests from drowning out local voices.

I have lived in Laramie, Wyoming for the past 14 years. I was born in Cincinnati, Ohio but grew up in Pennsylvania. I moved to Laramie 14 years ago when I was offered a faculty position at the University of Wyoming College of Law.

I was a practicing lawyer in Pennsylvania for over 20 years when I decided to pursue a career in academia. I have taught in law schools in Michigan and Indiana before accepting a position at the University of Wyoming College of Law 14 years ago. I retired from that position two years ago and now hold the rank of Professor Emeritus. I specialized in teaching legal writing and trial advocacy. I served a two-year term as President of the Legal Writing Institute (an organization of over 2000 legal writing professors around the country and into Canada). I have co-authored a groundbreaking book on legal writing (now in its third edition). My combined 25 years of experience in teaching legal writing prepared me well to help write statutes as a member of the Wyoming legislature. Before I retired, I served one year as Chair of the University of Wyoming Faculty Senate. I have learned to collaborate with others, which is a vital skill in working with other legislators to get bills passed.

1. Affordable housing
2. Job opportunities

These two issues are the main drivers of outmigration of our young people. We need to help facilitate new industries and new, good-paying jobs to attract young workers, while also doing what we can to drive down the cost of housing. Since infrastructure is one of the primary costs in driving up housing, any assistance the state or local governments can provide to drive down the cost of infrastructure will help reduce the cost of housing.

3. Building stronger communities

We also need to insure that our local communities are welcoming and accepting of all people, old and young, gay or straight, regardless of political affiliation or religion. Diverse communities are strong communities and are places where people can thrive. There is much the legislature can do to promote strong and diverse communities.

Expand Medicaid. Support rural hospitals and health care delivery, including EMS services. Find ways to address the maternity deserts that are cropping up all over the state. Continue or expand support for WWAMI so that more young doctors locate in and hopefully remain in the state.

Absolutely not. I will support all efforts to let our federal representatives know that sale of federal land is a terrible idea, even if that land is transferred to state ownership. At the state level, I will continue to resist any effort to sell state land to private interests. I will consider supporting land swaps, where private land is traded for state land of equal or greater value. I will also support public/private partnerships to enter into conservation easements or other efforts to protect wildlife or migration routes or otherwise preserve open spaces in perpetuity.

I have proposed to the Joint Revenue Committee a new way of assessing land. The model is called "base year assessments." After a countywide fair-market-value assessment, all tax assessments would be frozen at that "base year" value for some period of time (say, 5 years) before being re-balanced. During the five year freeze, local governments would be given broader authority to adjust the millage rate to generate the revenue they need to provide the services that the public wants. It would be up to the local elected officials to have conversations with local residents to balance the need for services against the burdens of local taxes. And in the re-balancing years, local officials would be required to reduce the tax millage rate so that the total revenue generated in the rebalancing year is within a few percentage points of the previous year's revenue, so there would be no windfall in tax revenue due to accumulated inflation in real estate values.

At a minimum, the legislature should insure a level playing field so that all businesses, large and small, have equal access to resources and are not unfairly burdened by taxes or fees. But I am also open to targeted programs of loans, grants, or tax incentives to help attract new businesses, since providing jobs for our young people is also a high priority of mine. Those programs must be administered by a non-partisan board of citizens, who would evaluate all proposals on a cost/benefit basis according to established criteria. The amount of funding to support those programs should be appropriated by the legislature and adjusted annually after the effectiveness of each program is evaluated.

I am in favor of "bright money" while I am steadfastly opposed to "dark money." I define "dark money" as money that cannot be traced back to any individual or group of individuals. Thus, most political action committees (PACs) would be considered bright money, since they must report their donors to the state.

I was one of the founders of Wyoming Promise, a Wyoming PAC dedicated to calling for a constitutional amendment to ban dark money from Wyoming politics. I continue to advocate for that amendment in my role as a state legislator. Dark money is drowning out the ability of ordinary citizens to have their voices heard both in Cheyenne and in Washington. We need an amendment to the United States Constitution to overturn the Citizens United decision, and I will continue to work to get such an amendment proposed and ultimately ratified.
I've called Laradise home since 2004, moving from Georgia where my dad was stationed for his military career. I just celebrated my 20th year of marriage to a 6th generation Wyomingite from Wheatland.

I have 20 years of community building experience in Laramie, as an economic developer, a non profit director and public historian. I am a graduate of Leadership Wyoming, co-founder of the Albany County Housing Coalition, Laramie Mural Project, and am the senior member of the House Appropriations Committee. 

I utilize civility, honesty, optimism and empathy as essential leadership skills to serve my constituents, small businesses and hard working families. I enjoy learning new things and building relationships based on shared values. I am passionate about attainable housing, opportunities for young people, public education, access to mental health services and recreating on public lands.

We need to address the outmigration of our youth if we are to build strong communities. 

Young people want good paying jobs, attainable housing, affordable healthcare, access to mental health and maternity services, vibrant downtowns and outdoor amenities. The state should invest in these things and the systems that support them. 

We should establish an "economic infrastructure" trust as a long term funding source and work with the executive branch to get everyone “rowing” in the same direction. More agencies than just the Wyoming Business Council should be addressing this challenge. The legislature should work with the Dept. of Workforce Services, Tourism, State Parks, Dept. of Family Services, Dept. of Health, etc. to strategically leverage the state's resources to retaining our young people.

Wyoming is one of the most expensive states in the nation for health care. To address this, I will continue to support Medicaid expansion, increases to reimbursement rates for maternal and rural EMS services, educational incentive training and recruitment programs. 

Last session, I voted to use our Rural Health Transformation funds to support rural hospitals, for emergency and maternal care, primary prevention services, behavioral health, workforce education, and telehealth. 

I also support expanding Family and Medical Leave and opening the state Employee Group Insurance plan to school districts, small businesses and the self-employed.

Public lands belong to the public. They are NOT to be traded for corporate gain or sold to the highest bidder for private use. We need to ensure our state agencies that provide recreation (State Parks), hunting opportunities (Game & Fish), and conservation (Forest Service, WWNRT) are staffed appropriately with budgets that allow them to manage the land, collaboratively, with federal partners for the people of Wyoming.

Property tax relief should be targeted to those who need it most. I do not support blanket reductions for those able to pay, i.e. second homeowners and the ultra rich. Collected locally, property taxes provide essential community services - roads, water, emergency response, and public education that we all benefit from. 

I am opposed to further reductions in property taxes as this ensures services will decline, sales taxes will go up, and increases the likelihood of a statewide income tax.

The state should facilitate a landscape where hard working Wyomingites can innovate. I support policies that increase economic opportunities, good paying jobs and investments in community infrastructure. The legislature should facilitate, promote and invest in an entrepreneurial ecosystem that supports homegrown innovation while maximizing economies of scale. 

If we want to retain our young people, we need growth on our own terms. A commitment to making Wyoming the best version of itself. The state should support a robust economy built on our shared values, independent entrepreneurial spirit, and natural assets.

Constituents should have full access to the donations accepted by or spent on behalf of a candidate's campaign. From individual gifts to out-of-state corporations, voters have the right to know who is investing in and influencing our political process. 

More frequent reporting, random audits, and a requirement for organizations buying ads for or against candidates to reveal their funding sources, i.e. "dark money", would improve the transparency of our current system.
I have lived in Jackson since 2015. I currently live on the west side of town in a condo I purchased in 2018. I was born in Atlanta, Georgia.

I am a software engineer and small business owner, and I have had the honor of representing the most of the Town of Jackson in the Wyoming House since 2019, currently serving as House Minority Leader. I earned a computer science degree from Carnegie Mellon and spent a decade writing software before running for office.

Polarization has kept us from working on Wyoming. It has kept us from ensuring communities can thrive. It has threatened our access to the outdoors. And it has brought the vitriol from DC to Wyoming.

As Minority Leader I work across the aisle, because supporting Wyoming's communities has never been a partisan project.

The single most effective thing we could do is still pass Medicaid Expansion. Even after HR1, it would still reduce much of our uncompensated care which essentially gets passed on to all other payers.
No.

The public passed "Amendment A" to allow us to tax primary homes differently from other properties. Instead of implementing the option the citizens of Wyoming gave us, we've made an endless confusing shotgun approach to relief.

We need to clean up all of these exemptions and properly tax primary residences differently from other property, while keeping those most vulnerable to property taxes in their homes with programs like the refund program.
If we truly believe in thriving communities and ensuring our kids can grow up and start a business or find a great career, we will need to be part of making that happen.
Yes. The people should know who spends money on our elections.
I’ve called Rock Springs home for the past 22 years and have lived in Wyoming for the same amount of time. I was born in Denver, Colorado.

Rock Springs has been my home for 22 years. It’s where my husband and I raised our son and built our life. As an entrepreneur, I’ve spent years building businesses, developing programs and serving my community.

Politics was often the discussion around my family’s kitchen table, and I volunteered on my first campaign at the age of 16. That interest in public service has stayed with me throughout my life, including serving as Chairman of the Sweetwater County Republican Party. Those experiences taught me about the issues facing our communities and the value of steady, accountable leadership.

Wyoming’s greatest strength is its people and the industries that built our towns and communities. Our challenge is protecting our way of life while planning wisely for what lies ahead.

In the Legislature, I will work to protect Wyoming’s energy and mineral industries, keep taxes low, hold government accountable, support the infrastructure and public safety our communities rely on, and defend local control. I believe government should do the basics well, spend taxpayer dollars responsibly, and stay focused on the issues that matter most to Wyoming families and businesses.

Healthcare affordability and access are real challenges in Wyoming, but there is no single legislative solution. I believe the Legislature should work with healthcare providers and communities to identify practical solutions that improve access while keeping costs under control.
