That includes supporting rural providers and removing barriers that make it harder for Wyoming families to get the care they need.

Wyoming’s public lands are central to our economy, outdoor heritage and way of life. I support responsible multiple-use management that balances energy and mineral development, grazing, recreation, wildlife and public access. Wyoming should have a strong voice in decisions affecting our public lands, and I support policies that allow responsible development while ensuring these lands continue to support jobs, local communities and future generations.

As the discussion has shifted from property tax relief to property tax reform, we must recognize that taxpayer relief and local government funding are not competing goals. Wyoming families deserve a fair and sustainable tax system, and our communities deserve the ability to provide the essential services people rely on every day. Good stewardship requires us to pursue both at the same time.

The Wyoming Legislature should focus on building a strong foundation for economic growth, not picking winners and losers. If we want the private sector to invest in Wyoming, we must be willing to invest in ourselves by maintaining and building the arterial infrastructure that makes growth possible, including roads, water, sewer and utilities. Combined with a stable, predictable business climate, those investments create the foundation for businesses to grow, create jobs and strengthen our communities.

I believe Wyoming’s campaign finance laws should promote transparency and public trust while protecting the constitutional rights of citizens to participate in the political process and support the candidates and causes they believe in.

I’m a lifelong Wyomingite born right here in Rock Springs, raised in Uinta County, and settled in Sweetwater County for the past twelve years. My family’s roots have been in this soil for generations.

I’m a working-class Wyomingite, blue collar manual labor. I’ve spent years showing up for democracy as an election judge spending nights counting your ballots, traveling to other cities to be a delegate for Wyoming Democrats, phone banking, door knocking, and calling state and local officials who answered me with copy and paste form that amounted little more then silence. I follow the news, pay my bills, and still believe the process belongs to us. I've grown tired of watching decisions made about us, I've realized the people who live the laws should help write them. I’m not a career politician, just a Wyomingite who’s tired of seeing our voices left outside the Capitol doors. I’m stepping up so working wyomingites, the ranch hands, miners, teachers, cashiers, all have a seat at the table where their futures are written. If you've juggled bills, swung a hammer, or waited tables; you’re as qualified as any lobbyist to shape Wyoming’s next chapter for the better.

The biggest and broadest issue facing Wyoming as a whole is our dependence on the boom busted revenue. If mineral prices crash, severance tax vanishes and we slash schools, healthcare, and childcare leaving a barren system for growing families and an economic environment that can't support our graduates. I’d support enacting a 5% corporate profits tax on large out of state C-corps (currently its zero). Not raising taxes on the people struggling but on large corporations that use our labor and resources and help us reduce reliance on sales tax. The goal is to modernize Wyoming’s tax code so it supports diversified, long term growth, in addition to our extraction sector. Create a Stay & Thrive package with that revenue that would target critical pillars, such as universal broadband, health care, child care, education and public services. With these vital investments and fair corporate contribution, we can build the vibrant, supportive communities necessary to keep our young people home.

Healthcare costs are suffocating because our boom bust budget leaves hospitals under funded and workers under paid. Wyoming is one of ten states still refusing a corporate income tax, so we can’t build clinics, forgive rural nurse loans, or expand Medicaid wrap around funds that keep small hospitals solvent. I’ll fight for a 5 % profits levy on large out-of-state C-corps; the healthcare slice helps fund: loan forgiveness for providers who stay in frontier towns, subsidizes telehealth so ranch families see a specialist without a 300 mile drive, and caps out of pocket costs on chronic care prescriptions. No Wyomingite should delay care to avoid debt. I drive to Salt Lake regularly, burning leave time and motel money; I want to stay home, visit a local doctor, and sleep in my own bed. With fair corporate revenue we can keep hospitals open and paychecks here instead of exporting patients and their dollars across state lines.

As a legislator I support public lands remaining in the public domain; they belong to every Wyomingite, and once they’re gone, they’re gone for good. I would fight to ensure public lands serve Wyomingites by supporting revenue sharing from recreation leases and resource extraction that goes directly to local needs and infrastructure, not just the state, so the local community can use the money where it needs it without begging Cheyenne. State and federal contracts should favor local hiring, so when the BLM needs a fence built, a Rock Springs welder gets the paycheck, not a Colorado corporation. I’ll also push to create citizen oversight boards in every county, made of the people who live there, ranchers, hunters, tribal members, hikers, with real veto power over major land use decisions; if it doesn’t work for the people who ride, hunt, ranch, or pray on that ground, it doesn’t happen.

Property tax relief sounds great until small towns can’t keep their lights on and high schools are talking about cutting sports. I won’t support blanket cuts or hikes that slam fixed income neighbors. Instead, let’s keep owner occupied rates flat, give counties a menu: modest voter-approved increases on second homes, and a 3 % vacancy tax on empty second houses and shuttered storefronts that sit idle season after season. Every dollar from those measures and from a cut of a 5 % corporate profits levy on large C-corps flows back into the community. Let the communities decide how to use the funds, save athletic programs, keep the clinic open, fix the roadways. One size never fits a state where assessed values swing from Jackson condos to small single family homes; local control plus fair corporate and vacancy contributions keeps Wyoming’s promise of low taxes without stripping the services that let communities thrive.

The Wyoming Legislature should absolutely act as a facilitator in economic development. We must diversify beyond volatile severance taxes by developing knowledge intensive sectors. Focus on digital technology, embrace energy diversification, and agricultural technology that solve problems for resource industries while building standalone capabilities supporting us through boom bust cycles. The goal isn’t moving away from extracting resources but adding industries that don’t depend on the commodity cycles. The Legislature's role should help transition Wyoming from a resource dependent economy to one that also retains graduates and competes nationally in other sectors, keeping our youth here.. When we broaden our economic base, we strengthen every community from coal towns to agricultural centers ensuring Wyoming thrives no matter what markets do elsewhere.

I value elections where ideas beat bank accounts, not the biggest wallet. Wyoming should cap campaign spending based on registered voter counts for legislative races and set firm limits for statewide offices. Require every PAC or nonprofit to disclose the real, living donors, no more shell game front groups. Provide all qualified candidates with digital ad credits and broadcast airtime so ranch hands, teachers, and miners can run without courting millionaires. Public office should go to the person who best shares our values, not the one with the richest donors. By limiting overall spending, forcing full disclosure, and giving everyday candidates an equal microphone, we ensure every voter hears every voice. That keeps elections in the hands of neighbors, not out of state special interests, and restores trust that your representative is working for you, not for the loudest checkbook.
I was raised on a farm in Star Valley, Wyoming.  I currently live on a ranch north of Green River, Wyoming and have lived on this ranch for 32 years.

I am a retired Oil and Gas Manager and a Chemical Engineer by trade.  I have served in the Wyoming Legislature since 2021.  I currently serve as House Majority Floor Leader, Chairman of the House Minerals, Economic Development & Banking Committee, and Co-chair of the Select Committee for School Recalibration.  I am a member of the Joint Legislature Management Council, Wyoming House Rules Committee, 14 State Energy Council Executive Committee, and University of Wyoming's Energy Resources Council Board of Directors

Our minerals industry has lost 17% of their employees over the past several years as fossil fuels have been under attack.  Wyoming is rich in natural resources and we need to unleash the potential that Wyoming has to supply energy to the nation.  I have been working to bring a new coal-fired power plant to Wyoming to demonstrate the newest scientific and technological advances which allow coal to be used with very little emissions.  Through my efforts locally, and with the Trump administration, Wyoming could get 2 or 3 new coal-fired power plants in the near future.

Our most vulnerable citizens are our Senior Citizens.  I have been an advocate for taking care of our aging population and providing support for them to remain in their homes as long as possible.  I have sponsored budget amendments to provide in-home support for seniors and to help them stay independent as long as possible.

Public lands need to remain public.  In the past, Wyoming has not been involved in the public land decisions that impact our state.  I want Wyoming citizens to have more input into these decisions that impact us locally.  Jointly with the Federal Government, we be more effective managing our pubic lands, but we, as Wyoming, need a seat at the table

Not until true conservatives gained leadership positions in the Legislature 2 years ago, did Wyoming taxpayers finally get property tax relief.  To make up for much of the lost revenue to local government entities, we increased the Direct Distribution funds by nearly 50% last session. In this manner, we were able to bring relief to the Wyoming taxpayer while still providing the necessary revenue for local services.

Many of our communities need infrastructure repairs and improvements.  You cannot bring new businesses into a community if the wastewater treatment plant or potable water supply is insufficient.  Many towns need street improvements.  I have been working on a Consensus Block Grant Program for local communities to support infrastructure improvements and repairs.  With this program, local communities decide how to spend the grant money instead of a few bureaucrats in Cheyenne making those decisions

I rarely accept money from Lobbyists, Political Action Committees or groups that are trying to influence policy.  Those donations usually have strings attached.  I believe the Wyoming Legislature needs to stay focused on the needs of the citizens, and not paid lobbyists.
I live in Kemmerer. I have lived in Kemmerer for the past 53 years. Born and raised in Wyoming 64 years.  Born in Rawlins

I have served 32 years in the Wyoming Court System as elected Clerk of the District Court, where I have worked to uphold the integrity of the Judicial system and ensured accountability and fairness under the law. Currently a member of the Wyoming Game and Fish Commission being president from 2022-2023. Prior served 8 years on the Wyoming Board of Parol, the last two being chairman. Served on the state committee for Lincoln Bicentennial.

The State's economy depends on oil, coal, and natural gas to generate significant tax revenue, but consistent long-term market changes and price swings make them too susceptible to outside influence that we cannot control.  Economic diversification is a key ingredient to bring some balance; we need to build new industries utilizing Wyoming's pre-existing mines and current industry as anchors.  Advanced manufacturing and Technology working hand and hand with our businesses is a great opportunity. Create tax incentives for businesses that bring better paying jobs.

As a legislator I would champion Wyoming's heath care system. Recruit physicians, nurses, mental health providers. Keeping our local hospitals in business and clinics assessable is a must. Paying for the medical services in rural areas is an obligation and I believe a big part of living in our great state. We need to expand primary care, increase price transparency increase insurance competition and expand telehealth care. Let's not forget our great EMS professionals we need to recruit new personnel and train them adding incentives.

I would never support any selling or transferring public land in Wyoming. Public land is Public. Everyone that lives in Wyoming enjoys outdoor recreation and our magnificent wildlife.  We are entrusted to protect and preserve or public land for future generations and wildlife.

In Wyoming, property taxes do not mainly fund the state government, they fund local communities. They pay for county roads, sheriff's offices fire districts, libraries, emergency services, senior centers, weed and pest districts, and many of the everyday services people rely on without even thinking about them. Everyone loves cutting taxes myself included. Tax relief should focus on our seniors and fixed income citizens first and foremost. Economic Diversification and keeping our younger population in the state would help the government short falls and create revenue. Cutting taxes and laying the burden on the local governments does NOT solve any problems it just creates new ones.

The legislature should help economic development not hinder it.  Let the Business council, Wyoming Business Alliance, city chambers and other economic development organizations lead the charge, they are the professionals who know what is best for the state. As a legislator I would listen carefully and make conscious and thoughtful decisions on funding.
I see no problem at this time as long as we maintain full transparency and we monitor it closely, if needed we can create new legislation.
I live on the Sommers Ranch along the Green River in Sublette County.  I have lived there my entire 67 years of life. I was born in the hospital in Jackson Wyoming.

I am a life long Sublette County rancher that has served both the rancher community, my local communities, and the State of Wyoming for decades on various boards and commissions.  I served for 12 years in the Wyoming House of Representatives, and I was Speaker of the House for the 67th Legislature (2023-2024).

At a statewide level, how does Wyoming do a better job of youth retention. This is coupled with the greater challenge of how does Wyoming grow through economic development (providing jobs for Wyoming's youth), while preserving the custom, culture, values and landscapes of Wyoming and its people. Wyoming cannot say no to everything. We have to enable and welcome industries  to Wyoming, but under our terms.  I am a big supporter of local control.  Let local communities decide what is best for them through their local siting regulations.  The State needs to make sure industries don't negatively impact our water, our environment and our electric grid. 

Locally, the issues and politics around the Colorado River and its tributaries are of paramount importance.  Wyoming needs to invest in our Attorney General's office to fight for our water, investment in the State Engineer's office, and invest in water projects in the basin and around the state(including water conservation projects).

Having served 12 years in the Wyoming Legislature, I know that I cannot be an expert in every arena. I think we need to turn to our very competent Department of Health, Wyoming Medical and Insurance professionals, and health advocacy groups to seek solutions that work for Wyoming.  Wyoming's small population creates incredibly difficult challenges for providing low cost healthcare.

I support SJ0009, Keeping public lands protected and decisions local  - This resolution passed last session. The core of it is this:
  That the Wyoming Legislature opposes any state or federal legislation or policy that promotes the broad or indiscriminate sale or exchange of public lands.
  That the state of Wyoming demands Congress and federal agencies respect existing land management frameworks, county land-use plans and the voices of local communities.
  That the Wyoming Legislature supports responsible, locally supported land exchanges, adjustments or disposals under existing laws and procedures when they demonstrably enhance public access, natural resource use or community benefit.
  That the Wyoming Legislature supports adequately funding state and federal agencies and the corresponding programs that exist so that the agencies can efficiently and effectively review land purchases, exchanges or limited disposals under the existing legal framework.

I prioritized property tax relief during my term as Speaker of the House, we passed at least five pieces of legislation designed for tax relief. Subsequently, the Legislature has passed even more relief.  We are now at a point where we are impacting local government services and funding for education. Any property tax relief going forward must be carefully weighed against its impacts to local government and education funding. Property taxes was how Wyoming's founders, literally its embedded in our Constitution, funded local government and education. Property taxes is embedded in so many areas of the law that eliminating them would require years of work.  How would we replace those revenues and with an increase in what kind of tax? I believe I have the experience to understand these big picture issues, and how to navigate the best solutions.

I support the Wyoming Business Council and the Wyoming Energy Authority. I support both of their efforts to attract business to Wyoming. We need jobs to retain our youth. Every state in the nation does this. I do agree that  we need to take a hard look at the Wyoming Business Council to determine what is working, what is not working, and how we should move the Council to best serve Wyoming.

I accept personal donations to my campaign from individuals.  I am NOT currently taking any PAC money for my campaign.  I have done it both ways in past elections. I think Wyoming's system is within the constraints of what the courts will allow.  Any real reform would take a US Constitutional Amendment. I am open to the idea of campaign reform. The Dark Money PACs that have invaded Wyoming politics are making campaigns more expensive and are often misleading voters.
Bondurant, Wy. Physically lived there 20 years. Born Wilmington DE. While on active duty, Wyoming and Bondurant was my claimed residence beginning in 1978..
30 years active duty, US Navy. Nuclear Submarine Force. BS US Naval Academy, MSE The Catholic University of America. Eagle Scout. Active in observing and speaking to our legislature.

Water in the Colorado watershed. Energy development to include nuclear. AI and data centers. Regarding the watershed, there will come a time when it is in court. Wyoming must be prepared for this. Continued development of coal, oil and natural gas is important to our economy and tax receipts. Nuclear is on the horizon but must be developed. Data Centers are coming and Wyoming must decide how many and where they belong.

Increased staffing. The Program that pays education costs in exchange for future practice in Wyoming is very good and must be supported. Better use of internet and tele-medicine is important. Facility support should be considered.
Public access to public lands must be maintained (eg hunting, fishing and recreation). I find roadless areas too extensive for good forest management.

There are several ways to support these elements of county services. Probably the simplest is sales tax. As electric power generation expands taxation there may be useful. Property tax relief should be carefully applied to residential properties.

The Legislature must ensure that bureaucracy rules are minimized. Bureaucracies always tend to expand their reach and authority, the legislature must pay attention to that. Bureaucracies can expand to fill the money available. Too many "good ideas" can spoil an economy.
Reform is not needed. The people should be able to donate to their candidate of choice. The only thing that needs to be carefully monitored is out of state donations.
Afton Wyoming.  I have lived here all my life except for my during my college years.

I have a deep love for the State of Wyoming that was instilled by my family who established the Valley and Western Wyoming.  
I taught/coached and advised at Star Valley High School for 29 years after teaching in Utah for one year.  I was also the high school teacher representative to the administration and school board for most of those years.  I also was active in many community activities and committees during that time including recreation, multiple use, forest collaborations and fish and game.   I then served as a precinct committeeman for the Lincoln County Republican Party.  I later served as the vice chair of that committee.  
I was then elected to House District 21 Representative.

Wyoming faces some major issues going forward but has many things in its favor.  
I like to focus on the many good people that have Wyoming's best interest in mind.  I believe that good ideas and leadership come from many different groups and factions.  However, the deep division in the Republican Party has caused more harm than good.  We need to focus on "being on the same" team and working to make Wyoming better and not to "burn it down" to make one point or another.  
I am focused on improving education, creating jobs and promoting our many energy possibilities.  We also must work to solve the problem of why our best and brightest are leaving the state.  Quality and quantity of good paying jobs have to be our focus as well as affordable standards of living.

We must make this a priority in word and deed.  We have to work together to recognize and fund our unique health care problems.  We have to get over comparing our state to that others.  We are unique but can make it better if we move this up on list of urgent issue to solve.  We have the resources to help and the hardworking people to do as much as they can  to proactively participate.
I do not support efforts to sell public land.  They are very important to our way of life.  We must make a strong stand on this and not let national interests overwhelm us that live here.

I have been very active in approving major steps to provide tax reform.  I will continue to do this.  I also recognize that we must fund our schools, towns, counties and special districts because they are what makes Wyoming great.
I believe that more oversight and  accountability can be provided.  I think the State can and should be a partner in selected projects to allow growth and development under tight and fair guidelines.
Campaign donations should be closely monitored.  Out of State donors must be identified and transparent.

I live in Jackson and have been here since 2008. Previously, I lived in Saratoga and Cheyenne, and have lived in Wyoming most of my adult life and spent summers in Saratoga as a child. I was born in Cleveland, OH.
68 years old

I am the Democratic state representative for Wyoming House District 23 and the President and CEO of the George B. Storer Foundation. I was first elected in 2022 to represent Teton County, where I focus on practical, bipartisan solutions that reflect Teton County values and strengthen communities across our state.

I lead my family’s foundation, which supports early childhood education, climate and conservation work, and Wyoming communities, and I’ve helped modernize its governance, investment policy, and strategic direction since 2008. A lifelong angler and conservationist, I was the first woman to serve on the U.S. Fly Fishing Team and was honored with the Theodore Roosevelt Conservation Partnership’s Conservation Leadership Award in 2019.

I hold BA and MFA degrees from the University of Southern California’s School of Cinema, and I bring that storytelling and strategic perspective to my legislative and philanthropic work.

Wyoming’s biggest challenges are economic dependence on boom‑and‑bust fossil fuel revenues, workforce and housing pressures, and protecting land, water, and communities amid rapid change. Our opportunities lie in diversifying the economy, investing in people and infrastructure, and leveraging world‑class natural assets and communities like Teton County as innovation hubs.

As a legislator, I would:

Pass a long‑term fiscal plan that diversifies revenue and supports local governments.

Invest in education, job training, childcare, broadband and cell service so Wyomingites can thrive in new industries.

Tackle housing and land‑use policy so workers and families can afford to live where they work.

Protect public schools, clean water, and public lands as pillars of our economy and quality of life.

Model civil, solutions‑focused leadership rooted in Wyoming values.


.

As a legislator, I would do the following: 

Expand Medicaid with a Wyoming‑specific plan to cover thousands of low‑income adults, cut uncompensated care and stabilize premiums and rural hospitals.

Use the new federal Rural Health Transformation dollars to keep local hospitals, EMS and clinics open and invest in telehealth, behavioral health and primary care.

Raise targeted Medicaid reimbursement rates (maternity care, EMS, rural providers) so more doctors and hospitals can afford to serve Wyoming patients.

Spur competition and innovation in the insurance market, including exploring a high‑risk/catastrophic pool and stronger rate review to push down premiums.

Grow a homegrown healthcare workforce through loan‑repayment, training partnerships and incentives for providers to practice in rural and frontier communities.

I do not support large‑scale sale or transfer of Wyoming’s public lands to private interests or away from public ownership. Our lands are the backbone of our economy, outdoor heritage and communities, and once they’re sold, we don’t get them back. 

As a legislator, I would:

Oppose public‑land sell‑offs and support keeping lands public, open and accessible.

Ensure state trust lands are managed to grow long‑term value for school and other beneficiaries while balancing conservation and community needs on the ground.

Back “multiple use, local voice” management that balances grazing, hunting, recreation, conservation and responsible energy with real input from Wyoming communities.

Require agencies to plan for climate‑driven drought and wildfire and invest in forests, rangelands and watersheds to build long‑term resilience for people, wildlife and local economies.

I support targeted relief that keeps people from being taxed out of their homes without blowing a hole in county and school budgets.

As a legislator, I helped champion Constitutional Amendment A, which gives us a constitutional way to lower taxes on owner‑occupied primary homes, not second homes or commercial property. By contrast, the 4% cap is delivering the biggest breaks to wealthy second‑homeowners and creating huge disparities in how similar properties are taxed, which raises serious questions about whether our system is still fair, uniform and equal under the Wyoming Constitution. 

Going forward, I’d lean on Amendment A, targeted state‑funded relief tools, and fairer state‑local revenue sharing so every county can tailor solutions that protect primary‑residence homeowners while keeping schools, EMS and local services strong.

The Legislature absolutely has a role in smart economic development that serves Wyoming people, not just the next boom. I believe we should be more aggressive and strategic in using a portion of our permanent funds for rotating, low‑interest loans through SLIB and the Business Council to build community infrastructure, workforce housing and other critical projects the private market is struggling to or can't finance on its own. Done prudently, these investments keep the funds intact, recycle dollars through Wyoming communities, help grow our workforce, keep more of our kids and grandkids here and diversify the economy. By investing in our communities and people, new industries will be more inclined to consider Wyoming as a place to grow a business.

I believe transparency and accountability in campaign finance are essential to earning and keeping the public’s trust. I support taking donations that reflect Wyoming values, disclosing them clearly and on time, and refusing money that comes with strings attached or undermines public confidence.

Wyoming’s current disclosure system is better than it used to be but still has big gaps, especially around late, hard‑to‑track contributions and outside "dark money" spending. I think the Legislature should strengthen reporting rules so voters can easily see who is funding campaigns before they cast their ballots, improve enforcement and auditing, and bring more sunlight to dark‑money “issue” campaigns that are really about influencing elections.

I currently live in Cody, Wyoming. I was born in Cody, grew up here, and have spent nearly my entire life in Wyoming.

After graduating from high school in 2004, I moved to Las Vegas to attend UNLV’s William F. Harrah College of Hospitality, where I prepared for my future role in our family business, Blair Hotels. After graduating in May of 2009, I attended real estate school, earned my license, and built a successful real estate career in Las Vegas.

In 2014, I returned home to Cody to carry my share of the family business and continue building my life in the community that raised me. Other than my time in Las Vegas from 2004 to 2014, I have been a lifelong Wyoming resident.
I will be 40 years old.

I am a fifth-generation Park County resident, a third-generation hotelier, and a father to two young children — my son, Quin Jude, and my daughter, Brittania Adalyn “Ruthie” Blair. I was born and raised in Cody, and other than my time in Las Vegas attending UNLV’s William F. Harrah College of Hospitality, Wyoming has always been home. At UNLV, I graduated with a 3.96 GPA, completed Honors studies, and earned a Marketing minor.

I serve as Chief Marketing Officer of Blair Hotels, a family business operating five Wyoming hotels, including three in Cody. My work has given me practical experience in business leadership, budgeting, workforce challenges, tourism, marketing, and economic development.

My public service includes two terms on the Wyoming Office of Tourism Board, where I served as Vice Chair. I currently chair the Park County Travel Council and Cody Fireworks Committee, serve with the Cody Community Foundation, I serve on the committee of PEAKS to Conga and ride it each year.

The greatest challenge facing Wyoming is protecting our way of life while preparing responsibly for the future. That challenge is made harder by outside influences that do not understand Wyoming, our values, or the people who built this state.

We must protect the freedoms we hold dear: free speech, the Second Amendment, property rights, local control, and the pursuit of liberty and happiness. At the same time, we have to make sure Wyoming remains strong, competitive, and affordable for the next generation.

As a legislator, I would focus on accountability, fiscal discipline, and common sense. Every tax dollar should be spent carefully, with zero tolerance for waste, fraud, or abuse. We should make sound investments in industries that strengthen our state’s finances, encourage business, support working families, and defend Wyoming against federal overreach and the erosion of our rights.

The Legislature should focus on practical solutions that lower costs, keep care close to home, and strengthen the providers we already have.

In Wyoming, access is often just as important as affordability. We need creative, conservative solutions that improve quality of life while minimizing costs and maximizing efficiency. One example is helping seniors age in place. Supporting family caregivers, home health services, transportation, meal delivery, senior centers, and basic home modifications can help older Wyomingites stay safely in their homes longer. That is better for families, better for communities, and often far less expensive than institutional care.

We should also expand telehealth, support rural clinics and hospitals, improve EMS coverage, recruit and retain healthcare workers, and remove unnecessary barriers that make care harder to provide. The goal should be simple: better access, lower costs, stronger local care, and responsible use of taxpayer dollars.

No. I do not support selling or transferring public land in Wyoming. Public land belongs to the public, and it should remain available for the benefit of the people; not sold off, locked up, or handed over to special interests.

Pride in the land is a core Wyoming value. Our public lands support hunting, fishing, grazing, energy, tourism, recreation, conservation, and the freedom to enjoy the outdoors. They are also part of the heart and soul of what makes Wyoming special.

The best path forward is responsible, Wyoming-first management. That means keeping access open, respecting multiple use, involving local voices, protecting wildlife and natural resources, supporting agriculture and energy where appropriate, and pushing back when federal agencies make decisions without understanding the people who live here. Public land should be managed for the people, by the people.

Property tax relief is important, but Wyoming should not pretend every county has the same economy or the same options. The answer is not a blanket approach from Cheyenne. It is giving counties room to use the strengths they already have.

In Park County, that strength is tourism. The lodging tax is overwhelmingly paid by visitors, not local families. It helps market our community, support local businesses, strengthen jobs, and bring outside dollars into our economy.

That matters. Roughly 30% of Park County’s sales tax is paid by visitors, helping lower the tax burden on the people who live and work here year-round. Those visitor dollars support local services and infrastructure without relying as heavily on residents to cover the cost.

As a legislator, I would support real property tax relief while protecting local tools that allow counties to solve problems their own way. Local solutions beat one-size-fits-all government.

The Wyoming Legislature should play an important role in economic development by creating the conditions for businesses, workers, and communities to thrive. Government should not pick winners and losers, hand out unfair advantages, or subsidize business models that cannot stand on their own.

The Legislature’s role is to create the conditions where business can succeed. That means strong infrastructure, reliable roads, reasonable regulation, low taxes, access to workforce training, support for trades and agriculture, and a business climate that rewards hard work and investment.

Economic development should start with Wyoming businesses, Wyoming workers, and Wyoming communities. The state should remove barriers, protect opportunity, and make smart investments that strengthen our long-term economy, not use taxpayer dollars to fund favored projects while hard-working Wyomingites pick up the tab.

Wyoming campaigns should be driven by Wyoming people, Wyoming communities, and Wyoming interests. I am concerned when outside money flows into local races, we risk outside agendas that do not reflect our values or our way of life. Elections should not be bought by those with no real stake in the communities affected by the outcome.

I will always value local support over outside influence, and believe campaign finance rules should help voters see who is trying to shape Wyoming’s future.

If reform is needed, it should be practical and focused. Reports should be clear, public, and easy to search. Rules should be simple enough for grassroots candidates to follow, and penalties should target fraud and intentional violations, not honest mistakes. The goal should be trust, not additional bureaucracy.

I live in Powell.  I have lived in Powell for 6 years since retiring from the military.  I have been a Wyoming resident my entire life, but was physically in different areas while in the military after college.  I was born in Cody.

I am a third-generation Wyomingite.  I was born in Cody and grew up in Powell.  I attended Northwest College, then the University of Wyoming and earned a bachelor's in electrical engineering.  I joined the Navy as an Officer after college and worked at Naval Reactors and Naval Sea Systems Command designing ships, submarines, and their systems.  I have received many awards for my leadership and hard work for our Nation's security including the Department of Defense Value Engineering Award and the Department of the Navy Cyberspace/IT Person of the Year.  I am a Wyoming Republican Party Platform candidate.  I will protect personal rights to life, liberty, property, and the pursuit of happiness as absolutes.  I will protect parent rights and their right to choose what is best for their children.  I will protect gun rights.  I am a fiscal conservative and will work to reduce taxes while driving for more economic investment to balance budgets.  I will protect public land.

I will start by saying we live in the most amazing and beautiful State in the Union.  We have a wealth of public lands which we can all enjoy.  There are forces attempting to take and sell these lands.  I will fight to keep our lands public.  Second, I am deeply concerned with the volume and proliferation of the theft of personal data by big tech firms and even auto manufacturers.  I will introduce legislation to protect personal data from theft.  Third, I am concerned by the push to erode parental rights.  I will protect parents’ rights to choose what is best for their children. 
I am a fiscal conservative and will work to reduce taxes which have disproportionately impacted our community seniors.  I will drive for more economic investment to balance budgets.  I am also concerned that we are not making care at home as accessible for our seniors as it should be.  I will fight to make the common sense and more economically viable solution available for all our seniors.

I remember how affordable health care was before Obamacare, so the obvious answer is rolling that back.  Unfortunately, that is a federal issue to tackle.  What we can do is cut unnecessary regulation that forces overhead in the health care systems in Wyoming.  We have tremendous people doing their best at our local hospitals and care facilities.  The 307 Health model should be examined.  In Powell, it has proven to be successful and affordable.  I would point out that part of that success is lack of overhead and bureaucracy. I believe that care at home for our seniors is another critical aspect.  The Powell Senior Center is another example of what right looks like.  Making hygiene care, transportation, food, and social gatherings simple and affordable.  The Wyoming legislature should support these examples and decrease overhead and bureaucracy to the largest degree possible.

Public land in Wyoming needs to remain public land.  I would support transfer of the land from the Federal government to the State government provided there was a plan to continue to support the land financially.

I fully support property tax relief.  Wyoming has one of the most pro-business environments in the nation.  We should be able to attract business to Wyoming to offset the property tax relief.  I will make this a priority.  We need to offer more local jobs to keep our youth here.  We should consider consumption or point-in-time tax options.  Unfortunately, our property taxes disadvantage our seniors who have already paid their dues and are now living off of a fixed and very modest income.  This affects widows even more.  If we can end unnecessary bureaucracy with a scalpel in the state's budget that will help as well.  It will require a lot of work, but I am up for the challenge.

Wyoming has an incredible business environment.  We need to evaluate why businesses aren't flocking here like they are to Texas and Florida.  I am sure part of it is infrastructure and part of it is perception that hiring will be challenging in a low population area.  We need to address these issues with industry.  I strongly believe we can address both of those concerns, but we need to advertise all of the advantages to industry and help provide any missing infrastructure and alleviate concerns about hiring.  Wyomingites have grit and determination and make the absolute best employees.  We need to capitalize on this fact with industry.
My values are absolute transparency at all times.  I am new this year, but from what I can tell Wyoming's system for monitoring campaign donations is reasonable.

Powell, Wyoming, 34 years, New York
Living in Powell for 34 years, I am rooted in the community.
I have spent 20 years as Powell Mayor and City Council Representative. I also served as President of the Powell Chamber of Commerce. Those roles were working positions that put me at the table for real decisions affecting real people.

Beyond city government I have represented Powell on the Wyoming Association of Municipalities board, served on the Park County Travel Council, and aided citizens from the Yellowstone County Assistance Network board. I have been part of projects ranging from building homes with Habitat for Humanity to developing the city’s high speed internet infrastructure, bringing a gluten free oats processing facility to Powell, building the community aquatic center, and championing a Shoshone River boat ramp south of town.

What connects all of that is a simple philosophy: show up, do the work, and remember that the best outcomes happen when a community works together.

Wyoming faces challenges with an economy heavily dependent on mineral revenues that fluctuate, a workforce shortages and communities working hard to remain viable as demographics shift.

The opportunity is that Wyoming still has choices other states have already lost. We have no income tax, manageable debt, and industries — energy, agriculture, and tourism — that the rest of the nation depends on.

As a legislator I would focus on three priorities: local control — the people closest to a problem are best equipped to solve it, protecting Wyoming's public lands for the hunters, ranchers, recreationalists and energy producers who depend on them every day; and ensuring that the revenues generated in communities like Park County find their way back to those communities in the form of roads, water infrastructure, and services that make them places worth staying.

Wyoming's future is bright if we make smart decisions now. I intend to be part of making those decisions well.
When the nearest specialist is hours away and premiums consume a growing share of a family's income, that is a real problem with real consequences for working people.

The Legislature should focus on policies that increase competition and transparency in healthcare pricing, reduce administrative burdens on rural providers and critical access hospitals that are already operating on thin margins, and explore workforce incentives that attract and retain medical professionals in underserved rural areas.

I am also open to honest conversations about what other states have done to address rural access issues, evaluated on their actual outcomes rather than their political labels.

What I am not interested in is top-down mandates that add cost and bureaucracy without improving care. The goal is more providers, more options, and more affordability — and getting there requires listening to the healthcare professionals and patients in our own communities first.
I do not support the broad sale or transfer of Wyoming's public lands, and I say that as someone who has spent years championing outdoor recreation and public land access in Park County.

Public lands are part of Wyoming's identity and its economy. They support hunting, fishing, grazing, energy development, and tourism. Transferring those lands to private ownership or to underfunded state management would put access at risk.

I have real concerns about federal land management decisions that are made far from Wyoming by people who have never set foot here. The interest of Wyoming's people is not always reflected in federal agency priorities, and the Legislature should be an aggressive advocate for local input into how these lands are managed — grazing allotments, access roads, energy permitting, and wildfire management included.

The goal should be keeping public lands public while insisting that Wyoming's voice is heard loud and clear.
Property tax relief is a concern for many Wyoming families, particularly long-term residents on fixed incomes.

Local services — roads, emergency response, schools — depend on a revenue base that cannot be legislated away from Cheyenne without consequences. A one-size-fits-all property tax solution risks solving one county's problem while creating another's.

My approach would be to pursue targeted relief — protecting primary homeowners and long-term residents from runaway assessments — while preserving local governments' ability to fund the services their communities need. Programs that cap tax burden as a percentage of income, exemptions for primary residences, and phase-in protections for significant assessment increases are all tools worth examining.

I would resist legislative mandates that strip local communities of the revenue flexibility they need to govern themselves responsibly. Local control must mean something, and property tax policy is where that principle gets tested.
The Legislature's most valuable contribution is creating the conditions where private enterprise can take root and grow. That means keeping taxes low and predictable, maintaining a regulatory environment that doesn't punish investment, and building the infrastructure — roads, water systems, workforce pipelines through our community colleges — that allows businesses to succeed once they arrive.

The Legislature doesn't create jobs — but it absolutely creates the environment where job creators choose Wyoming over every other option on their list.
I believe in full transparency. Voters deserve to know who is funding campaigns and in what amounts. Wyoming's current reporting system is functional, but I am open to reforms that improve timeliness and accessibility of disclosure data. What I am not interested in is a system that creates barriers for ordinary citizens and small donors while doing nothing to address the larger influence questions. Dark money is influencing Wyoming elections therefore, some regulation should be considered. Transparency and accessibility should go together.
Big horn county, Lovell. Lived here since 1999. Born in Montana

Homeschool dropout who has a heart to do right. Worked in construction since I was 12, and helped my dad provide for his family. What makes anyone qualified? An interest in what is good, true and virtuous is what I can call upon for my qualifications.

The dissolution of truth, or the deviation from the standard of reality is a great challenge facing our country at large. We have the opportunity to raise the standard of truth to the nation, and innovate independence from the unstable markets that control our economics. Allowing free market enterprise within the state is important.
Stop subsidizing it. Artificial or Government interference has a tendency to unbalance the system

I would support the sale of public land that is of no benefit to the public, to individuals who can use it productively (homesites, businesses, schools, churches etc.) and not corporations or large entities intent on making a profit. I would support the state taking over management of federal lands in Wyoming, and to ensure our public lands are managed we need statute to do so. It seems important to preserve places from certain development for the enjoyment of all, and for posterity.

Spend less. Reevaluate what we actually need. I support homestead exemption from all property taxes. We should not be renting our homes from the government. Counties have means to raise funds without appropriation from the state.
Very little. No subsidies, just uphold justice and make sure everyone keeps their promises. Make commerce uniform and consistent.
I have little to no knowledge regarding this topic, therefore I shall forbear speaking upon it, except in this wise. Don’t cheat, don’t steal, don’t take a bribe. Be moral and virtuous.
I live in Worland. I grew in Worland but moved away to go to law school. I returned to Worland in 2001
70 years old

I grew up in Worland and graduated from WHS. I also graduated from UW with a degree in Political Science and graduated Baylor Law School. I was a business litigator in Houston for many years until we began our journey back west.

I loved growing up in Worland, where I first learned about the importance of being a good neighbor and of getting involved in the life of my community. As an adult, I began to realize that communities like Worland don't happen by accident. They happen because good people devote their time and talent to make the community a better place. Their selfless commitment makes communities great! 

When I moved back to Worland, I wanted to give back to the community. I was honored to serve for many years on the Washakie Museum Board and was President of the board when we build the new museum. I also served on the Washakie Development Ass Board,  Northwest Community College Foundation board and Washakie Leadership – an initiative to develop local leaders.

1. Using our minerals resources to create new opportunities that provide more good paying jobs for Wyoming Citizens
2. Workforce development 
3. Mental Health services particularly for our youth
4. Rising cost of healthcare
5. Strengthening local communities
6. Aging infrastructure 
7. Affordable Housing

I would approach solving these problems in this way - Gather relevant, unbiased information,   listen to the publics' concerns or ideas, consider which level of government should be leading the way, work with other elected officials to craft the best policy for Wyoming.

This is an important challenge. I believe the new Rural Health Transformation Program is giving Wyoming an opportunity to address issues related to access. This  is an important first step in addressing rising costs. The state legislature must take a deep dive on other causes for the steep rise in healthcare costs. When state regulations are identified  as a reason for added cost, the legislature will need to address that. When state reimbursement rates are an issue, we need to consider adjusting those rates. Some of the solutions to rising healthcare costs must come from the federal level as Medicare and Medicaid are federal programs.

I believe we should keep public lands in public hands. Wyoming needs to have more input in the management of federal lands. State lands are to be used by the state to generate revenue. It is the fiduciary duty of those who manage state lands to produce income.

As a legislator representing two hardship counties that have been adversely affected by the property tax cuts, I am very concerned about further cuts. I believe it is important for the citizens of each county to understand how any additional cuts will affect their local government services including special districts. I am disappointed with efforts by some to disconnect local taxes from local services.

The legislature's role begins with appropriating adequate funding for economic development. The legislature also has a role in guiding the focus and priorities of economic development through statutory changes. The executive branch also has a role to play as well.

I would support more transparency in campaign donations. I believe the priority should be protecting the people of Wyoming from out of state dark money. Greater transparency is the key. However, some of that transparency must come from the federal government changing the rules under 503(c) of the Federal Tax Code. Additionally, when groups fail to comply with Wyoming campaign finance laws, there should be stiffer penalties.
Meeteetse, Wyoming, born in Cody, WY

Allen Hogg, 66, is a third-generation rancher who has worked the Upper Greybull River country his entire life. He operates the Hogg Ranch near Meeteetse the land his family has worked since 1918. Allen grew up in the Basin, earned his degree from Northwest College.
He and his wife Kristine have been married 46 years and have raised three sons. 
Allen brings more than two decades of public service to the race. As Vice-Chairman of the Park County Fair Board,  Treasurer of the Park County Predator Board, he helped secure state funding for programs protecting both livestock and Wyoming's wildlife habitat. He is a former President of the Wyoming State Association of County Fairs, and has worked alongside researchers from across the country to ensure that wildlife management decisions are grounded in both scientific research and the on-the-ground knowledge of the people who live and work on the land

One of the biggest challenges facing Wyoming is the declining revenue from the mineral industry, protecting our public lands and protecting Wyoming water. I will work with other legislators to find alternate funding where possible and trim the fat in the budget. I will always support keeping public lands in public hands and protecting water rights.
I support the open market for insurance because there is not enough competition to help lower the premiums.
No!
We need common sense tax relief when there is room in the budget. We must find balance, to keep the vital services available to Wyoming citizens.
Wyoming Legislature should play a limited role in supporting local governments, in economic development.
No
I am a 4th generation rancher out of Otto Wyoming. I was born in Cody Wyoming. I have lived here in Wyoming my whole life.

I am a rancher who has been compelled to stand and fight for our rights. I have been forced to fight the State Engineer office, the DEQ, and in the courts to defend my constitutional rights. I have shown I am willing and able to stand, fight and win against government over-reach.

There are many challenges facing Wyoming today. Cost of living, over spending, and government over-reach.

To fix government over-reach we need to provide a simple mechanism for recourse to hold government officials accountable when they violate the law. We need the people to be able to enforce their rights and receive compensation for injury when government officials violate law without being forced into costly litigation.

Over-spending requires that we relentlessly attack inefficiencies in government. Creating true reform by isolating unnecessary systems and allowing unnecessary individuals to return to the free market for work.

We need to address the fraud that exists in the healthcare system. Look at cash pay costs verses government subsidized insurance cost. The numbers vary wildly! Look at the cost of medication in the USA verses other countries. 

We must get the corporate fingers out of taxpayer pocket and make healthcare more affordable for our families and seniors.

The only transfer of public land I would support is bringing management of federal lands back to the state of Wyoming. The people of Wyoming deserve public lands to stay public for the general public’s benefit. We should not sell our public lands. 

Our lands should be accessible to those who want to enjoy nature; God’s most beautiful creation.

It is difficult to address property tax reform without addressing our overspending. The fear of loss of funding makes people worry about property tax relief.

Let’s look at it from a principled perspective. Private property ownership is fundamental to our democracy. If we must pay the government for land we own under threat of removal, do we really own our private property?

We must address over spending in order to respect people’s private property rights. We should not be forced to endlessly rent our land from the government. That’s not the Wyoming way.

Its role is to establish and maintain a level playing field. Economic development is best managed by the free market. Excessive government intervention is not a far cry from socialism. Taking from some to elevate others. That is not the governments job.

I believe campaign contributions are a form of political speech protected by the First Amendment. My values are simple: full transparency so voters can see exactly who is supporting a candidate, combined with the freedom for Wyoming citizens to voluntarily support the candidates and causes they believe in without excessive government restrictions.

I currently live in Thermopolis, where my family and I have lived since 2020. I was born in Montana and have spent my entire life in the West. I first came to Wyoming while working in the mining industry in 2011, and over the years have lived and worked in several Wyoming communities.
My military service and work as a government contractor took me overseas for extended periods, but Wyoming is the state where I have lived the longest. My wife, Natalya, and I chose to make Thermopolis our home, where our daughters attended school and where I am an attorney, and currently serve on the Hot Springs County School Board member, and as the municipal attorney for both Thermopolis and Shoshoni.

I am a Navy veteran, engineer, attorney, small rancher, small business owner, and member of the Hot Springs County School Board. I served as a Navy SEAL with SEAL Team Three and later returned to the Navy Reserves, retiring in 2025 after additional deployments.
After earning a degree in Geological Engineering from Montana Tech, I worked in mining and engineering throughout the West, including Wyoming. I later earned my law degree and currently practice law, representing local municipalities. My wife, Natalya, and I live in Thermopolis, where our two daughters attended school.

With a wide range of professional experiences in military service, mining, engineering, law, agriculture, and local government, I have firsthand insight into the opportunities and challenges facing Wyoming families, businesses, schools, and communities.

Wyoming's biggest challenges include maintaining essential services, attracting and retaining a skilled workforce, expanding access to housing, diversifying our economy, and addressing long-term fiscal pressures facing local governments. At the same time, Wyoming has tremendous opportunities through our mineral and agricultural resources, outdoor recreation, and strong communities. 
As a legislator, I will support policies that encourage economic growth, strengthen education, and help young people build careers in Wyoming. We must also address housing shortages that make it difficult for workers, retirees, young families, teachers, healthcare professionals, and first responders to live and work in our communities. Finally, we must be responsible stewards of taxpayer dollars while ensuring local governments have the flexibility and resources needed to serve their citizens. My focus will be on practical solutions that support working families and preserve Wyoming's quality of life.

Access to affordable healthcare is a major challenge, especially in Wyoming's rural communities. We need to strengthen and retain our healthcare workforce by supporting training programs, expanding opportunities for healthcare professionals to live and work in Wyoming, and reducing barriers that make it difficult to recruit providers to rural areas (such as housing).

The Legislature can also support responsible use of telehealth, improve access to mental health services, and work to preserve local hospitals, clinics, and emergency medical services that are essential to rural communities. We should look for ways to increase competition and transparency in healthcare pricing so patients can make informed decisions and costs can be better controlled.

There is no single solution, but by focusing on workforce development, rural access, innovation, and affordability, we can help ensure Wyoming families receive the care they need close to home and at a price they can afford.

I do not support the wholesale sale or transfer of public lands. Wyoming's public lands are a vital part of our heritage and provide opportunities for hunting, fishing, recreation, grazing, mineral development, and tourism. These public lands are a part of Wyoming culture and belong to the people. They should should remain accessible for future generations and for all Wyoming citizens- not the just the billionaires who will end up with it if it's sold. 

I support responsible multiple-use management that balances conservation, recreation, agriculture, and mineral production. As a legislator, I would advocate for greater input from local communities and stakeholders, encourage active land management to reduce wildfire risk, create more grazing opportunities, and improve land health. All of this is only possible if the people who live, work, and recreate on these lands are actually part of the decision making process.

Families across Wyoming are feeling the effects of rising costs, and the government should be just as mindful of spending taxpayer dollars. The Legislature has already provided significant property tax relief through the recent 25% residential property tax reduction and other existing exemptions. 
I do not support the proposed ballot initiative for an additional 50% cut to residential property taxes. Local governments, schools, hospitals, fire districts, libraries, and others depend on property tax revenue. Such a large reduction could have serious unintended consequences for local services and infrastructure. Wyoming already has the third lowest tax burden in the Nation. 
As a legislator, I would assess the effects of the current cuts and focus on responsible budgeting, spending, and preserving the property tax relief already enacted, while ensuring communities get the resources they need. I believe local governments should have a strong voice in decisions that directly affect them.

The Legislature has an important role in economic development. The government cannot create prosperity, but it can support the infrastructure, workforce development, and business climate that allows communities to succeed.
I believe Wyoming should continue to support economic development efforts, including organizations such as the Wyoming Business Council, while holding them accountable for results and ensuring taxpayer dollars are used effectively. Programs should be regularly evaluated and improved. Wyoming should be actively recruiting more industry- especially around mining, manufacturing, and water conservation technology companies that will benefit our rural communities. Cheyenne is way too far removed from the people of Wyoming- some of these offices need to be regional. 
My approach is to support practical, accountable economic development that strengthens local communities and respects local decision-making rather than imposing one-size-fits-all solutions from Cheyenne.

I believe campaign contributions are a form of political participation, but transparency is essential. Voters have a right to know who is supporting candidates and how campaign funds are being spent. I am committed to complying with Wyoming's campaign finance laws and to providing timely and accurate disclosure of contributions and expenditures.
Wyoming's campaign finance system is generally built around transparency, and that principle should remain the foundation of any reforms. I am open to improvements that make reporting easier to understand, increase public confidence, and ensure the rules are applied fairly and consistently. Any changes should balance transparency and accountability with the ability of citizens to participate in the political process.

Currently reside: Sheridan, WY since June 2015. Born: Wenatchee, WA
I'm a small business owner, mother, and Sheridan neighbor who believes the best government is the smallest one, and the one closest to home. For over a decade, my husband Nate and I have built our life here including two small businesses and children who love Wyoming as much as we do. 

As owner of Whirly Girl Flowers, I've learned to listen first, work with what's available, and tend to the details that matter. My professional experience includes budgeting, bookkeeping, and hands-on operations for human services non-profits, practical skills suited to representing District 29.

I hold an AA from Pikes Peak Community College and a BS from Colorado State University, and I'm active locally with WY Vote Sheridan County, and the Sheridan County Chamber of Commerce.

I'm an independent Republican, registered since 1998, accountable only to my neighbors in Sheridan. I bring no fixed agenda, just a readiness to listen and serve.
Wyoming's biggest challenges are also its greatest opportunities: a revenue base tied to extractive industries, rising healthcare costs, and the pressure to grow without losing what makes Wyoming worth living in. The opportunity is that we get to thoughtfully choose what comes next, 

As a legislator, I'd focus on responsible economic diversification that builds on what's already rooted here. I'd work to remove regulatory burdens that stifle small business growth, support working families, and ensure that any new development fits who we are and serves Wyoming people, not outside interests.

The best decisions come from listening first. I don't arrive with a fixed agenda. I come prepared to problem-solve with the resources available, set honest priorities, and make hard calls without losing sight of what matters most to the families I'd represent. Wyoming's strength has always come from people who show up and do the job. I want to bring that same ethic to the legislature.
Every Wyoming family deserves access to honest, affordable care; whether you're self-employed, ranching an hour from the nearest clinic, or quietly struggling in ways that don't always show. As a small business owner without employer-sponsored insurance, this isn't abstract to me. I've navigated those costs firsthand.

The Legislature can help by removing barriers to recruiting and retaining qualified medical professionals, supporting rural access through telehealth and community health infrastructure, and examining licensing and regulatory barriers that limit provider options. 
Real solutions will be practical, locally informed, and sustainable, not ones that look good on paper but break under Wyoming conditions. I'll listen to providers, patients, and rural communities to find approaches that meet people where they are.
Wyoming's public lands are part of what makes this place worth protecting. They support ranching, hunting, recreation, and the rural way of life that defines us. I do not support broad efforts to sell or transfer federal public lands out of public ownership. Once that land is gone, it's gone, and the people who lose the most are everyday Wyomingites, not outside buyers.

That said, land management decisions should be made locally with Wyoming's interests at the table, not dictated by federal agencies disconnected from local realities. I support giving communities and counties a meaningful voice in how nearby public lands are managed, and I'd work to strengthen that relationship rather than sever it.

Good stewardship means watching over the natural beauty and resources that make living in Wyoming so rich, and passing them on intact to the next generation. That's a responsibility I take seriously.
Property tax policy has to reflect that different communities have different resources available and needs to fill. I believe in keeping the government as close to home as possible which means local communities should have real input into how relief is structured and how revenue needs are met.

Property taxes have risen faster than many families' ability to absorb them and we need to continue working together to provide relief where possible while also acknowledging the real cost of simply cutting taxes. Relief has to be paired with honest conversations about what it funds: schools, roads, emergency services, and how we backfill those needs responsibly.
Wyoming's economy is strongest when the government gets out of the way and lets hardworking people build. My role as a legislator isn't to pick winners or engineer outcomes, it's to remove unnecessary barriers, protect property rights, and create conditions where entrepreneurs and small businesses can thrive.

That said, the Legislature does have a role: investing in infrastructure, supporting workforce development, and ensuring our mineral, agricultural, and tourism industries have a stable foundation to operate from. I'd also look at thoughtfully diversifying our revenue base so Wyoming isn't perpetually at the mercy of commodity cycles.

Economic development that fits Wyoming should grow from what's already rooted here, honor our values of independence and hard work, and serve the people already living and working in our communities, not outside interests looking to extract value and move on.
I'm accountable to my neighbors in Sheridan, no one else. That principle shapes how I approach campaign donations. I've focused on building a grassroots campaign funded by people in this community who know me, trust me, and want a representative who answers to them.

There’s opportunity for reform and transparency as more outside interests pour money into Wyoming through campaign expenditure accounts. The people of Wyoming deserve to know who’s trying to influence their vote
I was born and raised in Sheridan and have spent nearly 36 of my 43 years here.  I moved away a couple times, but Sheridan always calls me home.

I have spent most of my life serving others and helping strengthen the communities where I live. For 15 years, I served as a pastor in the Wesleyan Church, walking alongside families through life's challenges and learning the value of servant leadership. 

As Director of Marketing for the Sheridan County Chamber of Commerce, I gained a firsthand understanding of the importance of a strong business community and the role collaboration plays in solving local challenges. Later, as Executive Director of Sheridan KidsLife, I worked closely with children, giving me an appreciation for the challenges they face.

Today, I serve as Director of Development at The Hub on Smith, where I help secure funding for essential services that support older adults as they age in place.

These experiences have given me a broad perspective on the needs of Wyoming families and businesses, and reinforced my belief that strong communities are built through service, responsibility, and neighbors working together.

One of the greatest challenges facing Wyoming today is ensuring that our economy provides enough opportunity for the people to stay, work, and raise a family here. Too many people leave Wyoming in search of better job opportunities, while many employers struggle to find the workforce they need. At the same time, residents are facing a housing market that has become increasingly difficult to afford. As a family of six, we are faced with these realities every day.

I believe these challenges present some of our greatest opportunities. I would make economic growth and job creation a top priority by attracting new businesses and creating an environment where employers can invest and grow. 

I would also be committed to addressing the rising cost of housing. We should be able to afford to live in the communities we serve. Finding practical solutions that increase housing availability and affordability will be critical to Wyoming's future.

My family has experienced the rising cost of healthcare firsthand. With four kids, our second largest expense each year, after housing, is healthcare. Wyoming consistently ranks among the most expensive states in the nation for individual health insurance, and rural access challenges contribute to higher overall healthcare costs.

The Legislature should focus on solutions that work for our rural state. We need to strengthen our healthcare workforce by expanding programs that attract and retain doctors, nurses, and other providers in rural communities. We need to continue to supporting telehealth services and expanding broadband access to help connect patients with specialists without requiring hours of travel. In addition, supporting rural hospitals and clinics is critical to ensuring that Wyoming residents can receive care close to home.

No single policy will solve this challenge, but every piece can help make healthcare more affordable and accessible for Wyoming families.

Wyoming's public lands are one of our state's greatest assets. I’ve spent a lifetime enjoying them and would love to see my children and future generations do the same. They support agriculture, energy production, outdoor recreation, wildlife habitat, tourism, and our way of life. I do not support efforts to sell off public lands because once those lands are gone, they are gone for future generations.

As a legislator, I would support policies that protect public access, respect private property rights, and ensure that land management decisions are informed by local stakeholders. I also support efforts to improve coordination between federal agencies and state and local governments, so Wyoming's concerns are heard and addressed.

The goal should be responsible stewardship that preserves our public lands, supports Wyoming's economy, and ensures these resources remain available for future generations to enjoy and benefit from. Our kids will thank us.

I understand why many homeowners are asking for relief. I own my home and the cost of living affects us daily. At the same time, property taxes help fund essential local services such as schools, roads, law enforcement, and fire protection. These services affect us daily as well. 

I believe we should pursue responsible property tax relief that helps homeowners while preserving the ability of local communities to provide the services their residents rely on. Any proposal should be carefully evaluated for its impact on counties, municipalities, and taxpayers before it is implemented.

Rather than shifting costs from one place to another or creating unintended consequences for local governments, we should look for sustainable solutions that provide relief while maintaining local control. Local communities should have a voice in decisions that directly affect their revenue and their ability to meet local needs.

The Legislature should focus on the fundamentals that make Wyoming an attractive place to invest and create jobs. That includes maintaining a business-friendly regulatory environment, investing in critical infrastructure, supporting workforce development, and ensuring that housing is available and affordable for working families. 

The Legislature should work with local communities, businesses, educational institutions, and economic development organizations to remove barriers to growth and encourage private-sector investment. Strong communities, a skilled workforce, affordable housing, and a healthy business climate are all essential to Wyoming's long-term prosperity.

My values when it comes to campaign donations is simple: transparency and honesty. Voters should be able to easily see where campaign money comes from and how it is spent. Wyoming's campaign finance system has traditionally emphasized transparency. However, I am open to reviewing the system to ensure disclosure requirements remain clear, timely, and accessible to the public. Any reforms should focus on increasing transparency and public trust.
Gillette, 20 years, 20 years.
60 years old

I have been representing the hard working people of Gillette for three terms.  I’m a Christian and veteran naval officer. I married my college sweetheart Sage. We raised two children in Gillette and our daughter lives here with her family.  My family and friends are the primary reason I wish to serve the people of Campbell County.

Today the families in House District 31 are facing inflation that is cutting into their budget everyday.  I have advanced real and lasting tax relief for the people of Wyoming and I will continue to do that as well as striving to ensure that state and local governments do not add to the cost of achieving the American dream.

The One Big Beautiful Bill will provide nearly $1 billion to Wyoming for Rural Health Transformation.  I authored the bill determining how this money will be spent.  Wyoming must take advantage of this unprecidented opportunity and expend these funds wisely to increase access and the sutainability of our healthcare industry.

I have no interest in selling public lands.  If Wyoming residents want to have the public lands managed for their best interests, then the government closest to the people of Wyoming should be the government to manage those lands.  The recent Rock Springs Resource Management Plan(RMP) proved that the people in New York and California have no business dictating how our lands are utilized.  That federal control will lead to zero human access if we continue to allow the federal government to prioritize so called environmentalism above hunting, fishing, camping, and mineral utilization.  I believe the state should take all lands that are public and that our constitution should require that public access be maintained at or above current levels.

I would ensure, as I already have through my work as chairman of the House Appropriations Committee, that local governments have access to steady streams of revenue other than residential property taxes.  I led the way in redistributing sales tax, already being collected for the state, to local governments.  This is exactly what good conservative governance looks like.

The state should first and foremost create a business freindly environment by having the lowest tax  burden and the least restrictive regulatory burden.  The Wyoming Business Counsel should be the chief advertising and promoting arm of the state when it comes to developing new business relationships.  The infrastructure needed to support new economic devleopment should be streamlined so that local governents can effect change as quickly as they desire to see new businesses come to their communities.

I believe that the current system honors both transparency for those who wish to see who is funding whom and the first amendment rights of individuals who wish to participate in this representative government.
