Live in Gillette, WY, current residence since 2010.  I moved to Gillette and lived there since June of 1993.  I was born in Bismarck, ND

Ken is the founder and president of Gillette Physical Therapy, P.C., serving the Gillette community since 1993. A Doctor of PT and advocate for healthcare reform, he has focused on improving patient care, advancing standards, and expanding access.
Since 2022, Ken has represented HD 32 in the WY Legislature, serving on the Education Committee and the Labor, Health, and Social Services Committee. He completed the Milbank Program for Emerging Leaders in Healthcare in 2025 and has participated in national policy efforts.
He has led key healthcare legislation, including reforms to credentialing, prior authorization, reimbursement, and accessibility. His work also includes criminal justice, energy, and mental health policy.
Ken previously served on the Wyoming State Board of Education, Campbell County School Board (2022 WY School Board Member of the Year), and Campbell County Healthcare Board. He brings clinical, leadership, and policy experience to public service.

Wyoming’s biggest challenges are diversifying our economy, sustaining communities facing rising costs, and maintaining reliable public services in a rural state. At the same time, we have tremendous opportunities in energy innovation, healthcare delivery, and workforce development. As a legislator, I focus on practical solutions, such as supporting responsible energy production while encouraging emerging sectors, strengthening education and job training pipelines, and improving healthcare access. We must make smart investments that keep communities viable, attract and retain workers, and ensure Wyoming remains a great place to live, work, and raise a family.

Healthcare affordability and access require reducing administrative burdens, increasing provider availability, and improving transparency. I’ve worked on reforms to prior authorization, credentialing, and reimbursement to lower costs and keep providers practicing in WY. We should continue expanding telehealth, support rural clinics, and streamline regulations that drive up costs without improving care. Encouraging competition and innovative care models will help patients get timely, affordable services. My focus is practical reform that keeps care local, reduces delays, and ensures Wyoming citizens receive high-quality care without unnecessary barriers. I am looking at bringing a program from Montana that saved @ 12% on healthcare premiums.

Public lands are central to Wyoming’s economy, culture, and way of life. I do not support broad efforts to sell or transfer public lands out of public hands. Instead, we should advocate for balanced, responsible management that protects access for recreation, supports grazing and energy development, and preserves natural resources. The state should work collaboratively with federal partners, local stakeholders, and industry to ensure decisions reflect Wyoming values. Maintaining public access, economic use, and long-term stewardship must remain the priority for the benefit of current and future generations.

Property tax relief is important, but it must be balanced carefully with the need to fund essential local services like schools, emergency response, and infrastructure. A one-size-fits-all solution doesn’t work in Wyoming. I support targeted relief that prioritizes homeowners most impacted, while maintaining flexibility for local communities to meet their needs. We should also examine alternative revenue options and efficiencies to reduce reliance on property taxes over time. Thoughtful, balanced policy ensures tax relief without undermining the services Wyoming communities depend on. I have worked on and co-sponsored long-term homeowners' relief, the 4% cap, and have offered other solutions that were community/county specific.

The Legislature plays an important role in creating the conditions for economic growth, not picking winners and losers. Our job is to ensure a stable regulatory environment, invest in workforce development, and support infrastructure that enables businesses to succeed. By fostering innovation, supporting small businesses, and leveraging Wyoming’s strengths, such as energy, natural resources, and rural entrepreneurship, we can build a more resilient economy. Strategic, responsible policy helps attract investment while preserving the values and quality of life that make Wyoming unique. We need to make Wyoming the most business-friendly state, I have worked each year to lower our Workman's Compensation insurance rates and decrease regulations.

Transparency and integrity must guide campaign finance. I support full disclosure so voters understand who supports candidates and why. Wyoming’s system works well in many respects, but there is room to improve clarity, accessibility of reporting, and public understanding. Any reforms should strengthen transparency while encouraging, not discouraging, civic participation.
I am committed to ethical fundraising and to making decisions based on sound policy and the needs of my constituents—not contributions. I was disappointed by the recent “Checkgate” controversy, which was inappropriate and diverted valuable legislative time from addressing the real issues facing Wyoming.

I have three children and two grandsons.

Medicaid expansion, access to specialized treatments locally, expand mental health services.
I do not support.  Some efforts have to be made at the federal level such as funding much needed positions in natural resource agencies.
We have to be very aware of the funding issues with smaller municipalities and the drastic effects it will have on those communities.  One size don't fit all.

It should have some role but not the major role.  Educating communities on the pathway to economic possibilities for individuals is important and assisting in larger projects through transparency and partnerships.
The campaign system in the nation needs reformed.
I live in Casper, Wyoming. I've lived here my entire life. I've lived in Wyoming for 43 years. I was born and raised right here in Natrona County.

I'm a lifelong Natrona County resident, born and raised in Casper, and a proud University of Wyoming graduate. I've spent my career in education here at home, and my commitment to this community extends well beyond the workday. I've given my time to organizations that look out for our most vulnerable neighbors — serving on the Juvenile Justice Board, the Natrona County Child Protection Team, and the Self Help Center — and I've spent nearly a decade as secretary of the Wyoming Soccer Association. That service is rooted in a simple belief: the people closest to a problem are best equipped to solve it. As your representative, my priorities are straightforward: strong local control so decisions about our schools and our community stay in Natrona County hands, and public safety for the families who call this place home. I'm running because I believe Wyoming's choices should be made by Wyoming voices, not outside influence.

Wyoming's biggest challenge is keeping decisions in the hands of the people who live with the consequences. Too often, authority drifts away from local communities — like the recent school funding changes that cut roughly 8% from student activities and locked dollars into rigid categories, stripping local boards of the flexibility to spend where their kids actually need it. Families also feel real pressure from rising property taxes and an economy too dependent on a single revenue stream. But our opportunities are just as real: hardworking people, abundant resources, and communities where neighbors look out for one another. As a legislator, I'd restore genuine local control, keep families and first responders safe by backing the people who protect us, and make sure Wyoming's future is built by Wyoming voices — not outside money. The answer to nearly every challenge we face starts with trusting the people closest to the problem.

Healthcare costs and access top voters' lists for good reason — too many Wyomingites drive hours for care, and rural communities are watching providers grow scarce. The Legislature can't fix everything, but it has real tools. First, we should tackle our provider shortage head-on: expand loan-repayment and training programs that bring doctors, nurses, and midwives to underserved areas and keep Wyoming-trained talent here at home. Second, we should strengthen the critical-access hospitals and clinics that small towns depend on, and remove the red tape that keeps qualified providers from practicing to the full extent of their training. Third, we should expand Telehealth and require real price transparency so families can see costs before they're stuck with a bill. We need to keep care close to home and decisions close to the people, rather than waiting for solutions from far away.

No, I don't support selling off Wyoming's public lands. These lands belong to all of us: the hunters, anglers, ranchers, and families who've used them for generations. Once they're sold, they're gone — too often to the highest bidder from out of state, and the public loses access for good. That's not the Wyoming way. I do believe Wyoming deserves a far stronger voice in how the land within our borders is managed. Federal one-size-fits-all decisions rarely fit our state, and the people closest to the land — those who graze it, work it, hunt it, and recreate on it — understand it best. So, my approach is straightforward: keep public lands public and accessible, protect Wyoming's multiple-use traditions, and push for genuine local and state input in management decisions rather than handing our heritage over to distant interests. Wyoming's land should be managed by Wyoming voices, for Wyoming people.

Wyoming homeowners deserve real relief — valuations climbed fast, and folks on fixed incomes got squeezed hard. I take that seriously. But here's what often gets lost: property tax is the most local source of revenue we have. It funds our schools, fire, EMS, and roads — and as your question notes, no two counties raise revenue the same way. When the Legislature hands down blanket cuts, it can quietly strip communities of the power to fund their own priorities — which is its own kind of lost local control. So, I favor targeted relief for the homeowners who need it most, especially seniors, over blunt, across-the-board cuts that leave counties scrambling or force us to backfill with volatile state revenue, such as an increase in the sales tax. And I'd give counties more flexibility to meet their own needs rather than dictating one formula for all 23. Relief and strong local services aren't opposites — but getting the balance right means trusting communities, not overriding them.

The Legislature's role isn't to pick winners and losers — it's to build the foundation that lets Wyoming businesses and communities thrive: a skilled workforce, reliable infrastructure, and strong institutions. I support the Wyoming Business Council as a real partner to our communities, helping local leaders grow the industries that fit their region — because, as your earlier question noted, what works in one county won't work in another. And as a proud University of Wyoming graduate, I see UW as one of our greatest economic engines: training our workforce, driving research, and keeping young talent here at home. Above all, Wyoming has to diversify beyond the boom-and-bust of a single revenue stream. The Legislature should empower local communities to build on their own strengths and back the Wyoming institutions already doing this work — not chase one-size-fits-all schemes from outside. Steady investment in our own people is how we secure Wyoming's future.

My values here are simple: a campaign should be funded by the community it seeks to serve. I'm proud that in my last run, the vast majority of my individual donations came from right here in Natrona County, and my organizational support comes from Wyoming-based groups. When a candidate's money comes from home, they answer to the people back home — not to distant interests writing checks from out of state. That's exactly why I believe Wyoming's disclosure system needs reform. Voters deserve to easily see who's funding the campaigns asking for their vote — including PAC money and dollars flowing in from outside Wyoming. Our reporting is too hard to search and too easy to obscure. I'd support clearer, more accessible, more timely disclosure so sunlight can do its job. Transparency isn't partisan — it's how voters hold all of us accountable. The people of Wyoming deserve to know whose interests a candidate truly represents.

9 sponsored bills and great many co-sponsored bills are now law!

Challenges involve - workforce housing, good paying jobs and a skilled workforce.  Another huge issue is aging.  Also the lack of water and the condition of the highways .  

Uranium, rare earth minerals, tourism, outdoor recreation, renewed demand for coal and continued demand for oil, natural gas, helium and even gold are opportunities.  Our investment portfolio and pension funds are the envy of most states.  

We don't control all our challenges.  Our workforce can be enhanced by expanded public private partnerships.  The legislature must support the community colleges.  The Hathaway Scholarships need to be expanded. The Wyoming Community Development Authority and community banks will play a role in easing the housing crunch.   

Regardless of these, the greatest challenge we face is the growing national debt.

This is a complex problem that defies a simple answer.  Transfering the cost to government, is a possibility but it is expensive.  Relying on funding from a national government that will soon be $40 Trillion in debt is not sound sustainable.  We have taken preliminary steps to create a Physician Assistant degree program at the University.   The legislature has been adjusting the scope of practice for professionals like optometrists, pharmacists and athletic trainers.  There is more work to be done.  Given the situation, it is my opinion that every healthcare professional ought to be able to perform all of the practices that they are trained and capable of doing.   We lose practitioners when we limit their scope of practice despite their skills. Travelling health care might make sense.  Alaska has a model that might work here.  They rely on paraprofessional health aides, telehealth and air ambulances from hub hospitals.  Midwives should also be a bigger part of the health care system.

Selling off the public lands in a wholesale fashion is a horrible idea.  There may be, however, a few parcels of public land that, after public discussion and study, might make sense to sell or transfer.  We already do something similar from time to time.  For example, sometimes we can trade parcels of state land or school trust land so that the affected land owners, the public and the state all benefit.  If the trade gives public access to an otherwise inaccessible tract of land, that might be a good trade.  If the trade puts several sections of public land side by side and accessible instead of individual parcels scattered and largely inaccessible to sportsmen and recreationists, that could be a smart trade.  If we can identify a few parcels of public land to sell and the interested parties agree, having a blanket prohibition on sales or transfers would not make sense.   Once again, the answer is somewhere in between the extremes.

We had a proposal in 2025 that had great merit which we voted for and then reversed course.  The idea was to base the correction in property taxes to the percentage of tax increases that had actually occurred in each county.  Hypothetically, let's say a county had experienced only a 10% increase in property values during the years when property values and consequently property tax payments had exploded, why provide a 25% reduction?  We also amended the Constitution to allow residential property to be a separate class.  There is more we can do with that

We have over the years created legislative mandates for the Wyoming Business Council.  Review all of that and retain what makes sense today and repeal the rest.  The free market approach that many long for is simply no longer a reality. If Wyoming becomes the only state without such an operation, we will not compete with other states that are more than happy to provide incentives.  

One thing we should not do is set a precedent that whenever major industrial projects want to operate in our state the legislature must give a thumbs up.  That would make attracting businesses to our state, very difficult.  

The legislature should set up processes within the executive branch and local governments and then get out of the way.  Only if such a process is found to need correction, should the legislature get involved and only to the extent to correct the process, not rule on individual projects.

The system is fine.  The individuals giving and receiving donations need to look in the mirror and do the right thing.  Voters need to ask candidates during a campaign how much PAC money they are accepting and who are the folks making the big donations?  The idea that folks are spending $40,000 and up for a legislative race is NUTS!  

Voters can reward candidates who spend great sums or they can reject them.  If voters don't want dozens of oversized full color glossy postcards in their mailbox throughout a campaign season, let the candidates know!   If high dollar attack ads work to win campaigns, we will see more high dollar attack ads.  If voters see such ads for the manipulation attempts that they are, we can rein them in.  The best candidate may not be the one who can afford a great deal of advertising.  

I received a campaign check before the 2026 session and before I had declared my candidacy.  I sent the check back.  That captures my values.

Born in Kemmerer, WY. Raised in Big Piney, WY Currently live in Casper, WY since 2000.
I am a lifelong Wyomingite, educator, coach, entrepreneur, husband, and father. For more than two decades, I have taught and coached in Casper while also operating small businesses. Those experiences have given me firsthand insight into the challenges facing Wyoming families, students, workers, and employers.

My wife, Amy, and I raised our family in Casper because we believe it is one of the best places in the country to live. I am running because I want future generations to have the same opportunities Wyoming provided me.

I believe in local control, strong schools, responsible government, protecting our public lands, supporting a healthy business climate, and creating opportunities that allow young people and families to build their future right here in Wyoming. If elected, I will bring a practical, common-sense approach focused on listening, solving problems, and delivering more results and less drama.

Wyoming's biggest challenge is creating enough opportunity that our young people can build their future here instead of feeling they have to leave the state. Affordable housing, workforce development, access to healthcare, and a strong economy are all connected to that challenge. At the same time, Wyoming's greatest opportunity is our people, our natural resources, and our quality of life.

As a legislator, I will work to strengthen education, support responsible economic growth, reduce unnecessary barriers for businesses, and expand opportunities for career and technical training. I also believe we must protect our public lands and Wyoming way of life while continuing to support the industries that power our economy. My focus will be on practical solutions that help families thrive, keep communities strong, and ensure future generations can live, work, and raise a family in Wyoming.
Healthcare affordability and access are major challenges, especially in rural Wyoming. We need more healthcare providers, stronger support for rural hospitals and clinics, and expanded access to telehealth so people can receive care closer to home. I also support efforts to increase competition, improve price transparency, and reduce unnecessary regulations that drive up costs for patients and providers.

As our population ages, we should invest in services that help seniors remain independent, including home healthcare, preventative care, and transportation assistance. The goal should be a healthcare system that is accessible, affordable, and focused on the needs of Wyoming patients and families rather than bureaucracy.
I generally oppose efforts to sell or transfer public land because access to them is an important part of our economy, outdoor heritage, and quality of life. As a lifelong Wyomingite, I understand the importance of public access for hunting, fishing, recreation, and tourism.

At the same time, state trust lands have a constitutional responsibility to generate revenue for education. I believe managers, especially the State Lands and Investments Board, should consider the full value of these lands and not only the dollars they generate. Their benefits to wildlife, recreation, local communities, and future generations must be taken into account. The best approach is balanced management that protects public access, supports responsible multiple use, and ensures Wyoming citizens receive the greatest long-term benefit from both state and federal lands.
Wyoming homeowners deserve meaningful property tax relief, especially seniors and families facing rising costs. At the same time, counties, municipalities, and special districts rely on property tax revenue to provide essential services such as schools, roads, public safety, and infrastructure. Because Wyoming's counties have different economies, tax bases, and service needs, a one-size-fits-all approach is unlikely to work.

As a legislator, I would support responsible tax relief while carefully evaluating the impact on local governments and exploring ways to maintain essential services. I believe local communities should have a strong voice in these decisions, and any long-term solution must balance taxpayer relief with the need to keep our communities strong and well-served.
The Legislature has an important role in creating an environment where businesses can grow, workers can succeed, and young people can build their futures in Wyoming. That means investing in education and workforce development, supporting infrastructure, reducing unnecessary barriers to business growth, and ensuring Wyoming remains a competitive place to live and work.

I do not believe government should pick winners and losers, but it should be a partner in creating opportunities. If we want our young people to stay in Wyoming, raise families here, and strengthen our communities, we must continue creating good-paying jobs and pathways to success while preserving the Wyoming values that make our state such a special place to call home.
I believe campaign donations should be transparent and reported in a way that allows the public to see who is supporting candidates and how campaign funds are being spent. Wyoming's current system includes contribution limits and disclosure requirements that promote accountability and public trust. While I am always open to improvements that increase transparency and make information more accessible to voters, I believe any reforms should be practical, easy to comply with, and focused on informing the public rather than creating unnecessary burdens for candidates and citizens who wish to participate in the political process.

Encourage Congress to repeal the "Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act" and let Wyomingites buy the insurance they want and need. Reduce or eliminate residential property taxes; the most unfair tax as a home general does not produce income; just expenses.  Backfill the reduction from state funds. Government's role should be limited to infrastructure and not taxpayer paid subsidies to companies.  Government should not be in the business of private business and should not compete with our local businesses.

Yes.  There should be an earlier filing before citizens vote so that they are better informed.  I have pledged to only receive donations from Wyomingites and political action committees based in Wyoming.  I have limited donations to $250 per person/$500 per married couple and $250 for Wyoming political action committees.  This is a self-imposed pledge.  But I would not support a government-imposed limit as the U.S. 1st amendment gives anyone the right to contribute what they want provided the candidate wants to accept it.  The answer to money in politics is transparency.
I was born and raised in Casper. I have lived here my entire life, and now I have the great privilege of raising my wonderful children here.
37. My birthday is a month before the election.

I am a Wyoming native. I graduated from Natrona County High School, and I hold a bachelor's degree from the University of Wyoming in English literature. I also have a master's in education from St. Catherine's University in St. Paul, MN. My husband is a USMC veteran and is one of the few people who served in both Iraq and Afghanistan. Now, we are raising our children here in Casper.  
I work for the WyoUnited Education Project, and I volunteer with the Casper Unity and Solidarity Project, doing mutual aid work, and I volunteer with Meals on Wheels. I am also a former Woods Learning Center PTC president. My experiences volunteering have played a large part in my decision to run for office. I also serve as board secretary for the WyoUnited (c)(4) and on the Natrona County Historic Preservation Commission.

I think the biggest challenge facing Wyoming today is the brain drain. With young people leaving in droves, Wyoming will struggle as more people retire. We have to find and build opportunities for young people to stay. 

I was especially offended when the legislature recommended cutting significant funding from UW. This is a great asset to Wyoming as a whole, and I think it is important that we harness that. 

The biggest opportunity, in my opinion, is that because we are such a small state, we have the opportunity to pioneer our way forward in policy that could strengthen our communities and make equality a reality. We have the opportunity to build something better and set the example for other states.

We need to strengthen Wyoming's healthcare workforce by investing in programs that recruit and retain doctors, nurses, mental health professionals, and other providers, especially in rural communities. No one should have to wait months for an appointment or drive hundreds of miles to see a specialist. 

We should also explore and support policies that lower prescription drug costs, expand access to preventive care, and increase access to mental health services. Investing in prevention and early treatment saves money and improves outcomes.

Finally, we need practical solutions, not political talking points. Legislators need to listen to patients, providers, hospitals, and local communities to identify what is working and what is not. The experience of experts should guide healthcare decisions and what helps Wyoming families stay healthy and financially secure.

No, I do not support efforts to sell or transfer public land in Wyoming. People come from all over the world to visit Wyoming. Our wide-open spaces and public lands are a big part of that. Tourism is the second-largest industry in Wyoming. However, our public lands are worth more than that. As Wyomingites, we know that the value that our public lands add to our lives cannot be tallied in dollars. 

We have a responsibility to leave Wyoming's land, water, and wildlife better than we found them. I support policies that protect watersheds, reduce wildfire risk, restore wildlife habitat, strengthen reclamation requirements for development projects, and keep public lands in public hands.

I plan to listen to experts and to the people in the communities. Listening and working together is how we do what is in the best interest of Wyoming.

Wyoming families deserve tax policies that are fair and predictable, but we also deserve well-funded schools, safe roads, effective emergency services, and strong local communities. My approach would be to balance tax relief with fiscal responsibility and local control, ensuring that we don't solve one problem by creating another.

Wyoming needs to invest in the foundations of a strong economy: quality public schools, workforce training, reliable infrastructure, affordable healthcare, and vibrant communities where people want to live and raise families. We also need to support entrepreneurs and small businesses, which are the backbone of many Wyoming communities.

At the same time, we must continue working to diversify our economy. Wyoming has long benefited from the energy industry, and that industry will remain important, but we cannot rely on any single sector to carry our state forward. 

The Wyoming legislature should work to create good-paying jobs, keep young people in Wyoming, and ensure our communities can thrive for generations to come.

I believe voters deserve to know who is funding political campaigns and how that money is being spent. Transparency builds trust, and trust is essential to a healthy democracy.

Discussion of reform should focus on increasing transparency and public confidence while ensuring ordinary Wyomingites can participate in the political process. The goal should be a system that is open, accountable, and trusted by the public.

At the end of the day, I believe elected officials should earn votes through their ideas, their integrity, and their service to their communities—not through the size of their campaign accounts.
I was born in Riverton, WY and grew up on the ranch in Northwestern Natrona County.  I still reside there today.

I am a third generation rancher in Natrona County and I started an oilfield construction business in 2001.  I have served on numerous boards and commissions over the years.  I served on the Natrona Co Fire Protection District for 14 years as well as a Natrona Co Commissioner for 16 years.  Over the years I have gained valuable knowledge about business and the private sector as well as the knowledge of how government works and the Legislative process.  Having lobbied for the WY Stock Growers Assn, the WY County Commissioners Assn and the WY Business Alliance, this has given be the background and knowledge of how to get things done.

The biggest challenges include jobs, the economy, affordable housing, healthcare and funding for local governments. For the opportunities, there are a number of businesses that are interested in moving to our state. We need to continue to improve on our legacy industries so they continue to thrive.  We need to welcome new industries and business technologies into the state.  To attract them, we need to reduce some of the regulations so they will be able to produce the jobs that will keep our kids here and let our economy grow.  As far as housing, the Legislature and counties need to make it easier for housing development across the state.

Healthcare is a very complex issue.  We need to try and attract more insurance companies to come to the state.  Some of the problems in healthcare are the lack of nurses and doctors practicing in the state.  By funding those nursing programs in our local schools it will help keep kids in the state and increase the number of health care providers.

We shouldn’t sell the public lands however implementing trades in certain areas makes sense.  Over the year, Wyoming has been a good steward of the state lands.  Wyoming should have more of a say in management of federal lands.  We can manage them better locally.

Property tax relief comes at a cost to education, local governments and special districts.  At a state level, we need to figure out a way to back fill the cities and counties so they may continue to provide services to the public.  Reducing taxes is great in theory but it comes at a cost to all the services we are accustomed to and enjoy;  Law enforcement, fire protection, roads, parks.

The Legislature should continue to fund economic development programs like those of the WY Business Council and work together with the local county development programs throughout the state. Ie Cheyenne LEADS, Advance Casper and others.

I have no problem in receiving money from people, groups and Pacs that I am aligned with.  I believe, as candidates, we should list our donations and expenditures.  I don’t believe there is any need for reform.
I live in Casper, WY.   I graduated from NCHS in '02.

I am a community memeber that volunteers, a mother of 5, and current state legislator. I am a results-oriented leader who gets answers and is not afraid to ask the hard questions. I am a committed listener and seeker of practical solutions. I remain dedicated to listening closely to constituents and delivering the results Wyoming families deserve.

Residents are facing numerous challenges, including rising costs for food, animal feed, fertilizers, gas, water, property taxes, and utility bills. Relief can come through reforming state spending to collect less in taxes, thereby easing the financial burden on Wyoming families. Water issues present another major challenge this season, with low levels affecting irrigation, tourism, livestock, and residential use. In our arid state, water is life, vital for our businesses and communities. Pray for moisture. Access to quality local medical care is another pressing concern. We lack sufficient providers and continue to lose specialized practitioners. The following section addresses this.

Healthcare affordability is not just a budget issue it affects Wyoming families, businesses, and our ability to attract and retain residents. By focusing on core state responsibilities like  price transparency, rural infrastructure, workforce support, and prevention, the Legislature can deliver meaningful relief without undermining personal responsibility or market dynamics.

Public lands in WY are vital to our WY way of life and tourism industry. I will work to ensure that public lands in WY are lawfully managed by the state in a way that supports healthy wildlife, and outdoor recreation. I am learning more about this topic and welcome resources to read from.

This requires reform not just relief of property taxes. I am in suport of the state paying for education which would reduce all property tax bills, by 43 mills while protecting local budgets, and not shifting the tax burden on the tax payor in another format. The average property tax mill in WY is appox. 72 mills. Thus, this reform leaves money on your kitchen table while local budgets remain unchanged.

The Wyoming state government needs to focus on Wyoming’s infrastructure to ensure economic development can occur. In Wyoming, our roads carry our economy, from agriculture, to oil and gas, to maintenancing those fields/wells/pipelines, minerals, and tourism. Secure, structurally sound water and sewer lines are essential to keeping utility rates affordable for Wyoming residents. When state government focuses on its core functions, we strengthen our ability to attract businesses from out of state. Another business inviting piece of WY that allows for economic growth is our low tax structure that is business firendly.
I believe in campaign transparency. I believe the campaign donations need to be reported as recieved, and quarterly expenses reported.

Where do you live currently? - Casper Wyoming
How long have you lived there? - since 1978
How long have you lived in Wyoming? since 1963
Where were you born? - Morgan Town, West Virginia

I am Catherine D. McQueen, a lifelong Democrat, longtime Casper resident, and former precinct committeewoman. After the loss of my husband, I raised three children in Wyoming while working hard to provide for my family and build a better future. Today, my children have successful careers, and I am proud that they learned the values of hard work, responsibility, and service to others.

I am running because I believe government should work for everyday people. Wyoming needs leaders who listen, who understand the challenges families face, and who are willing to work across differences to find practical solutions. My life experience, community involvement, and commitment to public service have prepared me to be a strong advocate for the people of House District 38.

Wyoming faces several significant challenges, including affordable housing, access to healthcare, workforce development, and ensuring our economy remains strong for future generations. Many families are feeling the impact of rising costs while communities work to attract and retain workers and young families.

As a legislator, I would focus on practical policies that help create jobs, expand housing opportunities, support small businesses, and invest in education and workforce training. I believe we must prepare Wyoming’s workforce for the jobs of tomorrow while supporting the industries that have long been the foundation of our economy. I will listen to constituents, work collaboratively with others, and support solutions that strengthen Wyoming families and communities for the long term.

Healthcare is a concern for families across Wyoming, particularly in rural communities where access to providers can be limited. The Legislature should work to strengthen rural healthcare systems, support efforts to recruit and retain doctors, nurses, and other healthcare professionals, and expand access to telehealth services where appropriate.

We should also look for ways to reduce unnecessary costs, increase transparency, and encourage competition that helps patients receive quality care at a reasonable price. Preventive care and early intervention can improve health outcomes while reducing long-term expenses.

Every Wyoming resident deserves access to affordable, quality healthcare regardless of where they live. I will support policies that help families, seniors, veterans, and rural communities access the care they need without placing additional financial burdens on them.

Wyoming’s public lands are an important part of our heritage, economy, and way of life. They support recreation, agriculture, energy development, wildlife, and tourism. Any decisions regarding public lands should be made carefully, transparently, and with significant public input.

I believe land management should balance conservation, responsible development, public access, and the needs of local communities. Wyoming residents should have a strong voice in decisions that affect the lands they use and depend upon.

As a legislator, I would support collaborative approaches that bring together local governments, land users, conservation interests, and industry stakeholders to ensure our public lands continue to benefit current and future generations of Wyomingites.

Property taxes are a significant concern for many homeowners, especially seniors and families living on fixed or limited incomes. At the same time, counties rely on property tax revenue to fund essential services such as public safety, roads, and local government operations.

I believe we should pursue responsible property tax relief while ensuring communities have the resources necessary to provide important services. Any reforms should recognize the unique circumstances of different counties and avoid creating unintended financial hardships for local governments.

The goal should be a balanced approach that protects taxpayers while maintaining the services that residents depend on every day. I would work with local officials and constituents to identify solutions that are fair, sustainable, and responsive to local needs.

The Legislature plays an important role in creating conditions that allow businesses and workers to succeed. Government cannot create prosperity on its own, but it can help provide the foundation for economic growth through infrastructure, education, workforce development, and a stable business environment.

Wyoming should continue supporting entrepreneurship, small businesses, agriculture, energy production, and emerging industries that can help diversify our economy. We should also focus on retaining young people by expanding opportunities for good-paying careers within the state.

I support policies that encourage investment, innovation, and job creation while ensuring economic growth benefits communities throughout Wyoming. A strong economy provides greater opportunity for families and strengthens the future of our state.

Campaign contributions should be transparent, lawful, and disclosed in a timely manner so voters can make informed decisions. The public deserves confidence that elected officials are accountable to the people they represent.

My approach is simple: I will be transparent about campaign contributions and follow all reporting requirements. I believe voters should be able to easily access information about who is supporting candidates and how campaign funds are being spent.

Wyoming should continue evaluating its campaign finance reporting systems to ensure they remain transparent, understandable, and accessible to the public. Public trust is strengthened when citizens can clearly see how campaigns are funded and when elected officials are committed to openness and accountability.
Rock Springs, Wyoming all my life. 40 years.
Bio from my website is fine.

Wyoming’s biggest challenge is creating opportunities for future generations while protecting the industries and values that define us. Energy, mining, agriculture, and construction remain vital, and we must support them while responsibly diversifying our economy.

We can grow without losing what makes Wyoming special. That means investing in education, creating good paying jobs, and strengthening communities so young people can build their futures here.

As a legislator, I support policies that encourage growth and innovation while avoiding unnecessary burdens on our core industries. Continued investment in infrastructure, workforce development, and education is essential to keeping Wyoming competitive.

At the same time, we must preserve our open spaces, wildlife, and strong sense of community. Wyoming doesn’t need to change its identity to succeed we can grow while staying true to who we are.

Healthcare affordability and access are challenges for families across Wyoming, especially in rural communities. We must ensure our rural hospitals, clinics and providers remain viable so Wyomingites can access care close to home. We also need to recruit and retain healthcare professionals by supporting training opportunities and reducing barriers for providers who want to practice in Wyoming.

Increasing competition can help lower costs and expand choices. Wyoming's small population often means limited insurance options. I believe we should strongly encourage our federal delegation to pursue reforms that allow health insurance to be sold across state lines, giving Wyomingites access to more plans and greater competition in the marketplace.

Our focus should be on practical, fiscally responsible solutions that improve access, strengthen local healthcare systems and help make healthcare more affordable for Wyoming families.

For the most part, I believe in keeping public lands in public hands. Growing up in southwest Wyoming has taught me there can be exceptions, but they should be limited, practical, and locally driven.
When communities need room to grow, there should be a reasonable process for acquiring small parcels of public land. Land exchanges should remain an option when they benefit the public, and private landowners should not be unfairly landlocked.
The key is balance. Wyoming has a long history of cooperation among landowners, local governments, industry, recreationists, and public land managers. We should continue that approach and ensure decisions reflect Wyoming’s needs and values.
I do not support opening large swaths of public land for sale or transfer. Public lands are an important part of Wyoming’s economy, heritage, and way of life.

Wyoming's counties are not all the same, and a one size fits all approach to property tax relief can create unintended consequences. I support providing meaningful property tax relief to homeowners, but we must also recognize that property taxes fund essential local services such as schools, roads, law enforcement, fire protection, and other community needs.

My approach is to pursue targeted, sustainable relief while giving local governments the flexibility to address their unique circumstances. Any reduction in property tax revenue should be carefully evaluated to avoid simply shifting the burden elsewhere or forcing cuts to critical services.

It would be foolhardy to chase property tax relief only to create the conditions for an income tax or ever-increasing fees and assessments to make up the difference. Wyoming's competitive advantage has long been its low tax environment, and we should be careful not to trade one tax burden for another.

The Wyoming Legislature has an important role in economic development, but government should be a partner, not the driving force. Our job is to maintain a tax and regulatory environment that encourages investment, job creation, and long-term growth.
Wyoming should have someone there to answer the phone when opportunities come calling. We need to be responsive, competitive, and ready to showcase what our state has to offer. That means supporting workforce development, investing in critical infrastructure, and ensuring permitting processes are predictable and efficient.
Economic development is not about picking winners and losers. It is about creating an environment where businesses of all sizes can grow and succeed. Wyoming's low-tax, pro-business climate is one of our greatest strengths, and we should continue building on it while protecting our quality of life.

Transparency in campaign donations is essential. Voters should know where candidates’ funding comes from to make informed decisions.

Wyoming’s system offers some transparency, but it can be improved. The Legislature should continue reviewing it thoughtfully.

I support clear disclosure rules that inform voters while respecting constitutional rights and avoiding unnecessary burdens. Open campaigns and accurate information help build public trust.
I live in Buffalo, Wyoming. I have lived in Buffalo for over 30 years. I was born in Worland, Wyoming.
I will be 71.

I am a lifelong Wyomingite, raised in Johnson and Sheridan counties, where I learned the values of hard work, personal responsibility, and service to others. I raised my children in Buffalo. I understand how important it is to keep Wyoming a place where future generations can live, work, and raise their families. For three decades, I have served Wyoming through public service and community leadership, including a Johnson County Commissioner, a CNA, AEMT, Public Health Response Coordinator, Emergency Management Coordinator, and Public Information Officer for local fire districts. These experiences have given me firsthand insight into the challenges facing Wyoming families, farmers and ranchers, healthcare providers, emergency responders, and local governments. I am running to bring practical experience, fiscal responsibility, and conservative Wyoming point of view to the Legislature while protecting our freedoms, strengthening our communities, and securing Wyoming’s future.

Wyoming's greatest challenge is ensuring long-term economic stability.   As markets change and federal policies continue to impact industries, we must protect our core economic drivers while encouraging responsible diversification and innovation.

Another challenge is retaining and attracting young families, skilled workers, healthcare professionals, and first responders.  We must create opportunities that allow them to stay and succeed here.

Protecting Wyoming's water is another challenge. I would support our agencies with additional resources to meet this challenge.

Wyoming can capitalize on its strong work ethic, independent attitude, abundant natural resources, low-tax environment.  I will support policies that strengthen our energy and agricultural sectors, promote responsible economic growth, improve workforce development, and ensure state government lives within its means.

I will focus on practical solutions, local control, and responsible stewardship of taxpayer dollars.

Wyomingites struggle with rising insurance costs, long travel distances for care, and shortages of healthcare professionals.

 We must support rural hospitals, clinics, EMS services, and long-term care facilities that are often the backbone of healthcare in our communities. Also, we should continue reducing barriers to recruiting and retaining healthcare professionals, including nurses, physicians, mental health providers, and emergency responders.

We should review regulations and mandates that unnecessarily increase healthcare costs.   It is also important to support the efforts of the Wyoming Department of Health to manage and distribute the Rural Health Transformation funds.

My work experience has given me firsthand insight into the challenges facing patients and providers. As a legislator, I will work to strengthen Wyoming's healthcare workforce, support rural healthcare infrastructure, promote innovation.

Wyoming's public lands support ranching, energy production, recreation, wildlife habitat, tourism, and the outdoor traditions that define our way of life. I do not support broad efforts to sell public lands that would reduce public access or limit opportunities for future generations.

As a legislator, I will support multiple-use management that balances responsible energy and mineral development, healthy forests and rangelands, wildlife conservation, and public access. I also support stronger coordination among federal agencies, state and local governments, and private landowners to ensure decisions reflect local knowledge and Wyoming values.

The goal is balanced stewardship. By emphasizing local input, responsible management, and long-term planning, we can ensure Wyoming's public lands continue to benefit the people who live here today and future generations.

Property tax relief is warranted, but it must be balanced with the reality that counties, cities, schools, and special districts rely on those revenues to provide essential services.
 As a legislator, I will support a thoughtful approach that provides meaningful tax relief while carefully evaluating the impact on local governments and taxpayers. 
I would advocate for a state backfill or stabilization framework to mitigate revenue losses in counties with limited alternatives, particularly those without strong mineral production or tourism-based economies.
Any property tax relief should be accompanied by transparency and accountability regarding how lost revenue will affect services such as public safety, roads, senior services, and local infrastructure. We must avoid simply shifting costs from one group of taxpayers to another or creating long-term budget shortfalls.
Wyoming taxpayers deserve both tax relief and responsible government that lives within its means.

The Legislature should not attempt to direct the economy but instead create the conditions for private-sector growth, support local decision-making, and ensure Wyoming remains a place where businesses can invest, expand, and create good-paying jobs.
The Legislature should focus on maintaining Wyoming’s competitive advantages—low taxes, reasonable regulation, reliable infrastructure investment, and a strong commitment to property rights. These factors are essential for attracting and retaining businesses and families.
The Legislature can help by investing in workforce development, career and technical education, and partnerships between schools, community colleges, and industry. A skilled workforce is one of the most important factors in long-term economic growth.

Wyoming’s campaign finance system should be regularly reviewed to ensure transparency, public trust, and fairness in elections. Overall, I believe voters have a right to know who is funding political campaigns and how money is being spent.
If improvements are needed, they should focus on modernization, efficiency, and clarity so that Wyoming voters can easily see who is influencing elections and make informed decisions at the ballot box.

I live in Johnson County Wyoming with my wife and two children. Like over one-half of the current Wyoming residents I was born in another state, in my case Virginia, but I moved here first as a young man and again permanently over five years ago.

I am a retired big game and small game biologist who spent three decades managing game populations. For the last six years, I have worked as a National Director and Expert for Gun Owners of America protecting American's Right to Keep and Bear Arms. I have B.S. and M.S. degrees and over 30 years of experience in governmental policy. I am a strong believer in the United States and Wyoming Constitutions and in the right of all people to be left alone by an out-of-control government.

Some of the biggest challenges facing Wyoming include: young people leaving Wyoming for other states, maintaining enough water for current and future needs, federal overreach into every aspect of our lives, and threats against our core economic engines of coal, oil, gas, other mining, agriculture, etc. As a State Legislator I would work to reduce taxes to keep Wyoming affordable and fight regulations that stifle free enterprise and all businesses.

We need to remove unnecessary regulations that make healthcare more expensive. Many people forget that Obamacare changed American healthcare in catastrophic ways, and the state needs to work to get back to a system that allowed doctors and companies to operate without the excessive government rules and costs. I had a doctor friend who retired rather than spend millions to comply with the excessive regulations forced upon doctors by Obamacare.

I do not support efforts to sell public lands. I do believe federal lands need to be managed better - especially in terms of forest management which has been neglected for decades. I would work for state actions that could facilitate better cooperation between the state and federal government to ensure Wyoming residents benefited from public lands for hunting, fishing, hiking, camping, and other recreation. I would also work towards land swaps when those were beneficial for both the private landowner and the public. These would not be forced upon anyone, but there are cases where both the private landowner and the pubic could benefit from trading lands.
I would work to cut waste, fraud, and abuse to ensure our state workers can be compensated fairly and that we can fund essential services like roads, bridges, police, fire, and rescue.

The government should not pick winners and losers in business. It is wrong to use taxpayers money to support industry X while ignoring industry Y. We need the Wyoming legislature to remove unnecessary taxes and regulations on all businesses to allow those businesses to thrive on their own. For example, I have a close friend with a large company in Buffalo, and he would hire several high-paying positions immediately if not for unnecessary state restrictions on his business.

I ran in 2024 and raised 98% of my money from Wyoming donors. My opponent, the current incumbent, took tens of thousands of dollars in Political Action Committee (PAC) money. My 2024 records prove that I stand with the citizens rather than with the power-brokers.
I live in Cheyenne, I have lived here for 51 years. I was born in Colorado.
My background is in Criminal Justice. I have worked with juveniles and adults.

What I find to be a challenge to Wyoming and it's citizens is the lack of available mental health care and the lack of access to all healthcare. As a legislator I would address these situations by pointing out how much this lack of care costs the state both financially and in human costs.

I realize that Medicare expansion is not popular in Wyoming as most Wyomingites don't want the federal government involved any more than necessary, however, what is the value of the health of Wyoming citizens? We need to take another look at Medicare expansion in Wyoming.
Sell or transfer it to whom?

I would love to pay less in taxes myself, but again, at what cost? The money has to come from somewhere and it all boils down to the same pocket, which is the pocket of the taxpayer. I really think it is a circular discussion, six on one hand, half a dozen on the other hand.

Economic development is clearly an important issue, especially considering the previous question. Instead of limiting higher education and spouting off about indoctrination, Wyoming needs to work toward integrating continuing education, both University and Trade Schools as the norm in our state. Without a critically thinking, educated populous, we have no chance at success in economic development.

This is my first campaign, and a very broke one at that. I don't know how campaigns that receive large amounts of funds are monitored. I believe in being held accountable for the funding that I do have, and it is simply fiscally responsible and ethical to be transparent with donor dollars. Constituents should know where their donations are being applied.
I have lived in the North-West side of Laramie County and been in Wyoming for about 15 years. I was born in Minnesota but moved to Colorado as a teenager where I spent most of my career.

My career has been in technology, computing facilities, construction and management. I started at 12 when I designed and built my first computer. I received my degree in computer science and began in a computing center. My "big picture" skills were recognized, and I proposed, designed and constructed larger and larger projects. I chaired a computing steering committee at a 1500+ employee company with multiple divisions and subsidiaries. I led teams to propose, design, construct and manage million-dollar technology projects with a focus in networking, computing and data centers. During that same period, I worked with city, county and state boards to create plans, construct and manage technology projects. I retired early from my career due to personal reasons. 

I bring a unique knowledge and perspective that can benefit the people and the state. I understand the challenges of issues from both sides and have strived to develop strategies where both parties can achieve their goals.

Responsible Business Development - As a community we need to understand the pros/cons of business development coming into the area and state. 

Promote Public Education/Healthcare - Without well supported education, healthcare and public support we will continue to see people leave the state for areas with better resources.

Retention of Public Lands and Funding Them - Let's face it, many of us have come to Wyoming to have better access to wilderness areas within the state. We need to save these lands and fund them.

Removal or Reduction of Property Taxes - As with many aspects of tax reduction, it all sounds good initially. But when you look at who it benefits, what stresses it puts on existing public systems and what would need to be done to keep the same level of services available to the public, the tax reduction can look not as bright and shiny.

The government should be a good steward to the public interests and balance economic growth with public functions and operations.

Like other states, Wyoming can initiate programs which help its citizens with healthcare without major increases in taxes. These programs require the different government segments to work together to develop a functioning and sustaining program. 

Healthcare along with education is of primary importance to the state to retain and expand its population and workforce.

No. Public lands are a promise to the people by the government to maintain this proud country. Wyoming has some of the most beautiful areas and wildlife in the country which is why many citizens have moved or stay in this state. The government should also provide and maintain adequate funding to these areas to retain the beauty and operation for future generations.

Selling or the transfer of public lands breaks this promise to the people.

This is one of my top issues. it is irresponsible of the government and people to cut property taxes without understanding the impacts to public services we enjoy.

Wyoming's property taxes are well below many states in comparison which already strains the local service systems. Cutting them even further without adequate backfill funding sources is a design to push people away and out of the state. 

The government's job is to help support its citizens and local services is a large function of that support. I personally would like fire and rescue, public education, police and the many other services to stay and even be enhanced in my community.

The government is the stewards of the public and the public pays for the government.
It's the government's responsibility to ensure the state stays viable by bringing in new businesses and diversifying the economy. 

History has taught us that having a single large business or businesses in the same sector can mean devastation if the business or business sector wanes or collapses. 

Economic development in many business sectors makes for a strong economy which helps all people and the community as a whole.

I believe with all the major issues needing to be addressed in the state, campaign reform could be addressed but its priority is low compared to them.
The state is dealing with issues which could heavily impact it and its citizens. These issues should take priority before campaign donations and expenditures are addressed.

Thank you for allowing me to voice my opinions and address these questions.
My desire is to represent the public and their interests as best as I can if I am elected to office.
I currently live in eastern Laramie County and have lived here for 10 years. I have lived in Wyoming since 2005 and was born in Victoria, TX.

I grew up on a cattle ranch, an experience that led me to Texas Tech University, where I earned a degrees in Range Science, focusing my graduate research on prescribed fire and grazing. Today, my husband, Jason, and I own and operate our ranch in Laramie County and a consulting company. Through consulting, I provide expertise in agriculture, conservation, and policy. Before focusing on our businesses, I served as Executive Director of the Wyoming Stock Growers Land Trust and as a policy advisor to former Wyoming Governor Matt Mead. I’ve also worked with the Western Landowners Alliance, the WY Department of Agriculture, the Agricultural Research Service, and Northwest College. Across these roles, I’ve worked to keep working lands productive and economically viable. The values that guide this work—practical problem solving, respect for local knowledge, and a commitment to community—have broad application beyond land management and would serve me well in the legislature.

Wyoming is built on hard work, strong communities, and the responsible use of our natural resources. We face real challenges — from pressures on our core industries to the need for a more resilient, diverse economy and the increasing outmigration of our young people. But these challenges also create opportunities to strengthen what we already do well and build new pathways that keep our kids and grandkids here at home.
As a legislator, I would bring my experience working with landowners, businesses, and local governments to support the industries that define Wyoming while encouraging thoughtful growth and diversification. Our future depends on good jobs, strong schools, and communities where families can put down roots and thrive.
I believe we can honor the values that make Wyoming what it is while building something that lasts — an economy that works for working people, an education system that prepares the next generation, and communities that remain resilient and connected.

The rising cost of healthcare and the difficulty of accessing services are real challenges for Wyoming families, especially in rural communities. We need reforms that reduce costs, expand access, and meet people where they are. The Legislature can help by exploring opportunities such as expanded telemedicine, increasing the number of providers, and strengthening home health and in home support options.
As a legislator, I would work closely with the members of the 2026 Health Insurance Affordability Task Force and other partners to fully understand the barriers Wyoming citizens face and to develop practical solutions to deliver the quality healthcare our communities deserve.

I do not support broad efforts to sell or transfer Wyoming’s public lands. Our federal lands provide real value to the people of this state — from recreation and hunting to livestock grazing and mineral development. I support the multiple use concept and would continue working to ensure these lands remain productive, accessible working lands. Wyoming’s state trust lands are also an important asset, offering open space, recreation, grazing opportunities, and critical revenue for our schools. 
While I oppose sweeping efforts to dispose of public or state lands, I recognize there may be individual situations where selling or transferring a parcel is clearly in the best interest of Wyoming’s people and those opportunities should be carefully considered.
I believe in keeping working lands working and understand the balance required across our state to manage them responsibly for future generations.

Property taxes must be continually refined so people can afford to buy homes, stay in their homes, and build equity and pride of place. At the same time, I recognize that every Wyoming county depends on a different mix of revenue, and our core industries—as well as efforts to diversify our economy—rely on stable, predictable local funding.
Taxes play a critical role in maintaining the infrastructure we depend on: roads, schools, emergency services, and more. Wyoming is a revenue generating state, and we must continue supporting programs that strengthen our economy and serve our communities well.
I am not interested in raising taxes, but I do believe we need honest, informed conversations about how we provide relief while keeping counties functional. That means exploring responsible adjustments that help homeowners who need relief—without jeopardizing essential services.

The Legislature plays an important role in strengthening economic development. At the forefront of that role is creating clear, consistent processes and regulations so businesses have confidence when they look to Wyoming. We should streamline and reduce cumbersome regulations and permitting timelines that slow down investment.
Economic development also depends on maintaining and improving critical infrastructure and supporting workforce development so Wyoming has the skilled workers our industries need. Careful investment of state revenues through a balanced budget is essential to sustaining these efforts over the long term.
When these pieces work together—predictable regulations, strong infrastructure, a skilled workforce, and responsible budgeting—they have the potential to positively stimulate Wyoming’s economy. I can be part of the solution by helping create a stable, reliable environment that encourages growth while protecting taxpayers.

I appreciate every campaign donation because it represents trust in the work I’m doing and the future we’re building for Wyoming. When someone chooses to give, they’re partnering in a vision—not trying to influence it. I take that seriously.
Currently, I do not see a need to revise Wyoming’s system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures.
I live in Laramie County, near Cheyenne. I've lived in what is now HD43 for almost 16 years, and in Laramie County since 2002. I was born in Chicago.

As the incumbent representative for House District 43, I serve on the Corporations & Revenue Committees. During my time in office, I have sponsored or cosponsored 86 bills, 17 of which are now law. Before serving in the Legislature, I spent much of my adult life in retail banking, working in banks & credit unions. I worked my way up to executive management, retiring as Executive Vice President of a local credit union, with a strong background in personal finance, budgeting, lending, compliance, human resources, & financial analysis. I was a loan officer for many years & have experience in real estate, commercial, & consumer lending. I have a BS in business management with a minor in accounting. I've led local financial seminars & hosted two radio shows in Cheyenne to help people understand money management. I understand the financial pressures Wyoming families & small businesses face, & I believe government should budget and spend responsibly, transparently, & accountably.

Wyoming’s biggest challenge is keeping government limited, transparent, and accountable to the people who pay the bills. State government must control spending, root out waste, and limit taxpayer dollars to necessary functions. Families are being squeezed by rising costs, including property taxes, and government must not make that worse.

We also face serious challenges in education, election integrity, energy, and growth. Economic development should benefit citizens, not special interests, and Wyoming must protect its energy economy, water, and private property.

Government spending, contracts, records, and court decisions should be open to the public. I oppose government using NDAs and believe the judicial branch needs far greater transparency.

Wyoming must do better for children. Parents must remain central in education, test scores must improve, and we must protect children, born and unborn.

I don’t work for the government. I work for you.

Wyoming currently spends almost a billion dollars annually on healthcare through Medicaid, CHIP, behavioral health, aging services, and other programs. The question is not simply whether government can spend more, but whether those dollars produce better access, lower costs, and better outcomes.

The Legislature should focus on transparency, competition, and accountability. Patients must be able to understand prices before care. We should reduce barriers, support rural providers, expand telehealth where appropriate, and audit state programs for waste and duplication.

We must protect vulnerable citizens, including children, seniors, people with disabilities, and those who truly cannot provide for themselves. But we must do that in a way that preserves personal responsibility, protects taxpayers, and does not build a larger, less accountable system.

Healthcare policy should be judged by results: Can Wyoming families get care? Can they afford it? Are taxpayers getting value?

I do not support backroom deals or blanket efforts to sell off Wyoming’s public lands. Government at all levels must be transparent in all land sales, transfers, exchanges, and management decisions. That means public notice, full disclosure of appraisals and terms, and input from affected citizens.

Wyoming land should be managed for Wyoming people, not federal bureaucrats, political insiders, or special interests. That means protecting public access, private property rights, grazing, energy and mineral development, wildlife, water, recreation, and the long-term value of state trust lands.

The Legislature should require clear records, open meetings, accessible maps, disclosed negotiations, and accountability for any state land transaction. Citizens should know who benefits, what the land is worth, what access is gained or lost, and whether the deal serves the public interest.

Good land management starts with sunshine. Wyoming people deserve to see the facts before decisions are made.

Property tax relief must start with spending discipline. Counties are not all alike, but taxpayers are being asked to fund government growth faster than their income has grown. Paying rising property taxes over a lifetime is unsustainable.

Laramie County is not poor. Its FY2025 budget showed more than $292 million in total available revenue, including years of substantial cash carryforward. At the same time, major data center development could add many millions to the local tax base. Since data centers are industrial in use, they should be taxed at the industrial rate, not treated as ordinary commercial property nor receive any tax breaks of any kind.

As a member of the Revenue Committee, I helped craft the 25% residential property tax reduction & will continue looking for responsible ways to provide more relief. Local revenue needs matter, but government must go on a spending diet before asking homeowners for more. We do not have a revenue problem as much as a spending problem.

The Legislature’s role in economic development must be limited, transparent, and focused on core infrastructure, not picking winners and losers. Government should provide a fair tax and regulatory climate, roads, water, power, broadband, and public safety, then let private businesses compete.

The Wyoming Business Council needs real oversight and evaluation. It is a state agency, yet uses corporate language like “CEO,” manages grants, loans, & investments to non-Wyomingites, and has not proven taxpayers are getting results. Economic development must not be welfare for the well-connected.

I supported Speaker Neiman’s approach to move unspent Business Ready Community money into county consensus grants for capital projects, maintenance, & public infrastructure. That is a better model: local agreement and public projects.

Before spending more, the Legislature should require full reporting, ROI analysis, & a hard look at what should be kept, transferred, privatized, or eliminated.

Campaign donations must be transparent, lawful, and easy for citizens to review. I do not believe candidates should be controlled by PACs, lobbyists, or out-of-state interests. Voters deserve to know who is funding campaigns and whether elected officials represent citizens or donors.

The data I reviewed shows 4,516 PAC-related contributions from mostly unions, over 16 years, totaling $2,170,461.88, with an average contribution of $480.62. Based on contributor location, at least $1.84 million came from non-Wyoming PACs, not counting one $50,000 RGA entry with no city/state listed. Governor candidate records totaled 238 contributions and $593,931.63, averaging $2,495.51.

Wyoming’s system needs reform. Reports must be easier to search, download, and compare. PACs rarely support conservative candidates, and voters should be able to see patterns in PAC giving, independent expenditures, amendments, donor locations, and cumulative totals.
Lived in Laramie for almost 12 years now and was born in Manhattan Kansas
I have worked in the private sector in engineering for the majority of my life and believe I will bring a new perspective and common-sense approach to the house that I use in the private sector.

Some of our biggest challenges facing our communities are the K-12 funding, the influx of big tech coming in and issues with our housing. I am looking to find other avenues of funding for K-12 and how we can uphold our constitutional duties. For the issues of big tech I want to see more transparency in how they intend on using infrastructure and will be good stewards of not just the infrastructure but the environment. As far as housing I would push for a common sense approach to a renters and home owners bill of rights.

I believe we have a system that works currently; however, as a state, we need to make sure that healthcare is a priority. There is no reason that someone should be denied medical attention they need because of financial status, and in turn, they should not be discouraged from reaching out for this help just because of the costs that they will incur. As a state, we need to take care of the one's in need through programs that assist individuals in need.

I do not, I don't see why giving up our greatest asset is a solution for a long-term problem. Not to mention land locking our public lands is something that should not be done since the public should have access to public lands. I will look at other streams of revenue that make sense over the long term and not just in the short term.

I will be looking into how we can generate other forms of long-term revenue to offset any tax relief. This is a very delicate balance between fixed-income individuals and the need for property tax revenue.
There are currently many avenues the state takes to boost economic development, some of which just need to have more transparency on the decisions the agencies make for the regions they will impact.

I feel that as long as there are checks and balances, and donations are watched over and looked at there isn't an issue. We as a state just need to make sure there is transparency in all that we do when running for office, be it at the local to state levels.
I currently live in beautiful West Side Laramie. My husband and I bought our home here in 2018 but I have lived in Laramie since 2014. I was born in Pueblo, Colorado.

It is an honor to currently be serving my third term. I serve on the Agriculture and Public Lands Committee and the Travel, Recreation, and Wildlife Committee. I have previously served on the Education and the Judiciary Committees. 

During my time in the legislature, I have passed laws protecting public land access and various bills aimed at government transparency and accountability. In 2024, after years of community organizing, I helped pass legislation that strengthened oversight of law enforcement in Wyoming. 

I am a nonprofit coach for organizations aiming to build grassroots power. I believe deeply that power is inherent in the people and I work to engage communities on how they can participate in their local governments. I earned my Ph.D. in Experimental Psychology at the University of Wyoming and I enjoy wildlife photography. You may have seen some of my recent work of Rebecca Bextel handing out checks to members of the Freedom Caucus on the Wyoming House Floor.

The greatest issues facing Wyoming is healthcare, the heartbreak of our young people leaving due to lack of opportunities, and out of state extremists funneling resources into Wyoming to divide us and dismantle our public institutions. 

The first two struggles can be solved together – funding programs in healthcare would create jobs for young people, support them in growing their families, and support our aging population. We need new programs that expand maternal healthcare, senior services, and rural healthcare solutions so the money Wyomingites put into healthcare is spent in here, not in bordering states. 

The issue of extremism plays a central role in our state legislature. This burden can be solved by the people in the upcoming elections. Outside of civic accountability through the ballot, I am committed to centering Wyoming problems and solutions, and I will continue working on anything that will improve the lives of the people of Wyoming so they have the freedom to prosper.

Given the recent cuts to healthcare at the federal level, the state should work towards providing insurance and healthcare services to the people of Wyoming beyond Medicaid.
