We need bold solutions that increase healthcare affordability for everyone while also supporting our critical access hospitals and the healthcare services that currently exist. A state-run healthcare system would provide jobs, increase the number of providers, reduce costs, and make it more accessible.

Short of creating a statewide healthcare system, we should create a public health trust fund that pays for healthcare services provided throughout the state from savings and investments. The state already has programs like this, such as the Wyoming Wildlife & Natural Resource Trust Fund which pays for conservation projects using income from investments. These funds could pay to keep our rural critical access hospitals and maternal services afloat while also helping increase services like community health clinics.

Hell no, I do not support efforts to sell off our public land. The idea that the federal government could transfer lands to the state for management is just a foot-in-the-door technique towards public land sales. The state does not have the resources to manage these lands and would ultimately sell most of them off. Once they’re gone, we will never get them back and our future generations will not have the same opportunities we do to enjoy wide open spaces and abundant wildlife.  

I support thoughtful, community-led efforts towards public land swaps, which means that a local community is able to trade property for public land which would increase public access.
 
In 2023, a bill I sponsored was passed which made it illegal for private property owners to post private property signs on public land and I have been the lead lawmaker towards protecting corner crossing. If re-elected, I intend to work towards a constitutional amendment towards no net loss of public lands in Wyoming.

The legislature has created a shell game where we have no plan for how to make up for lost revenues to pay for services, so some day the people of Wyoming will be forced to pay through increased sales tax or other means. No plan is a bad plan. Right now, the state has no plan for how to offset the cuts and no appetite for solving this issue. 

In 2024, the legislature addressed the spike in property values through the property tax refund program and the 4% assessment caps. Of course, we may need to bring a solution for how to make the 4% assessment cap constitutional if the courts find that it is not. I believe property tax cuts should only be available through means tests – where the people who need relief get it, and the billionaires who use Wyoming as a tax haven don’t.

The legislature also passed a constitutional amendment that allows for various tax rates for different types of properties which would allow us to increase property tax rates on data centers.

If the free market and low taxes were going to solve our economic diversification problem, it would have done so already. The state needs to help solve some of the problems that keep businesses from coming here – namely our housing shortage, unaffordable and inaccessible healthcare, and opportunities for young families such as childcare and preschool. 

Aside from structural changes to help businesses be able to support their workforce here, we also need to further support our economic development programs such as the Wyoming Business Council. With the current climate of Wyoming’s legislature being hellbent on tearing down the WBC, we are not going to be able to attract new business. The legislature should take a more supportive role towards supporting the people behind our small businesses and the programs on the ground that help them grow.

I believe that money in politics is corrupting our entire system. I don’t take AIPAC money and I never will. I prefer our campaign is funded by small donations from working class people who already share my values. 

When it comes to PAC money, my policy is that I will take a contribution and I will NOT hesitate to vote against their interests when they don’t align with my community’s values. This stance has meant that I can spend less time raising money and more time doing work on behalf of my community.  

Wyoming has a serious campaign funding problem – out of state interests have long viewed us as a cheap and easy place to influence. Wyoming should require more frequent reporting of campaign donations, especially since the reporting deadline is only a week before the election. We should mandate greater transparency in reporting for independent expenditures such as mailers that are used to sow division and spread lies. Wyoming elections should be Wyoming led and Wyoming funded.

I have lived here in Albany County for over 23 years. My house is about 10 miles south of Laramie, in House District 46. That's been my home ever since I moved to Wyoming after retiring from the military.

Education: BS, MS, PhD in electrical engineering, licensed Professional Engineer (PE) in Wyoming

Experience:  
30 years military service (Navy and Air Force, enlisted and officer).  Retired as a Lieutenant Colonel.  Leadership instructor, firearms instructor, R&D engineer, faculty member at the U.S. Air Force Academy.
23 years teaching as an engineering faculty member at the University of Wyoming, including 6 years as Dean of the College of Engineering and Physical Sciences.  Recently retired from UW.

Skills: More than four decades of real-world leadership experience for both small and large organizations, managing people (as many as 300+ people), budgets (as much as $758 million), and efficiently achieving goals and objectives while balancing available resources.  

My leadership training and experience have given me the ability to keep my ego and personal views out of decisions, avoid micromanaging, and trust competent people to do their jobs.  And always---integrity first.

Wyoming has great opportunities. Our core industries (energy, agriculture, mining, tourism, etc.) are making great strides, small businesses are expanding, and entrepreneurs are starting new businesses in the state. Wyoming has the most attractive business environment in the country, and our economy is poised for growth.  But there are challenges.  We must pay close attention to public concern about proposals such as those for datacenters and windfarms, and closely evaluate the advantages and disadvantages of such proposals both individually and as part of the big picture of what we want for Wyoming. We also want our children to be able to stay in the state, which means both a growing economy but also affordable, available housing. As a legislator, I would learn about and listen to all sides of these issues and others, get input from my constituents, ignore any pressure from out-of-state groups or narrow, personal agendas, and work on legislation that benefits Wyoming and our people.

I recently spent an entire day at the 2026 Wyoming Healthcare Policy Summit, and learned a great deal about the challenges facing the Wyoming healthcare landscape.  There are currently six rural hospitals in Wyoming that may have to close within a few years due to funding issues.  We have both a shortage of healthcare professionals, and a structural challenge of our medical facilities facing significant fixed costs, low patient populations, and inadequate reimbursement from sources such as Medicaid and Medicare.  We have to find innovative ways to attract and retain more healthcare professionals to the state (and this also involves the problem of insufficient affordable, available housing), and explore ways we can help medical facilities survive with low patient counts and low reimbursement. This is not an easy problem to solve, but it must be a high priority for the legislature.  Quality of life in Wyoming depends upon it.

In general, no I don't support that. There may be rare special circumstances that can be evaluated on a case-by-case basis, but overall I believe public lands should stay public, and they should be accessible to the public. Responsible use and stewardship of our public lands is something all citizens of Wyoming should expect and demand. We must work hand in hand with federal organizations such as the BLM and the Forest Service, and with our federal Congressional delegation, to ensure we protect our public lands.

Essential government services funded by property taxes cannot be allowed to wither and become ineffective due to loss of funding as a result of property tax reductions. While back-filling the losses incurred by cities and counties if property taxes continue to be reduced might seem to be a possible solution, it can’t be a permanent solution. Broadly disseminated, honest, and balanced communication to the population is essential on this topic. The voters must be made aware of the need for a reasonable level of property taxes, and convinced not to vote for an arbitrary reduction with little or no consideration to the unavoidable consequences. It's important for all of us to keep in mind that taxes are necessary to fund government services that can’t be funded or performed by individuals, but not more than that. Taxes should be broadly based, nondiscriminatory, and only at a level needed to fund those essential government services.  Nothing more than that.

I agree that the legislature can help stimulate economic development, but we must proceed with caution. We don't want to get into the trap of picking winners and losers, but we must also be cognizant that so many other states actively engage in legislative programs to attract industry to their state. If we do nothing, Wyoming will lose out.  That being said, keeping government out of the way, maintaining a favorable tax structure, and encouraging  expansion of both our core industries and small businesses in general, along with enabling the creation of new businesses... all that together will lead to the best way to expand our economy. Over-regulation, regulatory overreach, unfunded mandates, and an encroaching “nanny state” (mostly from the federal level) are some of the biggest obstacles to economic prosperity for Wyoming. The legislature should be alert to these obstacles and find ways to reduce them whenever possible.

Funding for political campaigns should be as transparent to the voters as possible.  Big donors hiding behind anonymous, typically out-of-state, organizations that try to manipulate Wyoming politics can only hurt Wyoming in the long run.  I would support whatever the Wyoming legislature can do to make campaign funding more visible to the voters.  One possible problem currently is the deadline for reporting campaign donations is so close to the election (both primary and general) that few voters are able to use that information in time to consider that in their voting decisions. We should look into ways to make that information available in a more timely manner for the voting public.

We currently live at 1974 Harrison Street, Laramie.  We moved into the new house Nov. 28 1998 and have lived there since then. We lived in Rawlins prior to moving to Laramie April 14, 1997/July 1, 1997 (I worked for ACSO, but didn't get the family moved until July 1, 1997.  I was still preaching full time in Rawlins and travelled between the two until the family move and training academy was completed in mid June 1997.  I was born in Scott City, Kansas
I will be 76 years old on election day.

Gary Wilken is a retired United Methodist pastor, law enforcement professional, and lifelong public servant in communities across Colorado and Wyoming.
Gary earned a B.A. in History from Colorado State University and a Master of Divinity from the Pacific School of Religion, where he also trained as a police chaplain and served with the Oakland Police Department Reserve Program.
Throughout his career, Gary has united ministry and public service, pastoring rural congregations, advocating for environmental protections in eastern Colorado, and serving the Albany County Sheriff's Office as a detention deputy, corporal, and dual-certified patrol officer. He later worked as Albany County's Code Enforcement Officer, addressing public safety, zoning, and hazardous waste concerns alongside residents and state agencies.
Gary and his wife, Bernice, have been married 46 years, building a life rooted in faith, family, and service- values that continue to guide his leadership today.

The biggest challenges facing Wyoming today are the issues of training and providing housing, healthcare, education, a living wage and care for the environment that houses us.  As a legislator I will engage in continuing education and investigation of these issues with my colleagues in the legislature, and offer constuctive changes to meet our challenges through cooperation and compassionate care, listening, and discussion.
The legislature and citizens of wyoming need to come together to study, share information and diversify many of the approaches to medical, educational, and systemic challenges.

I do not support the sell or transfer of public land.  I call for stewardship and responsible care for the public lands that are under our responsibility.  We as citizens of Wyoming need to accept the responsibility to care for as well as enjoy the local, state and federal lands we have within our state boundaries.

As a legislator I will respect the property rights of our citizens while recognizing the need to provide taxes and programs to care for public land.  I have experienced the cutbacks of limited income that we have gone through in Albany County, with significant Public lands that do not pay taxes in Wyoming.  A PILT (Payment in Lieu of Taxes) program helps level the use of state and federal lands to meet the growing costs of living in Albany County
The legislature needs to continue being in dialogue with the business community, as well as the extractive recource users, the manufacturing and research and healthy growth of our state
I appreciate financial support from our constituents, but will try to limit contributions to a equitable level so that all persons have input but not domination of free choice.
Rock Springs, since 2021.  In Wyoming since 2019.  Previously lived in Cody, WY 2001-2004

I am an Army Special Operations veteran and a military-trained Physician Associate specializing in Orthopedics. For most of my adult life, I have served as either a protector or a healer.

My wife Tracy and I have been married for nearly three decades. We have four children and three grandchildren. We moved to Rock Springs in 2021 and previously lived in Cody from 2001-2004 and 2019-2021.

I currently serve in the Wyoming House of Representatives on the Labor, Health and Social Services Committee and the Transportation, Highways and Military Affairs Committee.

Before serving in the Legislature, I spent years caring for patients and helping families through difficult medical decisions. In the Legislature, I have focused on protecting constitutional rights, supporting parents, improving healthcare access, promoting fiscal responsibility and ensuring government remains accountable to the people.

I am running for reelection because Wyoming is worth protecting.

Wyoming's biggest challenge is preserving our way of life while keeping the state affordable for working families, retirees and young people. Rising property taxes, government spending, healthcare costs and workforce shortages are concerns I hear regularly from constituents.

Our greatest opportunities remain the industries and people that built Wyoming: energy, agriculture, small business, natural resources and a strong culture of independence.

As a legislator, I will continue working to reduce the tax burden on Wyoming families, protect private property rights, support reliable and affordable energy production, improve healthcare access and reduce unnecessary regulations.

Wyoming does not have a revenue problem; it has a spending and prioritization problem. Government should focus on its core responsibilities, live within its means and respect the taxpayers who fund it.

As a healthcare provider, I have seen firsthand the challenges facing patients, providers and rural hospitals.

The Legislature cannot solve every problem in healthcare, but it can remove barriers that drive up costs and reduce access. We should continue expanding telehealth where appropriate, encourage more healthcare professionals to practice in Wyoming and reduce unnecessary regulations that make care more expensive.

We should also increase price transparency so patients know what they are paying for and can make informed decisions about their care.

Many rural communities struggle to attract providers. We should look for practical ways to support healthcare delivery in those areas while maintaining quality care close to home.

Healthcare decisions should be made by patients and their providers, not bureaucracies. My goal is simple: lower costs, increase access and ensure Wyoming families can receive quality care when and where they need it.

I do not support the sale of Wyoming's public lands and would vote against legislation that broadly authorizes their sale.

Public lands are important to Wyoming's economy, culture and way of life. Hunters, anglers, ranchers, energy producers, outdoor recreationists and local communities all rely on access to these lands.

At the same time, I believe Wyoming should have a stronger voice in how federal lands within our borders are managed. Decisions made in Washington, D.C., often fail to account for the realities faced by the people who live and work here.

Land management should follow multiple-use principles that balance recreation, grazing, wildlife, energy production and responsible stewardship.

The people closest to the land generally understand it best. Wyoming's interests are best served when local communities have a meaningful role in land management decisions.

I strongly support meaningful property tax relief and ultimately believe Wyoming should work toward eliminating property taxes on primary residences.

People should not spend a lifetime paying off a home only to face the possibility of being taxed out of it. Property ownership is one of the most basic rights we have.

At the same time, counties, municipalities and special districts provide services that citizens depend upon. The answer is not to continue raising taxes. The answer is to prioritize spending and focus government on its core responsibilities.

Wyoming has significant financial resources and reserve accounts. Before asking taxpayers for more, government should carefully examine where money is being spent and whether those expenditures are truly necessary.

I believe Wyoming can provide meaningful property tax relief while maintaining essential services through responsible budgeting, spending discipline and long-term planning.

The Legislature's primary role in economic development is to remove obstacles, not create new programs.

Government does not create prosperity. Wyoming workers, ranchers, energy producers, small business owners and entrepreneurs do.

The Legislature should maintain a stable regulatory environment, protect private property rights, keep taxes low, support reliable infrastructure and ensure businesses have the freedom to grow and hire.

I do not believe government should be in the business of picking winners and losers. When government starts favoring certain industries or businesses, someone else ends up paying the bill.

The best economic development policy is simple: lower taxes, fewer unnecessary regulations, affordable energy and a government that stays focused on its proper role.

I have chosen not to accept direct campaign contributions from PACs or professional associations. My campaign support has come from individual citizens who believe in the principles and policies I advocate.

That is a personal decision, and other candidates are free to make different choices within the law.

Transparency is the most important part of any campaign finance system. Voters should be able to easily see who is contributing to campaigns and how campaign funds are being spent.

Wyoming's reporting requirements already provide substantial public disclosure. If reforms are considered, they should focus on improving transparency and public access to information without creating unnecessary burdens that discourage citizens from running for office.

Ultimately, informed voters are the best safeguard for accountable government.
I currently live in Rock Springs - for the past 34 years. Prior to that I lived in Green River. I am a lifelong resident of Sweetwater County, Wyoming

I worked 20 years for Church & Dwight Company (Arm & Hammer Baking Soda) in various positions. For 17 years I worked shiftwork and served as the Maintenance Supervisor when I left to join the Fire Service full time. I worked rotating shifts for 17 years and drove 80 miles round-trip to work every day. 
I have been a full-time Fire Chief for 20 years - 9 years with Sweetwater County Fire District 1 and 11 years for the Rock Springs Fire Department. I currently serve as the Chair of the Rock Springs-Sweetwater County Airport Board (13 years, 11 as Chair), Vice-Chair of the Sweetwater Combined Communications Board (16 years, 5 as Chair), and Chairman of the Wyoming State Emergency Response Committee.
I am the 1st Vice President of the Wyoming Fire Chief's Association, and the Missouri Valley Division Director to the International Association of Fire Chiefs representing the states of Colorado, Nebraska, North Dakota, South Dakota, Iowa, Kansas, Missouri, and Wyoming.

Ensuring that we have our fair share of water tops the list - it is literally our life force. Access to mental health resources and suicide prevention - we have lost enough of our own. Promote economic development - let the world know that Wyoming is open for business. Ensure an effective education for every child in Wyoming - meet the student where they are regarding CTE, post-secondary education, etc. Wyoming needs affordable housing to accommodate the workforce needed to promote growth.
Explore and expand options for Mobile-Integrated healthcare and Community Paramedicine. Bring healthcare to where the people are.

I do not support the sale or transfer of public lands in Wyoming except in very narrow circumstances - only parcels that abut municipal corporate limits and then only for affordable housing. I would lean heavily on our US Congressional Delegation to keep our lands and access intact with multi-purpose use in the forefront.
Property tax is the tax closest to the people and is the tax which most supports local government. These funds should be managed with objective restraint and discretion.

We can't expect people to invest in Wyoming if we do not invest in ourselves. Responsible investment in infrastructure such as water, waste water, power transmission, and roads will attract investment from outside. At the local level, communities also need to invest in themselves to create and improve quality of life.
I am not well informed enough to answer this question.
Cody, Wyoming - 5 years, Wyoming 11 years.

I am an attorney, former Drug Court Judge, and community leader who has dedicated my career to public service, the rule of law, and strengthening Wyoming communities. I earned an MBA and law degree and have spent years helping individuals, families, businesses, and local governments navigate complex legal issues. I currently serve as President of the Cody Country Chamber of Commerce and have been recognized for professional excellence by various groups including being one of the Wyoming Chamber of Commerce's 40 Under 40. My background in law, business, and community service gives me the experience and practical perspective needed to represent House District 50 and fight for limited government, local control, and Wyoming values.

Wyoming faces three major challenges: rising costs for families and businesses, economic uncertainty tied to the energy industry, and a growing disconnect between government and local communities. At the same time, Wyoming has tremendous opportunities to strengthen its economy, develop new energy technologies, support small businesses, and attract families who value freedom and our way of life.

As a legislator, I would focus on making Wyoming more affordable, protecting Wyoming's energy industries while encouraging innovation, and returning more decision-making authority to local communities. I support responsible government spending, reducing unnecessary regulations, and preserving the constitutional rights and freedoms that make Wyoming unique. My goal is to keep Wyoming affordable, prosperous, and independent for future generations.

Healthcare in Wyoming is expensive for many of the same reasons it is expensive across the country: a lack of competition, excessive bureaucracy, and policies that drive up costs without improving care. The Legislature should focus on increasing access to providers, reducing unnecessary regulations, expanding telehealth options, and improving price transparency so patients can make informed decisions.

Wyoming should encourage market-based solutions that increase competition and lower costs while ensuring rural communities maintain access to essential healthcare services. I support efforts that give patients more control over their healthcare decisions and dollars, while avoiding one-size-fits-all approaches that increase costs or create greater dependence on government programs.

I do not support the sale or large-scale transfer of Wyoming’s public lands. Public lands are an important part of our state’s heritage, economy, and way of life. They support recreation, hunting, grazing, tourism, and multiple-use access that benefits Wyoming residents and visitors alike.

I do support stronger local input and better management of state and federal lands. Decisions affecting Wyoming should involve the people who live, work, and recreate here, not just distant bureaucracies. I will advocate for policies that promote responsible multiple-use management, protect public access, support responsible resource development, and ensure these lands continue to benefit future generations of Wyomingites.

Wyoming taxpayers deserve meaningful property tax relief, but we must also recognize that no two counties are the same. Counties that experienced dramatic increases in property valuations should not be treated the same as counties that saw little change. A one-size-fits-all reduction risks creating winners and losers across the state.

I support returning residential property valuations to 2019 levels and establishing a reasonable cap on future increases from that point forward. This approach would provide substantial relief in areas where valuations surged while allowing counties that were less affected to maintain a stable tax base and continue funding essential local services. It offers a balanced solution that restores predictability and affordability for homeowners while respecting local needs and preserving local control.

The Legislature has an important role in creating an environment where businesses, workers, and communities can succeed, but it should not try to pick winners and losers in the marketplace. The state's primary responsibility is to provide a stable regulatory environment, maintain essential infrastructure, support workforce development, and keep taxes competitive so businesses have the confidence to invest in Wyoming.

As President of the Cody Country Chamber of Commerce, I have seen firsthand how local businesses drive economic growth and strengthen communities. Economic development is most successful when it is driven from the ground up by entrepreneurs, local leaders, and private investment—not directed from Cheyenne. I support policies that reduce barriers to growth, encourage innovation, and allow Wyoming businesses and workers to thrive while preserving the values and quality of life that make our state unique.

Campaign contributions should be transparent so voters know who is supporting candidates and political causes. Wyoming's elections should be decided by Wyoming voters, not by outside organizations or special interests seeking to influence our state's future.

I am particularly concerned about the growing role of out-of-state money in Wyoming elections. Regardless of political ideology, Wyoming residents should be able to see who is funding campaigns and independent expenditures and make informed decisions accordingly. I support strong transparency and disclosure requirements so voters have access to timely and accurate information. While Wyoming's current system generally provides meaningful disclosure, I am open to reforms that increase transparency and help ensure Wyoming elections remain accountable to the people of Wyoming.

I have lived in Sheridan for 20 years and resided in Gillette for 10 years prior.  I also lived in Cheyenne in the 1980s for a total of 32 years in Wyoming. I was born in Deadwood SD and grew up in Spearfish, SD.  I returned to Spearfish for a few years before moving to Wyoming in 1996.

Following a career as an defense industry engineer and manager, Susan returned to her hometown of Spearfish, SD, where she started working in economic development.  In 1996, she moved to Gillette to work for five northeast Wyoming counties. In 2006, following a successful grant application to expand Gillette College's technical training programs, she was hired by Sheridan College where she served as the Vice President for External Relations and Economic Development. Susan represented the Sheridan Economic and Education Development Authority (SEEDA) for the Weatherby relocation to Sheridan.   In 2018, Susan formed Bigelow Grant Writing Services, LLC, to continue to secure funds for Wyoming projects. In 2024, she served as the lead consultant for the Wind River Development Fund's $36 M grant award. 

Susan's education includes an associates degree in science,  bachelor's degree in electronic engineering, masters in business administration, and Ph.D. in higher education leadership.

Wyoming is at a crossroads. Our structural reliance on the minerals industry leaves us vulnerable to revenue declines, forcing WYDOT into a strict "preservation mode" for critical infrastructure and leaving our community colleges struggling to fund facilities. At the same time, employers tell me their top constraints to growth are a shortage of skilled technical labor and a lack of attainable housing for employees.

We can build our own resilience. Over my 30 years in economic development and higher education leadership, I’ve seen what works. In Sheridan, we strategically expanded career technical programs and built a vibrant light manufacturing sector, recruiting employers like Weatherby, Inc.

As your legislator, I will bring this practical expertise to Cheyenne. I will fight to co-fund critical infrastructure, champion career and technical education (CTE) to secure our workforce, and work with communities to reduce the high cost of home construction.

To make healthcare affordable and accessible, the legislature must address the structural realities of rural medicine. My approach focuses on four key pillars:

Balance the Payor Mix: Wyoming hospitals face a multi-million dollar uncompensated care crisis, driving up private premiums. Increasing state Medicaid reimbursement rates will better cover care costs, start to balance the payor mix, and reduce the financial burden on insured families and employers.

Leverage Purchasing Power: We should explore state-backed Group Purchasing Organizations (GPOs) to bulk-buy supplies, lowering fixed operational overhead for rural facilities.

Secure the Workforce Pipeline: We must fund clinical training grants, expand nursing pathways in community colleges, and support medical residencies to retain homegrown talent.

Prioritize Prevention: We must strengthen local mental health and early intervention frameworks (look at Sheridan's new model) to keep patients out of costly emergency rooms.

I will fiercely oppose any efforts to sell or transfer Wyoming’s public lands to private hands. Our landscapes are the absolute foundation of our economic pillars, our outdoor heritage, and our daily way of life. From grazing and mineral development to hunting, fishing, and recreation, these lands must remain accessible to the public to ensure our outdoor traditions are preserved for generations to come.

I strongly support the framework of SJ0009, which demands that the federal government respect our state’s "Multiple Use" heritage. Decisions regarding Wyoming’s unique landscapes should never be driven by detached federal overreach or arbitrary regulatory mandates. Instead, land management must be driven by sound science and local stakeholder input. As an avid outdoor enthusiast who treasures the Big Horn Mountains, I am committed to defending local sovereignty and ensuring that both state and federal land management serves the true interests of the people of Wyoming.

Recent property tax changes preserved school funding by making up the gap with other state revenues. The Legislature also kept cities and counties whole through direct distributions, but they left a critical gap: our special districts. Local hospitals, fire districts, and weed and pest districts received less revenue because they lacked backfill funding. True tax reform means protecting homeowners without crippling the essential emergency and health services that keep Wyoming communities safe.

My priority will be ensuring the state establishes clear mechanisms to shield our vital fire and hospital districts from devastating revenue losses. We must deliver meaningful tax relief while fully supporting the local first responders who protect us.

I am an unapologetic supporter of state and local economic development because Wyoming’s future depends on our willingness to invest in our own success. To build a predictable, pro-growth environment, I focus on three core pillars:

Infrastructure Investment: Co-funding critical infrastructure—like water, sewer, utilities, and regional air travel—to lower the barrier to entry for private employers, while keeping regulations streamlined and taxes pro-business.

Targeted Recruitment: Aggressively marketing the state for large-scale energy projects and light manufacturing, replicating our success in bringing Weatherby to Sheridan and leveraging the Energy Dominance Fund.

Workforce & Housing: Supporting robust skills training by strengthening career technical education in K-12 and community colleges, and working with communities to lower the high cost of building homes for our workforce.

I believe in personal commitment and true grassroots efforts. My first campaign donation came out of my own pocket. Next, I called on people who share my values and asked for their personal support. While I do accept contributions from political action committees whose values align with mine, I firmly believe Wyoming’s campaigns should be funded by candidates, Wyoming citizens, and Wyoming interests. To date, my only out-of-state donations have been from former Wyoming residents. Because our current process is transparent, I do not believe Wyoming’s system for monitoring campaign donations and expenditures needs reform.
I am a 5th generation Campbell County Native .
I turn 67 on 09-25-59

I have worked in the Coal, Oil, and Gas industries for over 45 years and have ran a small cattle and sheep operation for 20 plus years on my grandparents' homestead. I have formed the Wyoming Constitutionalist, and was chairman of the Campbell County Constitution Party. I have served in the Wyoming House of Representatives in 2021 and 2022 and feel a need to return as Data Centers and Nuclear energy are taking center stage using Presidential Executive Orders that are unconstitutional. I feel we need to slow walk Wyoming through these industries so future generations can enjoy Wyoming as much as I have.
Executive Orders forcing Data Centers and Nuclear on Wyoming. Executive Orders would have to be addressed as Unconstitutional and needs to be repealed.

All cost for goods and services is a by product of devaluation due to inflation created since 1913 to June 2026, there is about 97% decline in the dollar. Allow more competition in the healthcare sector.

Article 1 Section 8 of the United States Constitution says, what the federal government can own, and it is very little. I believe we should turn to the homestead act using a lottery system to pick parcels for citizens, not corporations, to get public land and minerals back into the hands of private ownership.
I believe in property tax elimination with a consumption tax to make up the difference only after all hidden taxes savings and investment accounts are revealed.

If a business comes to Wyoming it should have a business plan that does not require State and Federal hand outs, therefore the State should not be picking winners and losers with economic development using the citizens tax dollars.
I pay for my own campaigns out of my pocket and do not have to answer to others about what I say or do. I believe there could be more transparency for PACS and Super PACS.
Gillette Wyoming 58 years born in Gillette

I am a fourth-generation resident of Wyoming. I own several small businesses and have to children both grown. I have 12 years of experience as a Campbell County Commissioner in my younger days and 5 years of experience as a Wyoming State House member. I am Chairman of Corporations, Elections and Political Subdivisions, Chairman of Management Audit and sit as a member of the Minerals Business and Economic Development Committee. I have also served on Travel Recreation and Wildlife as well as the Select Committee on Capital Finance and Investing. My years as a commissioner allow me to weigh local government needs and partnerships while giving me budget experience. The 5 years as a member of the House and the number of Committees I have served on give a wide span of learning important sectors of Wyoming economy and needs. My decision making includes research, public testimony and conservative principles.

Wyoming continues to face challenges for future revenues which have typically come from our minerals industry/ Those revenues due to past "climate" administrations have been on the decline. With our current national need for energy dominance Wyoming should reduce restrictive regulations for our legacy industries. I have supported legislation creating bonding pools for small oil and gas operators, lowered severance taxes twice on coal, and will continue to strive removal of the low carbon regulations on power plants. I have voted for education bills that give more choices and enable our classro0ms to have the wages and tools to support Wyoming kids. Now that we have calibration we need to concentrate on additional changes if needed and ensuring revenues for the future. Our roads are a priority as we look to maintaining our infrastructure nod maintenance. Budgeting should align with the purpose of limited government and its priorities for our citizens.

Healthcare is an issue for most rural communities. Cost is a result of patient expectation, increased technology and expense to meet the expectation and the insurance industry as well as Medicaid and Medicare that pay for that cost ultimately increasing for the patient. The rural healthcare funds could help in providing small communities with the base level of care and EMS service for next level regional transport.

The federal government should transfer land and minerals to the state in an effort to equalize the eastern states entry into statehood. The west is at a disadvantage in developing both minerals and land use. Wyoming can better manage our lands and resources for multi-use and solve many outstanding land use issues in the process. I do not support the sale of public lands unless it resolves locked areas, reduces, the checkerboarding and makes sense for Wyoming citizens in the use of resources and public access.

When we passed property tax relief it was the height of inflation and costs of insurance, medications, food and energy. Folks were deciding if they had to skip eating or paying property tax. The House voted in every proposal it sent to the senate backfill for local governments which we carved out in budget priorities during the supplemental budget. All those attempts were defeated by the sente or line item vetoed by the governor with $100 million going to one company through energy matching funds instead of loan program for fire damages to local communities. Direct distribution to cities towns and counties helps but does not take into account local improvement and service districts or water districts. Further property tax reductions need to include a solution for these entities. Every revenue and taxation move has a ripple effect and the Revenue Committee is working hard to find answers I look forward to seeing this session.

Government should use regulation reduction, tax relief when appropriate and building up infrastructure such as roads, water and sewer, and other infrastructure helping communities to prepare for growth. We should not be picking individual companies for cash infusion. Every job we bring in that is not paid for costs us over $20.000 each in services we provide. This leads to employee shortages, housing shortages and other issues that government continues to be expected to pay for. Water issues, waste repositories, grid problems are serious consequences we need to address and decide on. We should build upwards on the resources available and abundant in Wyoming. This includes some parts of the nuclear chin like uranium mining and processing. Working with industry and energy for our trona mines and new coal plants. Chasing industries that only present increased challenges but contribute little actual revenue need more thought.

Campaign donations are protected by the Supreme court ruling and the free-speech rights of the constitution. It would be great to get money out of politics but challenging facing the use of donations as a voice to represent our views and votes as citizens. I try to keep that principle in mind as we have worked on dark money bills and PAC transparency in the past. I list all campaign donors on the secretary of state reporting forms and like the fact that they are easily accessible to the public.

I was born in Monticello, Utah
We have lived in Wyoming for 46 years and,
We have lived at our current address for 31 years

My wife Rebecca and I have been married for 47 years come August, with six children, all graduating from a university, five from the University of Wyoming.
We have 23 grandchildren. Only 2 of the five children live in Wyoming.
My brother, father and I owned a construction company serving the petroleum industry for 35 years, starting with 3 employees and growing to 100.  
All  the afore mentioned background prepared me well to serve in the legislature.  I am in my 14th year of service to the citizens of House District #54 and the State of Wyoming.
During my time in the legislature I served 8 years on the Appropriations Committee, four years on the Minerals Committee, four years on the Labor & Health Committee, 10 years on Tribal Relations, two years on the Transportation Committee and as well several select committees and task forces.

1. Healthcare, including mental health and emergency medical services is the biggest challenge we have.   

2. Over the past 4-years we have seen a significant erosion for private property rights.  People are now subject to the will of neighboring land owners when make  a decision on how they want to use their property or who they can sell their property to.

3. We must find additional funding for our highway system. What was an annual shortfall of $400-million dollars 10-years ago has now grown to $600-million annually.  We are not able to build the new roads needed nor replace bridges that are nearing the end of their life cycle.  We are merely trying to maintain existing highway infrastructure.

To begin with we need to understand some of the data. For example Wyoming ranks 4th in the number of hospitals per 1,000 people with 29 hospitals in the state in 2024, and it ranks 8th in the nation for the same time period for the number of staffed beds available at 1875.  These numbers are much better than the states around us.  We rank as the 14th highest state for medical cost per capita.   If you average out the state we have one hospital for every 18,000 residents, and Colorado on the other hand has 96,000 residents supporting each hospital.
Wyoming with its large geographic area and small population struggles to have a hospital close to every community and the low number of people supporting the hospital means they don't have the patient volume to keep prices down, and because the prices are higher residents often leave the state to have elective surgeries further exasperating the problem.
When you look and mental health the problem become even more complex.

We have always had the ability through a variety of ways to sell public lands or transfer lands through an exchange process. I think the objective should be to make sure there is a public benefit in the transaction.  A case in point was the sale of the Kelly parcel in Teton County to the federal government.  People in Wyoming supported the disposal of this irreplaceable parcel to the National Park Service feeling the process was very transparent and the financial benefit to our K-12 schools far exceeded the benefit received by leasing the property for grazing.  I believe there is an argument to be made to not reduce the amount of public lands and not increase the amount of public lands.  The citizens of the state have listed the protection of public lands as a top priority.  I am committed to protecting public lands, and making sure the public is a net benefactor of any sale or trade.

The legislature has responded to the public's desire for property tax relief by passing seven bills (2023 - 1, 2024-3, and 2025-3) in an attempt to provide relief.  We need to give the legislation some time to see if the bills produce the intended results.  It takes a little time for the medication to take affect, and we need to avoid over-prescribing which could have adverse impact on the entities that rely of property tax to provide services the people want including K-12 funding, county government, special districts i.e. hospital, cemetery, fire, and ambulance.
The next step is up to the voters.  If they vote for the constitutional amendment to reduce personal property tax by 50%, they will in effect be acknowledging that reduction will require a reduction of services in their county and they are saying with that vote they are willing to accept that reduction in services moving forward and should not expect the legislature to backfill the lost revenue with State General Funds.

Wyoming is in competition with every other state in the nation when recruiting business or industry to our state.  Without a central agency  like the Wyoming Business Council to field relocation questions and direct them to the appropriate city, town, or county, it is left up to the relocating business or industry to determine which city, town, or county they should reach out to. And perhaps even more important is the help provided to business and industry regarding our permitting process and assistance to navigate the requirements of our state agencies. No one is required to use the Business Council, but most everyone does.  
I also think the "Energy Matching Funds" have been incredibly successful in attracting business and industries that are associated with our core industries. The creation of the energy dominance fund is also critical and will contribute to Wyoming's long term stability.

Historically Wyoming has been a model of public participation in the election process.  People whether individuals or businesses can support the candidate of their choice in a variety of ways including financial support.  Financial contributions are recorded in the Secretary of State's office and is available to the public.  I would like to eliminate the PAC money originating in Wyoming or other states that are not required to identify who the contributors are and operate independent of any candidate.  I think this kind of money has planted seeds of contempt in the campaign process and stifled the practice of mutual respect among opposing candidates.
Riverton, WY; 70 years; Los Angeles, CA

I am a lifelong Riverton resident and have been married to my wife, Tanis, since 1980. Together we have two daughters and five grandchildren. Family and community have always been central to my life and my commitment to public service.

For more than 30 years, I have served on the Fremont County School District #25 Board of Trustees, working with educators, parents, students, and taxpayers to make thoughtful decisions that support our schools and communities. That experience has given me firsthand knowledge of budgeting, policy development, long-range planning, and the importance of responsible government.

Throughout my years of service, I have learned that the best decisions come from listening, finding common ground, and focusing on solutions. I am running for Wyoming House District 55 to bring that same practical, local perspective to Cheyenne and represent the people of Fremont County with integrity, independence, and respect.

One of the biggest challenges facing Wyoming today is the loss of civility and the growing difficulty of working together to solve problems. Too often, political disagreements get in the way of progress, and important issues are left unresolved.

I also see this as one of Wyoming’s greatest opportunities. Our state has a strong tradition of neighbors working together, respecting different viewpoints, and finding common ground. There is far more that unites us than divides us.

As a legislator, my approach will be simple: do the work. I have learned that good decisions come from listening, studying the issues, asking questions, and working with others to find practical solutions. Not everyone will agree on every issue, but we can still treat one another with respect and focus on what is best for Wyoming.

The people of House District 55, and Wyoming, deserve a representative who is willing to listen, collaborate, and get things done.

Healthcare access and affordability are critical issues for Wyoming, especially in our rural communities. We must ensure that basic healthcare services remain available while also addressing the funding challenges.
One area that deserves immediate attention is mental health. Wyoming continues to see the effects of rising suicide rates, substance abuse, and addiction on individuals, families, and communities. We cannot ignore these challenges. Investing in mental health services, crisis intervention, prevention programs, and treatment resources can improve lives while reducing long-term costs to taxpayers and the healthcare system.
As legislators, we need to work with healthcare providers, communities, and state agencies to identify practical solutions that improve access to care and strengthen essential services. Healthy communities are the foundation of a strong Wyoming, and ensuring access to basic healthcare and mental health resources must remain a priority.

No. Wyoming’s public lands are a vital part of our economy, our heritage, and our way of life. They support agriculture, energy development, recreation, tourism, and public access opportunities that benefit Wyoming residents and future generations.

I do not support efforts to sell off public lands. Instead, we must remain vigilant in protecting these resources and ensuring they are managed in the best interests of the people of Wyoming. That means advocating for responsible, efficient, and effective management that balances multiple uses while preserving access and opportunities for those who live and work here.

As a legislator, I will support a strong and proactive approach to public land management, working to ensure that Wyoming’s voice is heard in decisions affecting our lands and that these resources continue to serve Wyoming families, communities, and industries for years to come.

I understand the public’s desire for property tax relief, but we also have to be honest about the consequences of reducing revenue that funds essential local services. Wyoming’s counties are different, and what works in one community may not work in another.

We’ve put ourselves in a difficult position with recent cuts. We cannot continue a “take from Peter to pay Paul” approach, shifting money from one fund to cover shortfalls in another. Schools, local governments, emergency services, and infrastructure all require stable funding.

I don’t claim to have all the answers, but I believe we need to move quickly to explore innovative funding solutions and new revenue opportunities. We must have honest conversations about how we fund the services Wyoming citizens expect while maintaining fiscal responsibility. Ignoring the problem won’t solve it. It’s time to do the work, examine the options, and find practical solutions that serve Wyoming for the long term.

Economic development is critical to Wyoming’s future. The Legislature should help create an environment where businesses can grow, invest, and create jobs while allowing communities to shape their own economic priorities.

I believe the Wyoming Business Council is doing a fair job supporting economic development efforts across the state. We need to focus on attracting and growing sustainable industries that provide good-paying jobs and long-term opportunities for Wyoming families.

One of our biggest challenges is keeping young people in Wyoming. Too many leave because they don’t see enough career opportunities here. We should support economic development strategies that strengthen our communities, expand our workforce, and create jobs that allow the next generation to build their lives here.

Strong communities and sustainable job growth are essential to Wyoming’s long-term success.

I believe voters deserve transparency and honesty when it comes to campaign financing. People have a right to know who is supporting candidates and where campaign money is coming from so they can make informed decisions at the ballot box.

The last election cycle highlighted concerns that should not be ignored. Wyoming voters were flooded with negative mailers, misleading information, and campaign materials that often made it difficult to identify who was funding them. That does not build trust in our elections or our government.

I believe Wyoming should review its campaign finance and disclosure laws to ensure funding sources are clear, reporting is timely, and voters have access to accurate information. Regardless of political affiliation, campaigns should be conducted with integrity and transparency. Elections should be about issues, ideas, and qualifications, not confusion, deception, or anonymous attacks.

I currently live in Casper; my family moved back in 2003. I was born in Laramie and raised in Douglas; residing in Douglas from 1st Grade through 9th Grade. My family moved to Casper in 1988 where I attended and graduated from Kelly Walsh High School and Casper College. I then moved to Laramie and graduated from the University of Wyoming. In total, I have been a resident of Wyoming for over 46 years.

Serving on the Governor’s Wyoming Wildlife Task Force, I was fortunate to work with Legislators, Commissioners, Landowners, Outfitters, Sportsman, Anglers, and Wyoming Game & Fish. We focused on top-priority wildlife policy issues and provided recommendations to the Legislature and WG&F Commission. I learned the importance of understanding issues from a variety of perspectives, leaning on experts for guidance, and the critical importance of engaging those impacted by the decisions made.
I am a lifelong Republican who believes in local control, fiscal responsibility, and doing right by those who call Wyoming home. I have built a reputation of being a legislator who listens, works hard, and is an effective policymaker. I am actively engaged, accessible, and maintain transparent communication. Whether meeting with constituents, testifying in committees, or working on citizen-driven legislation, I am focused on practical solutions that benefit Wyoming and future generations.

Wyoming is facing several challenges including: shrinking populations, increased cost of living, lack of affordable and accessible healthcare and housing, workforce shortages, and lack of economic development, to name a few. Primary concerns I hear from constituents is the lack of job and career opportunities for our youth and young families, healthcare costs and access, and rising costs of living without comparable increases in wages. Wyoming has a very unique landscape with vast spaces and limited populations; theses factors create unique opportunities for public-private partnerships. We are positioned to significantly diversify our economy and improve overall economic growth. To do so, we need leadership willing to embrace change, say yes to new business, and establish policy that best protects and promotes Wyoming's best interests. I will continue to build relationships, understand the complexities of these issues, and focus on solutions that address Wyoming's needs.

The Wyoming Legislature needs to embrace collaborative efforts between public and private sectors. We can improve healthcare affordability and access by increasing and expanding Medicaid coverage, increasing rural hospital funding via the Rural Health Transformation Program, and expanding broadband and telehealth capabilities. The Legislature can also lower out-of-pocket costs by enhancing insurance purchasing power, passing laws to prevent restrictive healthcare provider contracts and non-compete clauses, and investing in primary care. We should focus on maximizing the Rural Health Transformation Program by funding rural facilities (specifically OB/GYN maternal health services) and training programs; recalibrating state investments toward preventative primary care; and leveraging Wyoming Department of Health healthcare workforce development initiatives.

I do not support efforts to sell or transfer public lands in Wyoming. Our Wyoming Constitution directs the Board of Land Commissioners to manage state trust lands for long-term growth in value and optimum, sustainable revenue production. Alternatively, federal public lands-those managed by federal agencies such as the BLM, BOR, and Forest Service-are required to be managed under a multiple-use mandate, which requires the consideration of all uses and users in management decisions. I co-sponsored SJ009, which opposes any state or federal legislation or policy that promotes the broad or indiscriminate sale or exchange of public lands; demands federal agencies respect existing land management frameworks, county land-use plans and the voices of local communities most directly affected by federal land decisions; and supports adequately funding state agencies so they can efficiently and effectively review land purchases, exchanges or limited disposals under the existing legal framework.

Balancing property tax relief with local revenue needs requires a targeted approach that supports our Constitutions' mandate that taxation be "equal and uniform" within each designated property class. I oppose total property tax elimination, which would starve local municipalities and force the elimination of services like fire protection, emergency medical services, law enforcement, road maintenance, water and wastewater systems, and public schools. I have supported and would support income-qualified refund programs and modest, constitutional statewide sales tax adjustments to protect our local essential services. If additional reductions in property tax revenue are adopted, to offset  those reductions we will need to explore increasing statewide sales and use tax or implementing a state income tax. As Wyoming attracts a significant number of tourists, the sales and use tax approach may broaden the tax base without placing an undue burden on local property owners.

I view the legislature's primary responsibility in economic development as maintaining a business-friendly environment by keeping tax burdens low and reducing regulatory red tape to attract private investment. Recognizing Wyoming's dominance in energy, our legislature plays a heavy role in supporting infrastructure development—such as carbon dioxide pipelines, enhanced oil recovery, and energy matching funds—to ensure our legacy industries remain competitive nationally and internationally. I believe our state government should continue playing a role in supporting our local municipalities by  funding foundational infrastructure, such as statewide broadband, expansion of roads, water and wastewater systems, etc., and community facilities needed for economic development. Efforts should provide a consistent strategy to encourage private sector development of companies that provide higher than average wages, create primary jobs, diversify the economy, and generate tax revenue.

Wyoming's campaign finance system operates largely on an honor system, with little auditing or enforcement unless complaints are filed. Meaningful accountability should start with random audits of candidate filings and reporting. Grassroots campaigning, door-to-door outreach, and relatively modest fundraising can still be enough to run a competitive campaign in many districts, preserving Wyoming's tradition of accessible citizen government. I oppose funding from out-of-state influences and believe citizens elected to serve Wyoming residents should only be allowed to accept locally sourced contributions. I would also like to see our state explore establishing campaign limits at all levels of government - capping total campaign amounts, limiting campaign timeframes, and blacking out dates when contributions may be accepted.

I was born in Denver, moved to Casper in 1972 and then moved out of State upon graduation from UW with a BS in mechanical engineering.  After a 33 year career in oil & gas, I'm currently retired and I moved back to Casper's House District 56 several years ago with my family helping support my Mom.

I've gained an extensive understanding of the oil & gas industry having 30+ years of front-line experience.  Managing large budgets, complex projects, working within teams holding dizzying arrays of diverse complexities, I'm well prepared to represent my district's multifaceted perspectives.  District 56 is one of the most politically mixed in the State.  As such, effective representation should position in the center.  I'm willing to reach in both directions so that the votes made in Cheyenne best represent the constituents rather than myopic views of the representative.  That said, I'm running as a Republican because I believe and support the Republican Platform.  I'll have no problem earning the endorsement of the Party when that day comes.  I'm transparent who I am and what I stand for so that the voters have a clear and informed understanding of expectations.  Please visit PeteFoxHD56.com to better understand my vision, plan and why I'm running.

My vision and plan highlight our middle class and youth as facing the most significant challenges and opportunities.  Supporting these two groups helps them and everyone else reliant on them, including the elderly.  What's good for our kids is good for Wyoming and improvements include housing, employment, training, education, healthcare etc.  However, balancing economic prosperity with long term protections for our beautiful State is an absolute must.

One perspective I've recently brushed with is the current pay scale of healthcare providers in our State to ensure we're providing up to date and competitive rates to our providers as a means of stabilizing this community and keep employees 'in-house' with reduced turnover.  I'll be looking further into this aspect to better vet these issues with those who truly understand the details.

Open dialog is a must when land management changes are proposed.  I'll emphasize transparency of information prior to decisions being made.  Broadly, public land is a treasure to Wyoming which must be protected so that citizens can enjoy and cherish these lands without jeopardizing its long term health and access.  Making closed door, willy-nilly decisions involving public lands simply cannot be allowed to occur.

The reductions to our assessed residential  property values via exemptions has allowed significant tax relief for us.  The big bump we had in our tax bills was largely driven by inflation and the exemptions have eliminated this bump.  Good!  However, increased prices are still there.  We feel it as does the government.  2026 dollars have a 44% reduction in purchasing power compared to 2013 dollars.  That's big.  Fortunately per the State Auditor, most cities, counties and special districts are spending less than the revenue coming in.  We're not going broke due to residential property tax reductions.  That said, I'm inclined to hold tight with further reductions in residential property taxes.  The 25% exemption we all received lowered our State's revenue by $100M.  To increase this exemption by another 50% that's on this fall's election ballot seems too much too soon.  Our Legislature will continue to wrestle with this issue and I'm confident balanced thoughtful decisions will prevail.

The Legislature can stifle development with over regulation, or allow major harm by taking too loose of a laissez faire stance.  Our beautiful State demands protection.  So do workers' rights.  Within my 'vision/plan' at PeteFoxHD56.com, I outline my desire to target our middle class and youth for SMART growth.  That comes by balance, having all key stakeholders involved in decision making.  And those conversations require team builders to create a balanced framework that fits all the demands and requirements associated with smart growth.  We have to help our youth and young families.  If we don't, our State will suffer severely in the years to come!

My campaign is small, lean and self-funded.  I have no 'strings attached' so to speak.  Personal donations are certainly welcomed, but there's no outside money coming into my campaign.  Seeing the issues with major electronic firms who organize campaign funding (e.g. 'smurfing' and 'dark money' funds), I've not used these serviced either to protect the donors who do choose to contribute.  If my chances of winning my election suffer from this minimalist approach, so be it.

I currently live in Casper, Wyoming. I am proud to have been raised on a small farm outside of Buffalo. Wyoming has always been home. After graduating from Buffalo High School, I left the state twice to pursue my education, but like many Wyomingites, I came back because this is where I wanted to raise my family. Wyoming’s values of hard work, personal responsibility, family, and community shaped who I am, and protecting those values is why I chose to serve.

I hold a doctorate in educational leadership, am a mother of two college students, and am the current State Representative for House District 57. I was raised with Wyoming values — work hard, live within your means, help your neighbor, and leave things better than you found them. My professional career has been dedicated to education, serving as a reading teacher and now as a curriculum director working to improve outcomes, solve problems, and support the communities I serve. As a legislator, I believe government works best when it governs closest to the people. My campaign was built on a simple idea: give the voice back to the people. Through my HD57 survey initiative, I ask constituents where they stand on important issues, publicly share the results, and use that information to guide my votes. Representation should be focused on the people we serve.

Wyoming’s greatest challenge is protecting what makes our state special while preparing responsibly for the future. We must address rising costs, property taxes, workforce needs, healthcare access, and economic stability, as well as the immediate challenge of water resource management, all without losing our Wyoming values. I believe solutions come from listening to the people closest to the issues — not assuming government always has the answer. Wyoming has incredible opportunities: strong communities, natural resources, innovation, and hardworking people. As a legislator, my focus is on supporting responsible budgets, protecting individual freedoms, maintaining local control, and creating opportunities so that future generations can continue to call Wyoming home.

Access to affordable healthcare is a significant concern facing Wyoming families. In a rural state, we need practical solutions that improve access while recognizing that every community has different needs. The Legislature should continue to look for ways to reduce barriers, support rural healthcare providers, increase transparency, strengthen the healthcare workforce, and encourage innovation, such as tele-health. Healthcare decisions impact families, seniors, businesses, and taxpayers, and their voices need to be included. My commitment remains the same — listen to the people of House District 57, review the information, and work toward solutions that serve my community.

I do not support the selling of Wyoming’s public lands. They support livelihoods, agriculture, energy industries, wildlife, hunting, recreation, and way of life. Decisions about land management must prioritize the people who live, work, and raise families here. I believe local voices should be at the center of conversations about land management because Wyoming people understand Wyoming land. It’s crucial that we protect these lands for future generations and ensure they remain accessible for everyone.

Property taxes impact families across Wyoming, especially those on fixed incomes and those concerned about being priced out of their homes. At the same time, we must recognize that local governments rely on revenue to provide essential services. The challenge is finding responsible solutions that provide meaningful relief while understanding the impacts on our communities. My role as a representative is not to push my own agenda but to represent the people of House District 57. Through my survey initiative, I gather input from constituents and use it to guide my votes on issues such as property tax reform.

I believe the government’s role is not to create winners and losers but to create an environment where Wyoming businesses, workers, and communities can succeed. Wyoming’s future depends on honoring our traditional industries while also being open to new opportunities. Economic growth should happen in a way that protects Wyoming values, supports local communities, and keeps decision-making close to the people.

When I accept PAC donations, I always make it clear that my votes are dedicated to the people of House District 57. I believe that transparency and accountability are fundamental in public service. Voters have the right to know who supports candidates and how campaigns are financed. Campaign finance systems should be clear, transparent, and accessible to the public. Any reforms should aim to enhance trust while ensuring that everyday Wyoming citizens can still participate in the electoral process.
Casper, HD57. I was born and raised in Casper, and lived in Natrona County my whole life.

I am a lifelong Casper resident, and a stepdad to three amazing little girls. I’ve grown up here, and I’ve watched where we have struggled to provide support to families who need it most. My family was forced to move during the market crash of ‘08, highlighting to me at a young age how fragile stability can be. 

I worked to save Rock the Block. I organized the Pokémon Go community to cleanup downtown Casper. I’ve helped on service projects at the Historic Trails Center. I’ve built playgrounds with my bare hands. 

I am running to give my neighbors a choice this year. I don’t believe an election should be decided by one party, at the primary election. 70% of voter-aged residents didn’t even show up at our last primary election, and it left me wondering what a “majority” decision looks like. I’m running because my representation feels designed to manage my concerns, not seek to sincerely understand my viewpoint, and I believe I can provide the representation I feel I’m lacking.

We have an opportunity to grow in several ways across the state. First, our kids are leaving, schools are being cut. Next, healthcare in Wyoming is struggling, from physical to mental wellbeing, and even addiction. Wyoming is often a leader across the nation in youthful suicides, with suicide being the second-highest leading cause of death. Finally, our minimum wage hasn’t increased in Wyoming for decades. 

I think that in order to solve these problems, we first need to understand the impact of tax cuts before recklessly implementing them. We should expand healthcare access across the state, while holding monopolistic organizations accountable for the harm they have caused our communities. And, let’s be frank, the economy isn’t survivable at $5.15/hour.

Wyoming already sees a shortage of doctors in nearly every county across the state. While we’ve seen that shortage, many of these communities have seen one or two specific resources take over where local doctors used to serve. It is imperative when we have access to federal grants, that we consider accepting and allocating those funds. 

We also see a shortage of mental health care. As a legislator, I would push for mental health emergency services, taking some of the most vulnerable in our community and providing solutions that allow officers to focus their efforts in service and protection of our community where it is better placed.

I want to be very clear here; I grew up here in Wyoming. I’ve hunted and fished these lands religiously since I was a child. These are also the same lands I take my children to learn, play, and grow. I appreciate the resources, labor, and love that are dedicated to caring for our land daily. I do not support any measure attempting to sell public lands, and would vigorously defend the land on behalf of the people.

I believe that the public deserves tax relief, but I’m not sure we’re approaching it the right way. Property tax relief specifically helps people who own property. Roughly a quarter of all Wyoming families are renters. Some are born and raised here, others have lived here for years but sold the family farm to be closer to relatives, and nearly every one I’ve talked to would love to find their own home to live in. 

I would like to see tax relief for all residents, which means ensuring that relief reaches the seniors and families in rentals, without doing so at the expense of my children’s future. I would advocate for legislation that aligns with these values.

I believe the Legislature's role in economic development should be to create the conditions for private industry to thrive, not to run or ruin it. The film industry is a perfect example.
 
Far too many productions have chosen to represent Wyoming with an older, out-of-touch view, and I believe allowing these productions to film here would encourage tourism, and create a more modern view of Wyoming across the world. When a $20,000,000-and-growing industry comes knocking at your door to spend money in your city, why are we saying no?

I believe in transparency for campaign donations. I’m not sure if I think the system needs reform at this time, but if I were to support any reform, it would be with ease of access for the voter, while not creating a barrier for potential candidates, at the front of my mind.
Bar Nunn. 10 years. 10 years. Garland, Texas

I'm originally from Alabama. I'm a combat veteran I served two tours in Iraq as a combat Infantryman. My little family has been here since 2016. I transport crude oil. We moved here because of the low taxes and less regulation. I'm currently serving as the the Mayor of Bar Nunn. I served on Bar Nunn Town Council previous to being for about 4 years. I have been married to Andrea Boyer for 18 years and we have 3 children together.

In my opinion three of the biggest challenges facing Wyoming are:
1. Young people leaving the state
2. Stagnant Economy
3. Poor Health care coverage
All three of these are interrelated
The opportunities we have here are primarily that we have a good base to work from. Oil, coal, gas and cattle have sustained the state for a long time and they provide a good foundation from which to build a more robust economy from.
I think one of the first things we need to do is really emphasizing and supporting the CTE programs for kids to move from high school to college to high paying jobs. We also need to change our mindset about incoming businesses and welcome them to the state. Looking at where regulations can be rolled back and taxes reduced would incentivize economic activity. Health care is tied to both of these issues. A robust economy will attract more health care professionals to the state as they will have high paying jobs to come too.
I've already talked about it but reinvigorating the economy will help immensely with health care as high paying jobs for Wyomingites will draw in doctors to treat the people here.

No except in the case of public lands being backed up against a municipalities growth boundary in which case those lands need to be carefully considered for annexation and development.
We need to have a state constitutional amendment for one day if the Supreme Court hands down a decision to return federal lands back to the states so that those lands are protected.

There are two ways in which we can address the property tax issue. 
First: we need to excite the economy so that people can afford their homes and property taxes.
Second: we need to get our legacy industries moving again through deregulation and lowering severance tax.
Third: An incremental approach of slowly raising sales tax while slowly reducing property taxes is just one idea to help people feeling the pinch.

Mainly to get out of the way of economic growth. Make sure there aren't burdensome regulations that will keep business away and keep the infrastructure in good repair. It signals to a business that the government is well run when infrastructure is well maintained and it allows for businesses to feel confident in establishing roots in the state.
No it does need reform.

I live in Bar Nunn, Wyoming. I have lived here or just outside of town for the majority of my life, minus a few years I lived in Casper. I was born in California but raised in Wyoming, as I have lived in Wyoming since I was 2 years old. That marks 20 years living in Wyoming
I will be 22 years old on Election Day.

I am a student and lifelong Wyomingite. I have a BA degree in Political Science from UW and 3 AA degrees in Secondary Education, Political Science, and History from Casper College. My teaching and academic experience includes being a writing tutor, teaching via community presentations, 260 hours of teacher practicum/residency teaching experience, attending conferences on pedagogy, co-running an academic journal, engaging in extensive research, and more.
